Let me do the math out loud, because this is where I live every day. In my world, if the numbers don’t work, there’s no backup plan. Not because I think everything should be cut—believe me, I use services—but because I’ve seen how fast costs spiral when nobody’s really watching the total.

Even with inflation the last few years, you feel it right away at the store, at the gas pump, and then everything else follows. So I appreciate the mindset of, “Okay, what are we actually spending, and is it sustainable?” I don’t always trust how they apply it, but the instinct to treat money like it’s real and limited—that makes sense to me in a gut, practical way.

What I hear sometimes is either “we should just step back and let other countries handle it” or the opposite tone that feels more about projecting strength than actually thinking through consequences. And I’m like—okay, but what keeps things from getting worse next year, or five years from now?

Let me say it this way: I don’t want us rushing into wars, obviously, but I also don’t think you can just disengage and hope problems stay contained. That’s not how the world works anymore. If there’s a policy, I want to know who’s coordinating with who, what the goal is, and how we avoid dragging regular families—our kids, eventually—into something bigger because we didn’t handle it carefully early on.

So it’s that lack of consistent, steady leadership on international stuff that bothers me. I need a clean answer, not a tough-sounding answer.

like_dem_candidate.text:
Here’s my bottom line: I could vote for her if I’m convinced she’s steady and tells the truth straight, even when it’s not convenient. At my age, I’m less interested in charm and more interested in whether a person’s words line up with what they actually do over time. What I’ve seen from her, in moments, is a kind of composure under pressure that I respect. There was a hearing—I don’t remember the exact year, but she was questioning an official and she didn’t raise her voice, didn’t grandstand, just kept tightening the question until the answer had nowhere to hide. That’s not about politics to me, that’s about discipline and honesty in how you handle responsibility.

I’m willing to change my mind about a candidate if I believe they’re fundamentally operating in good faith. If she shows me that consistently—no theatrics, no dodging—then yes, that moves me toward her.

dislike_dem_candidate.reason_category: Support or opposition based on party identity, ideological labels, or general alignment (e.g., liberal/conservative).
dislike_dem_candidate.text:
Here’s my bottom line: I’m a very liberal man in terms of social policy, but when it comes to party alignment, I don’t trust the Democratic Party’s governing instincts on money and scope, and she sits squarely in that lane for me.

I’m not reacting to her as a person so much as what she represents in the system. When I look at that party, I see a pattern of expanding programs first and figuring out how to sustain them later, and that’s where I get off the train. I spent decades watching what happens when you build something without a long-term maintenance plan—bridges, transit lines, doesn’t matter—and it always comes due with interest. That mindset, fairly or not, is what I associate with her politically.

One concrete example: I remember sitting through a local transit budget review years back—this was tied to Metro expansion—and the capital costs were fully celebrated, ribbon-cutting ready, but when you dug into the operating and maintenance projections, they were soft. Not wrong, just… optimistic in a way that made my teeth itch. That’s the same feeling I get when I hear Democratic policy framing in general, and she doesn’t signal a break from that to me.

So it’s not personal dislike. It’s that when I’m choosing, I default toward the side that at least talks like they’re measuring the full lifecycle cost, even if they don’t always get it right either.

like_rep_candidate.reason_category: Views on immigration levels, border security, undocumented immigrants, or asylum policies.
like_rep_candidate.text:
Here’s my bottom line: I pay attention to whether someone treats the border like a system that needs to be controlled, not just talked about, and Trump at least frames it that way—levels, enforcement, throughput, consequences. That matters to me.

I spent a career watching what happens when you pretend capacity limits don’t exist. You get failure, and then you get emergency fixes that cost more and work worse. Immigration isn’t different just because it’s sensitive. If you don’t know how many people are coming in, who they are, and where they’re going to land—schools, hospitals, housing—you’re not managing anything, you’re reacting. I don’t have patience for that.

One concrete example: a few years back, my grandson’s school had a sudden mid-year influx of new students who needed language support. Good kids, no issue there, but the school didn’t have the staffing lined up. Class sizes jumped, the teacher was stretched thin, and services lagged for everybody. That’s not compassion, that’s poor planning. Somebody upstream failed to match intake with capacity.

So when Trump talks about tightening the border and setting clearer limits, I hear an attempt—imperfect, sometimes clumsy—but an attempt to bring the system back into something measurable. Don’t guess at it, check it. If you’re going to let people in—and we should, in an orderly way—then you’d better have the numbers, the vetting, and the support lined up. Otherwise you’re just pushing the problem onto local communities and calling it virtue.

dislike_rep_candidate.reason_category: References to healthcare, Medicare/Medicaid, prescription costs, social safety nets, or aid to disadvantaged groups.
dislike_rep_candidate.text:
Here’s my bottom line: if I think someone is going to treat healthcare like a cost to be cut instead of a system to be maintained, that’s a problem for me, and it pushes me away from voting for them.

I’m at an age where Medicare isn’t theoretical, it’s infrastructure I rely on. I look at it the same way I used to look at a bridge—if you start shaving support off in the wrong places, it doesn’t fail immediately, it fails when people are already on it. What I’ve seen and heard around Trump, especially during his time in office, is a willingness to tinker with programs like Medicare and Medicaid without a clear, stable maintenance plan. And “we’ll figure it out later” is not a plan, that’s how systems degrade.

One concrete example: there were repeated efforts tied to his administration to roll back parts of the Affordable Care Act, including protections around pre-existing conditions. Now, people argue about the details, but here’s how I process it—if you weaken those protections without a fully built replacement that’s tested and funded, you’re introducing risk into people’s access to care. At my age, with a medical history in the family, that’s not abstract. That’s doctor visits, screenings, medications—all the things that keep you functional instead of in crisis.

Don’t guess at it, check it: healthcare systems need continuity, predictability, and funding you can actually trace. If I don’t see that discipline, I’m not comfortable. That alone is enough to make me consider voting against someone.

like_dem_party.reason_category: References to healthcare, Medicare/Medicaid, prescription costs, social safety nets, or aid to disadvantaged groups.
like_dem_party.text:
Yes—here’s my bottom line: I respect that Democrats, more often than not, treat healthcare as something you maintain before it fails, not after.

I’m at an age where that’s not theoretical. I see Medicare not as a handout, but as a system that keeps people from waiting until a small issue becomes an emergency room bill. And I pay attention when there’s talk about lowering prescription costs, because for a lot of seniors that’s the difference between following the treatment plan and quietly skipping doses. That’s not politics to me, that’s system performance—are people actually able to do what the doctor told them to do?

After my wife got sick, I got very strict about screenings and follow-ups. I remember one stretch where I had three different appointments in two months—labs, imaging, and a specialist—and every one of those was covered in a way that made it routine instead of a financial decision. That matters. If you make people stop and calculate every step, they delay, and delay is where you lose people.

So yes, I like that emphasis. You can argue about how efficiently it’s run—and I do—but the underlying idea that preventive care and access should be built into the system, that’s something I’m aligned with.

dislike_dem_party.reason_category: Support or opposition based on party identity, ideological labels, or general alignment (e.g., liberal/conservative).
dislike_dem_party.text:
Here’s my bottom line: I don’t like how the Democratic Party wraps itself in a kind of moral certainty about being “the compassionate side,” as if that settles the argument. That’s an identity problem more than a policy problem, and it bothers me.

I’ve sat in rooms—community meetings, church-adjacent outreach, that sort of thing—where a Democratic-aligned group was discussing housing support, and the tone was basically, “If you question any part of this approach, you’re against helping people.” I remember one meeting in South LA, must’ve been mid-2000s, where I asked a very plain question about how they were going to track outcomes over five years. Not oppose it—just measure it. The response I got wasn’t data, it was a look like I’d said something indecent. That’s a feelings answer dressed up as policy.

I’m a liberal man in how I think people should be treated, no question. But when a party builds its identity around being the “good” side, it starts to skip the part where you prove things work. And I don’t trust any system—political or structural—that thinks its intentions are enough.

like_rep_party.reason_category: Support or opposition based on party identity, ideological labels, or general alignment (e.g., liberal/conservative).
like_rep_party.text:
Here’s my bottom line: I like that the Republican Party, at least in its stated philosophy, puts a heavier emphasis on fiscal restraint and limited government. That lines up with how I’m wired. I spent a career watching what happens when people promise more than the budget can carry—projects stall, maintenance gets deferred, and then you pay double later when things start failing.

For me, that’s not abstract. Back in the late ’80s, I was on a project where the initial plan was solid, but the funding assumptions were… optimistic, let’s say. When the money tightened, the first thing that got cut wasn’t the ribbon-cutting piece, it was the long-term maintenance allocation. I remember sitting in a meeting thinking, “We’re building a future repair problem and calling it success.” That kind of thinking bothers me at a gut level.

So when Republicans talk about smaller government and tighter budgets, that appeals to me as a framework. I’m not saying they always execute it well—sometimes they don’t—but the underlying idea of living within constraints and being honest about costs, that’s a language I understand.

dislike_rep_party.reason_category: References to crime, policing, public safety, gun rights or gun control.
dislike_rep_party.text:
Yes. Here’s my bottom line: I don’t like how parts of the Republican Party talk about crime and public safety as if the only tool in the box is force, and more of it.

I’m not naïve about crime. I’ve worked in cities my whole life, I read the local reports, I know what unsafe streets look like. You need policing, and you need it to be competent and present. But when the conversation turns into “just be tougher, give them more leeway, worry less about oversight,” that’s where I step off. Any system without checks drifts. That’s not politics, that’s engineering—no feedback loop, no correction, you get failure.

I remember a job meeting back in the late ’80s, down near a transit corridor we were planning around. There had been a string of stops and searches in that area, and one of the younger Black inspectors on our team got pulled over three times in two weeks driving the same company truck, same route, same paperwork. Everything checked out every time. After the third stop, he started leaving earlier just to pad the delay into his schedule. That’s a system creating its own inefficiency, and it wears on people. When I hear politicians dismiss concerns like that as softness or “anti-police,” I think: you’re ignoring a design flaw that’s right in front of you.

Public safety isn’t just pressure, it’s calibration. You want officers supported, trained, and accountable, and you want policies that don’t treat whole neighborhoods like a problem to be managed. When Republicans lean too hard into the idea that questioning tactics equals being weak on crime, that’s where I disagree, and I don’t think the numbers or the lived outcomes support that approach.

like_dem_party.text: I mean… if I had to say something, it’s that they at least talk like they’re trying to help regular people keep up with costs. Like wages, bills, all that. Doesn’t always land, but that part I notice.

Look, I’ve seen enough over the years to know things aren’t as steady as they used to be—weather patterns, heat, all that. And the Democrats, Kamala Harris included, at least act like that’s something you plan around long-term instead of brushing it off. I’m not saying I agree with every idea they’ve got, not even close, but I do think our side sometimes digs in its heels a little too hard acting like there’s nothing there to deal with.

So if there’s a reason I’d even consider her, it’s that piece of it—thinking ahead on energy and not pretending everything’s fine forever. I don’t need slogans, I just need somebody to take it seriously.

I’ve been voting that way since I was old enough to pull a lever, and it’s not because I memorize every speech or fall in love with a candidate’s personality. It’s because I know which side I’m on. I line up with the people who believe in keeping government in its lane, respecting property, and not turning every problem into something run out of Washington. That’s my camp. Always has been.

So when Trump’s the Republican on the ballot, that’s where my vote goes. I’m not shopping around every four years like it’s a tractor dealership. I’m looking at which team is closer to how I see the world, and it’s not the Democrats. Hasn’t been for a long time.

You can call that partisan if you want. I call it being consistent. I don’t need a sermon about personalities—I know which side I trust more to run things without running over people like me.

dislike_rep_candidate.reason_category: Comments about temperament, honesty, likability, morality, or personal behavior.
dislike_rep_candidate.text:
Yeah. I’ll tell it to you straight: the man’s mouth gets him in trouble, and sometimes the rest of us along with him.

I don’t need a sermon, I need the numbers—but I also need a president who knows when to shut up and keep things steady. There’ve been times I’ve listened to him talk and thought, “Why are you picking that fight right now?” It’s like a bull pushing through a fence just because it’s there. You might like the bull, but you still end up fixing fence all afternoon.

It’s not about being polished or fancy—I don’t care about that. I’ve dealt with plenty of rough-edged men who were solid as a rock. But there’s a difference between plainspoken and just stirring the pot because you can. When it starts feeling more like ego than discipline, I get wary. I’ve run a place my whole life, and if I acted on every impulse or said every thought that crossed my mind, I’d have lost this farm 30 years ago.

So yeah—if I ever pulled back from voting for him, it wouldn’t be some policy paper. It’d be that. Temperament. Whether he can keep his footing without turning everything into a personal grudge match. That matters more than people like to admit.

dislike_dem_party.reason_category: Support or opposition based on party identity, ideological labels, or general alignment (e.g., liberal/conservative).
dislike_dem_party.text:
Yeah, there is. I don’t care for what they stand for, plain and simple. It’s a liberal party, and I’m not. That’s the root of it.

I’ve spent my whole life leaning conservative—handle your own business, don’t let somebody far off tell you how to run your land, your money, your family. The Democratic Party, as I see it, runs the other direction. More government, more central say-so, more belief that somebody in an office knows better than the people living it. That dog won’t hunt on this place.

And it’s not just one issue or another—I’m talking about the whole posture. The way they talk, the way they frame things, it all comes out of that liberal mindset. I read enough to know how they present it, and I’m telling you, it doesn’t line up with how I think a country ought to be run. I’m on the Republican side because it fits how I see the world working day to day.

dislike_rep_party.reason_category: Comments about temperament, honesty, likability, morality, or personal behavior.
dislike_rep_party.text:
Yeah, I’ll tell you straight: sometimes our own people act like fools, and I don’t have patience for it.

I’m on the Republican side, no question, but that doesn’t mean I’m blind. You get some of these folks—loud, puffed up, talking like everything’s a fight they’ve already won—and half the time they don’t sound serious, they sound like they’re performing. Chest-thumping, calling names, talking like decency is optional as long as you’re “owning” the other side. That may get applause somewhere, but it doesn’t build trust. And if I can’t trust your word, I don’t care what letter’s next to your name.

Where I come from, your word either holds or it doesn’t. You show up, you tell the truth even when it costs you, and you don’t act like a fool in public. Some Republicans forget that. They start sounding slippery or mean-spirited, like they’re more interested in scoring points than being steady. That dog won’t hunt on this place. I don’t need a hero, I need somebody who acts like an adult and keeps their story straight.

So yeah—I’m with the party, but I’ll say it plain: when our side starts acting like character doesn’t matter, that’s when I get irritated. Because it does matter. Always has.

like_dem_candidate.reason_category: Positions on abortion, Roe v. Wade, reproductive freedom, or pro-life/pro-choice issues.
like_dem_candidate.text:
I’ll be straight with you, the one area where I even pause is the whole abortion thing, Roe v. Wade, all that. I’m a church guy, so yeah, my instinct is pro-life, I don’t hide that. But I’ve also been around long enough to see real life not fit clean into slogans. It’s not always some simple “just do this” situation like people on my side try to make it.

What makes me at least listen to someone like her is that Democrats, including her, are pretty firm on keeping that decision with the woman, not the government. And even if I don’t line up with that fully, I get the argument that you don’t want politicians making those calls across the board. That part hits me more than some of the other stuff they talk about.

On our side, sometimes it feels like they want a one-size-fits-all rule and call it done. Let’s not get cute with it — life is messier than that. I’ve known people, not super close but around church and jobs, where the situation wasn’t black and white, and you can tell it weighs on them. So when Democrats push that it should stay a personal decision, I don’t just dismiss it.

Does that mean I’m jumping over and voting for her? Probably not. But if there’s one thing that even gets me to consider it for a second, it’s that — how her side handles abortion compared to mine.

dislike_dem_candidate.reason_category: Mentions of the economy, jobs, inflation, wages, taxation, government spending, or overall financial management.
dislike_dem_candidate.text:
Yeah, I mean… for me it comes down to money, plain and simple. I’m out here every day seeing what things cost now versus a few years ago, and it’s not subtle. Parts are up, gas is up, customers are more stressed about bills, and that rolls right downhill to guys like me. So when I look at her and that whole side of things, I just connect it with more of the same—more spending, more pressure, and regular people kind of eating it.

I’m not sitting there reading policy line by line, that’s not how I operate. I go off what I see in my work and what my customers are dealing with. And right now, it feels like their side doesn’t really get how tight things are for people trying to keep a small business running or just keep a house comfortable without going broke. It’s always talk, talk, talk—but my supply house invoice doesn’t care about talk. Numbers don’t have feelings.

So yeah, if I’m voting against her, it’s because I don’t trust that direction on the economy. Feels like our side is at least trying to ease the load, and their side just keeps adding to it. That’s how it looks from my truck, anyway.

like_rep_candidate.reason_category: Views on immigration levels, border security, undocumented immigrants, or asylum policies.
like_rep_candidate.text:
Yeah, I mean, for me it mostly comes down to the border situation, straight up. I’m not into all the back-and-forth talking points, but from what I see, Republicans at least act like they want some order there, and Trump especially—he’s been consistent on that. It’s not about hating anybody, I’m Hispanic, I’ve got family stories like everyone else. But there’s a difference between people coming through a process and just… everything feeling wide open.

Out here, you hear it from customers, from guys at the supply house—people feel like Democrats just brush it off or turn it into some moral argument, and meanwhile regular neighborhoods are dealing with the reality of it. And that bugs me, because it turns into “you’re either with us or you’re a bad person,” and I don’t buy that. You can respect people and still want rules that actually mean something.

Trump, for all his rough edges, he doesn’t play that game. He’s more like, no, we’re locking this down, period. And that lands with guys like me. It feels like at least someone’s saying, “Hey, this isn’t crazy to want some control over who’s coming in and how.”

So yeah, if I’m being honest, that’s the main thing. Not everything, but that’s the piece where I lean his way over the Democrats, no question.

like_dem_party.reason_category: Support or opposition based on party identity, ideological labels, or general alignment (e.g., liberal/conservative).
like_dem_party.text:
Honestly? Not really, not in a way where I’d say “yeah, that’s my side.” It’s just not how I see the world. They come off more big-government, more top-down, and I’ve always been more on the side of handling your own business, keeping things steady, not having everything run through politics.

I’m not saying every single person in that party is bad or anything like that, I’ve met plenty of decent people who lean that way. But as a party identity, it feels like they’re speaking a different language than guys like me. I don’t see myself in it.

So yeah, it’s less about one thing I “like” and more that, overall, it’s just not my lane.

dislike_dem_party.reason_category: Views on international relations, war, defense, global leadership, or national security.
dislike_dem_party.text:
Yeah, I’ll be straight with you—when it comes to how they handle the world outside our borders, I don’t trust Democrats to keep things steady. It feels like they’re always reacting, not setting the tone. Like, one minute we’re trying to look tough, next minute we’re backing off, and everybody else can see that.

From my side of things, you want the U.S. to be clear and consistent—friends know where we stand, enemies don’t test you. With Democrats, it comes off kind of mixed signals, like they’re more worried about how it looks than how it lands. And out here, regular guys like me, we’re the ones who end up paying when things get shaky—gas prices, supply stuff, all that.

I’m not saying Republicans are perfect, but at least it feels like our side believes in strength first, not second-guessing everything. With Democrats, I get this sense they’re trying to manage perceptions instead of actually leading. And in my experience, whether it’s a job site or a country, if you look unsure, people push you.

like_rep_party.reason_category: Views on international relations, war, defense, global leadership, or national security.
like_rep_party.text:
Yeah, I mean, if I’m being straight, the main thing is I feel like Republicans take security and all that global stuff more seriously. Like, they don’t come off as trying to be liked by everybody overseas, they come off as making sure we’re not the ones getting pushed around. That matters to me. I don’t follow every headline, I’m in attics half the day, but you still see the difference in tone—one side feels like “let’s be careful how this looks,” the other side feels like “handle it so it doesn’t come back on us.”

From my point of view, the world’s not some friendly job site where everyone’s playing fair. It’s more like a bad install—you don’t stay ahead of it, it bites you later. I’d rather be on the side that assumes that upfront instead of reacting after the fact.

And yeah, I know people argue about how they go about it, I’m not saying it’s perfect. But between the two, I trust Republicans more to keep things steady on that front. I don’t want drama overseas turning into problems here at home. That’s really it.

like_dem_candidate.text:
Yeah—honestly, the clearest thing for me is where she stands on abortion and keeping it legal.

I’m Protestant, so this isn’t abstract for me. I believe there’s real moral weight there. But I also live in the world as it actually functions, with students and neighbors and my own family, and I’ve seen what happens when policy gets too blunt. When Roe got overturned, it didn’t land as some clean moral correction—it landed as confusion, fear, and a patchwork where your zip code suddenly determines your options. And that hits working families the hardest, because they don’t have the money or flexibility to just “go somewhere else.”

So when Harris talks about protecting access, what I hear is: we’re not going to let this turn into a situation where a complicated, often painful decision gets forced into a one-size-fits-all rule enforced by the state. I can live with a country where there’s tension and people disagree and we try to reduce abortions through support and responsibility. I have a harder time living with a system that removes the choice entirely and pretends that solves the underlying reality.

I’m not trying to win a Twitter argument about it. I just think, on this specific issue, her position lines up more with how messy real life is, and that matters to me.

like_rep_candidate.reason_category: Evaluations of a candidate’s qualifications, preparedness, track record, or ability to govern.
like_rep_candidate.text:
Yeah—if I’m being very specific about it, the one thing that would push me in that direction is his track record on the economy pre-COVID.

I’m not talking in slogans, I mean the boring math of it. When I look back at those years, what I remember is relatively low unemployment and a sense—at least locally—that businesses were hiring and people could move jobs a little easier. In my world, with students who are juggling work and school, that matters a lot more than abstract GDP numbers. If the labor market is tight in a good way, my students have options, and that changes their whole decision tree.

Now, I’m not saying he single-handedly caused that or that everything about that period was clean or sustainable. But if I’m evaluating purely on “who has actually presided over a strong job market in recent memory,” he can point to that period in a concrete way. And for me, with a family and an income that’s not exactly cushioned, that kind of stability isn’t theoretical—it’s rent, groceries, car repairs.

So that’s the lane where I’d even consider it: not personality, not rhetoric, just that specific governing outcome and whether I think it would translate again.

dislike_dem_party.reason_category: References to crime, policing, public safety, gun rights or gun control.
dislike_dem_party.text:
Yeah—if I’m being honest, the place I tense up is around how some Democrats talk about policing and public safety.

I’m not coming at that as “law and order slogan guy.” I teach inequality for a living, I get the structural side. But I live in Dayton, I’ve got two kids, and I pay attention to what actually makes a neighborhood feel stable. When I hear messaging that sounds like “pull back on policing” without a really concrete, Tuesday-morning plan for what replaces it, that makes me uneasy.

Let’s do the boring math for a second: if response times go up, if officers are stretched thin or disengaged, the people who pay first are not the well-off neighborhoods. It’s my students who are already juggling jobs and childcare, it’s families who can’t just move. I’ve had students tell me straight up they don’t call things in anymore because they don’t think anyone’s coming. That’s not abstract—that’s daily life.

So it’s not that I’m against reform. I am for accountability, training, all of that. But when the party messaging drifts into what feels like minimizing the need for consistent, visible policing, I just don’t think it matches what working families actually need to feel safe. And I’m not trying to win a Twitter argument about it—I’m thinking about getting my kids home, getting to work, and knowing if something goes wrong, someone shows up.

like_rep_party.reason_category: Mentions of the economy, jobs, inflation, wages, taxation, government spending, or overall financial management.
like_rep_party.text:
Yeah—if I strip it down to one thing, it’s that they at least *talk like the math has to work* when it comes to spending.

Let me put it in my terms: I run my household on a pretty tight margin. Rent, groceries, car repairs—there’s no “we’ll just float it for a few years and see.” So when I hear Republicans emphasize cutting spending or being cautious about new programs, that part lands with me. Not because I think they always execute it cleanly—they don’t—but the instinct of “what does this cost, and who’s actually paying for it?” feels grounded in reality.

I think where it hits me emotionally is inflation. The last few years, you just feel it in the grocery store, and there’s this sense that nobody in power is really living inside that constraint. So when Republicans frame things around tightening the belt or not expanding government further, I find myself nodding, at least at the level of principle. I can live with imperfect, I can’t live with chaotic—and big, unchecked spending starts to feel chaotic when you’re the one absorbing the downstream costs.

Democrats—at least the way I see it—they’re the only ones even trying to keep folks from getting buried over doctor visits and prescriptions.

I’m not saying they fix it. Half the time it’s still a mess. But it’s the difference between somebody at least saying “we ought to help with that” and the other side acting like if you can’t pay, that’s your problem. When you’re living it, that matters.

Yeah—if I strip it down to one thing, it’s how I think she’d approach spending and inflation.

I’m pretty sensitive to anything that feels like adding fuel when prices are already hard to predict. In our house, we’ve built everything on consistency—fixed costs, planned increases, no surprises—and the last few years of inflation kind of blew holes in that, even at our income level. Groceries, insurance, even kids’ activities just drift up faster than you can “optimize” around.

So when I hear proposals that lean toward more federal spending without a really clear offset or a timeline for how it settles back down, I get cautious. I’m not saying don’t invest in anything—I’m not allergic to change—but I want to see the Tuesday version: what does this do to prices, borrowing, and taxes in a normal week, not a press release week? If that piece doesn’t feel grounded, that’s when I start leaning against someone.

I’m a systems person, and I need to trust that the basic rules of the system hold, especially around elections and peaceful transitions. The way he handled the 2020 election—pushing claims that didn’t hold up in court and putting pressure on officials—that breaks my confidence in the process itself.

So it’s not really about liking or disliking him personally. If I can’t rely on someone to respect the outcome when the numbers are clear, it’s hard for me to justify voting for them, because then the rest of the system starts to feel negotiable.

I’m not looking for perfection, but I do want a president who can set a plan and keep people aligned long enough to deliver on it. That consistency piece is where I’d hesitate.

I’m not anti-reform. I get that there are real issues in policing, and I’m not trying to defend bad behavior. But what makes me hesitate is when the policy vibe feels like it swings too far toward “pull back enforcement first, figure out the rest later.” That’s a failure mode I don’t like, because public safety is one of those systems where if it degrades, it degrades fast and unevenly.

Like, even just locally around Dallas, you can tell the difference between areas where enforcement is consistent versus where it’s kind of sporadic. Response times slip, petty stuff stops getting addressed, and then it compounds. It’s not dramatic headline crime—it’s the day-to-day baseline feeling of “is this area under control or not.” And once people lose that confidence, it’s hard to rebuild.

So when I hear her talk or see the kinds of policies she’s aligned with, my concern is: what’s the actual incentive structure for maintaining order while doing reform? Because if the implementation ends up signaling hesitation or inconsistency to law enforcement, you get this weird middle state where neither accountability nor safety is working well.

I might be wrong on some specifics—I’m not inside those policy rooms—but that’s the part that would push me toward voting against her. It feels a little too close to a vibes-based plan on something that really can’t be vibes-based.

Yeah—if I strip everything else out, the thing I consistently respect is how they treat healthcare as something you actually have to make accessible, not just “available on paper.”

That’s not abstract for me. My life is basically a recurring loop of specialist visits, labs, meds, insurance approvals—the whole stack. And the failure mode is super predictable: if coverage is spotty or costs spike, people delay care, then it turns into something more expensive and harder to treat. I’ve seen that even just inside clinic waiting rooms, like people spacing out follow-ups because of cost, then coming back worse. That’s a system bug, not a personal failure.

So when Democrats push for things like expanding Medicaid or capping prescription costs, I get why. It’s not about being nice, it’s about making the system actually function end-to-end. If your inputs are “people can’t afford to stay on their meds,” the output is worse health and higher downstream costs. Don’t make it a mystery problem.

Even with my situation—I’m on my parents’ insurance, high-income household, I’m not the one getting crushed by bills—but I’m close enough to the process to see how fragile it is. One coverage change, one expensive medication, and it gets complicated fast. So yeah, I don’t align with them on everything, but on healthcare access and cost control, at least they’re trying to solve a real bottleneck instead of pretending it’ll sort itself out.

Yeah—so if I had to pick something specific, it’s when Republicans actually focus on lowering prescription drug costs and making healthcare pricing less opaque. That’s not abstract for me, it’s like… weekly life logistics.

With my autoimmune stuff, I’ve been in that loop of specialty meds, prior authorizations, insurance back-and-forth, and the pharmacy just casually telling you something is four figures unless a bunch of boxes get checked. It’s not a “debate topic” at that point, it’s whether you can stay on the treatment your doctor says is working without turning it into a part-time job. And I’ve seen some Republican proposals—more on the transparency/competition side, not like giant system overhauls—that at least try to attack that mess where nobody can tell you the real price until it’s too late.

I’m not saying they’ve solved it, because honestly the system still feels like a black box half the time. But I do like the framing of “why is this so expensive and who’s actually setting the price,” and pushing on that. When you’re the one refreshing a patient portal, calling insurance, then calling the pharmacy to reconcile three different answers, anything that reduces that friction is very real. Don’t make it a mystery problem—healthcare billing is currently a mystery problem.

But there’s a difference between dealing with a problem and acting like the only acceptable answer is to blow up the current system before the replacement’s ready. That’s where I part ways with them. It feels like our side—people who actually have to live with the consequences day-to-day—gets treated like we’re in the way instead of being the ones who have to make it work.

If you want the truth, I don’t trust a plan that looks good on paper but doesn’t survive a bad winter or a long outage. Around here, if it doesn’t hold up under stress, it’s not a solution yet. Look, I’m dealing with this every week with my mother. After her stroke, we’ve got a stack of medications, and even with coverage, the pricing is all over the place. One refill is reasonable, next month same thing suddenly higher, no clear reason. And then you try to get a straight answer—pharmacy says call insurance, insurance says ask the doctor, doctor’s office says talk to the plan. That’s a whole afternoon gone, just chasing paperwork. That’s not a system, that’s a maze.

So when I look at someone like Kamala Harris, what I’m asking is simple: are you actually going to make this pricing transparent and predictable, or is it more talking? Because I’ve heard a lot of “we’ll lower costs,” but on the ground, for people like me managing real prescriptions and elder care, it doesn’t feel simpler or cheaper. That’s a brochure answer versus real life.

I’m not saying she’s worse than the other side—I still vote Democrat. But if I feel like it’s going to stay complicated and expensive with no real pressure on drug companies and insurers, then yeah, that makes me hesitate. Show me the numbers, show me the change in a way I can see on a monthly statement, not a speech.

I look at my actual costs—utilities, parts, payroll—and whether policy makes those steady or makes them jump around.

During his time, what bothered me was the unpredictability around tariffs and trade stuff. I remember very clearly when parts prices on some of my machines jumped because of import changes—nothing dramatic on TV, but for me it meant a repair that used to cost a few hundred suddenly creeping up, and you can’t just pass that to customers right away. That kind of swing, it messes with planning. I run three stores, I’ve got a commercial loan, I don’t have room for surprises like that.

So it’s not about liking or not liking him personally. It’s that I want boring, steady, predictable numbers. If policy makes my costs jump around and I have to keep guessing month to month, that’s enough for me to say no. Show me the numbers, make them stable, then we can talk.

At one of my stores, summer two years ago, my gas and electric jumped hard—machines running all day, dryers nonstop, and the rate changes… you feel it immediately. I had to look at upgrading a few older dryers and water heaters to more efficient ones, not because I wanted something “green” for image, but because if I don’t control that usage, it eats my margin. What I saw from the Democratic side—pushing efficiency standards, rebates, that kind of thing—it lines up with reality for me. Lower waste, more predictable energy cost, less surprise when the bill comes. That’s not politics to me, that’s survival math.

I’m not interested in slogans about saving the planet in some abstract way. I’m looking at: will this help me run machines without bleeding money every month, and will it keep things stable long-term when weather gets more extreme? I run three stores. If my attendant closes the register one way on Monday and a different way on Friday, customers start asking questions, and pretty soon nobody trusts the numbers. Same idea. When I hear Democrats say things like “don’t worry about this procedure” or push changes that feel rushed or uneven depending on who benefits, it makes me uncomfortable. Maybe their intention is good, maybe they think they’re helping access, I understand that part—but if people start feeling like the rules move depending on the situation, that’s dangerous. Once trust in the system goes, it doesn’t come back easy.

Show me the numbers, show me the same rules applied the same way every time, then I’m fine. I’ll give you one real example. Back around 2009, when everything tightened up, I had one store barely holding on — machines breaking, customers cutting back, bank breathing down my neck. At that time, programs and policies that leaned more on support — keeping people spending a little, helping small operators not collapse — that mattered. If everything just gets cut in the name of being “lean,” guys like me don’t have cushion, we just drop. Big companies, they survive. Small people, we disappear.

So when I hear that Republican message about shrinking things, pulling back, letting the market sort it out — I don’t buy it. That sounds clean, but real life is not clean. Show me the numbers, then we’ll talk, but from what I’ve seen, that philosophy leaves too many regular people exposed.

I mean, look, I’m pretty clearly on the Republican side, but I’ll give them this—Democrats don’t shy away from talking about people who are getting crushed by medical costs. Like, that part’s real. I’ve seen it even with customers… guys come in, solid job, and then one health thing hits and it just wipes out their cash flow. It’s not theoretical.

So yeah, I respect that they actually push on stuff like lowering prescription costs or making sure people don’t just get left hanging if something bad happens. I don’t love how they go about it half the time, feels like big system, big control, all that. But the core idea—hey, if someone gets sick they shouldn’t be financially wrecked for years—that’s fair.

I’m more in the “handle your business” camp personally, but I’m not blind to the fact that some hits are bigger than what most people can realistically absorb. That’s probably the one area where I’ll say, yeah, they’re at least aiming at a real problem.

like_rep_party.reason_category: Mentions of the economy, jobs, inflation, wages, taxation, government spending, or overall financial management.
like_rep_party.text:
Yeah, for me it’s pretty simple—it comes down to money and how the economy actually feels day to day. I like that Republicans, at least the way I’ve experienced it, talk about keeping taxes lower and not just spending like there’s no ceiling. When my income swings month to month, that stuff matters a lot more than people think. A strong month feels great, but if too much of that gets eaten up or everything around me is getting more expensive, it kind of kills the momentum.

I’ve just noticed—like, when our side is running things, it tends to feel more predictable to plan around. Gas, groceries, even how people are buying at the dealership… it all ties together. When things get tight, customers hesitate, deals slow down, and then my income takes the hit. So yeah, I’m going to lean toward the side that I feel keeps the economy moving and doesn’t make it harder to get ahead.

I’m not saying they’re perfect, but between the two, Republicans line up way more with how I’m trying to build something and actually keep what I earn.

dislike_rep_party.reason_category: Support or opposition based on party identity, ideological labels, or general alignment (e.g., liberal/conservative).
dislike_rep_party.text:
Yeah, I mean, I’m solidly on that side, but I wouldn’t say it’s perfect.

If I had to pick something, it’s when Republicans start drifting too soft trying to sound like the other side. Like when they blur the lines just to seem more “reasonable” or media-friendly, it kind of defeats the point. I’m on that team because it’s supposed to stand for a different set of values, not just be a slightly toned-down version of Democrats. When it starts feeling like they’re chasing approval from people who are never going to back them anyway, that’s frustrating.

I’d rather they just be clear and own what they believe, even if it’s not popular in every room.

like_dem_candidate.reason_category: References to race, gender, religion, or symbolic representation of social groups.
like_dem_candidate.text:
Yeah. I’ll be blunt about it—it matters to me that she’s a Black woman in that role.

Not as a slogan, not as some feel-good checkbox. I’m talking about what it signals in a very real, ground-level way. I’ve spent most of my life in workplaces where leadership didn’t look anything like a big chunk of the people actually doing the work. Warehouse floors, vendor calls, contractor counters—you see who’s carrying things and who’s making decisions, and there’s a gap. It’s not abstract to me.

So when someone like her is at the top of the ticket, it tells me—at least directionally—that the party is willing to put someone there who reflects more of the country as it actually is, not just the same recycled profile. That lands with me. It feels like movement, not talk.

If you want it cleaner than that: I’m more likely to vote for her because she’s a Black woman who’s actually in contention for the presidency. That’s the reason.

dislike_dem_candidate.reason_category: Evaluations of a candidate’s qualifications, preparedness, track record, or ability to govern.
dislike_dem_candidate.text:
Yeah—if I’m being blunt, it’s that I still don’t have a clear read on how she actually runs things when it’s her operation.

I’m not talking about speeches or positions. I mean execution. When something messy hits—conflicting priorities, agencies stepping on each other, timelines slipping—what does her decision process look like, and does it hold up under pressure? I haven’t seen a clean track record there. It feels like a lot of starts and resets, not a system that hums.

And for me, that’s the job. I don’t care how good the plan sounds if it doesn’t survive Monday morning. If I can’t picture how you’re going to move people, align teams, and stick the landing, that’s where I start leaning away.

like_dem_party.reason_category: Mentions of climate change, environmental policy, energy production, or sustainability.
like_dem_party.text:
Yeah. They at least take climate and energy seriously in a way that maps to the real world.

I work in a business where weather and energy costs aren’t abstract—they hit demand, supply, and margins all at once. Hotter summers, weird shoulder seasons, storms knocking out routes… that’s not a debate topic, that’s Tuesday. What I like is Democrats actually pushing for a grid that can handle that—more distributed energy, storage, upgrading transmission—so you’re not one failure away from a mess. Same with efficiency standards. People love to roll their eyes, but better equipment standards mean less strain on the system and fewer emergency calls when it’s 98 degrees and something gives out.

It’s not perfect, and half the time the rollout gets sloppy, but at least they’re trying to build something that survives contact with reality. The alternative is pretending none of it is happening, and that’s just dumb.

dislike_dem_party.reason_category: Non-specific praise or criticism (e.g., 'good,' 'bad,' 'for the people') without clear issue or trait content.
dislike_dem_party.text:
Yeah. They get way too wrapped up in sounding right instead of just being useful.

I’ll vote blue, I do, but half the time I’m listening to people from the party and it feels like they’re trying to win an argument on paper instead of actually helping somebody get through a Tuesday. Everything’s phrased perfectly, checks all the boxes, but you walk it into a real situation and it just… floats. No grip.

I see it at work all the time—someone comes in with a clean, impressive idea, and I’m sitting there thinking, “Okay, but who’s actually doing this at 7 a.m. when the shipment’s late?” If it doesn’t survive Monday morning, it’s just a slide deck. And the party drifts into that mode way too often for me.

dislike_rep_party.reason_category: Mentions of the economy, jobs, inflation, wages, taxation, government spending, or overall financial management.
dislike_rep_party.text:
Yeah—how they handle the economy, straight up.

I don’t buy this constant push of big tax cuts and “it’ll sort itself out.” I’ve lived through what that looks like on the ground. You cut revenue, you still spend, and then everyone acts surprised when things get tighter or more volatile. Meanwhile regular people are dealing with price swings, wages that don’t keep up cleanly, and businesses trying to plan without any stability.

In operations, if my inputs are unpredictable—costs, supply, labor—I can’t run a clean system. Same thing here. I don’t need perfect, I need steady. What I see from them is a lot of talk about growth with no real attention to how it plays out week to week for people trying to budget or run a business. It doesn’t survive Monday morning.

like_dem_candidate.reason_category: Concerns about democratic norms, elections, corruption, legal issues, or threats to democracy.
like_dem_candidate.text:
I’ll tell you plain: the main thing that would push me toward her is this question of whether we’re still going to respect the results of elections in this country. That’s not abstract to me. If we start acting like votes only count when your side wins, then everything else people argue about doesn’t even matter anymore.

I’m not being funny, I’m being factual. I’ve lived long enough to see rules bent, paperwork “lost,” decisions quietly changed after the fact. Once you let that mindset get comfortable at the top level—where people feel like they can challenge or ignore outcomes they don’t like—you’re asking for confusion at every level below it. Schools, courts, local offices… all of it depends on people accepting outcomes, even when they’re not happy about them.

So when I look at Democrats versus Republicans right now, I see one side that, whatever else I may disagree with, is at least saying, “The process stands, the vote stands.” And the other side has folks who still can’t say that clean and straight. That troubles me. I’ve got grandkids coming up—I need them in a country where the basic rules are the basic rules, not something people negotiate after the fact.

That doesn’t mean I agree with everything she or her party does. I don’t. But on that specific point—keeping the structure intact, respecting elections—that carries real weight with me. Because once that goes, you don’t just fix it with a new slogan.

dislike_dem_candidate.reason_category: Support or opposition based on party identity, ideological labels, or general alignment (e.g., liberal/conservative).
dislike_dem_candidate.text:
I’ll be straight with you—it’s the party she represents. I don’t line up with that side of the aisle, and that matters to me more than any one speech or moment. The Democratic approach, as I’ve seen it over the years, feels too loose with structure, too quick to grow government, and not grounded enough in personal responsibility. And I’m not being funny, I’m being factual—that’s just not how I was raised or how I run my household.

I’ve voted enough years to know I’m not switching back and forth every cycle. I might call myself independent, but when it comes down to it, I lean the other way because it fits my values better—order, accountability, doing what you said you’d do. So when I look at her, I don’t separate her from that whole system. She’s part of that team, and that’s the part I don’t trust.

like_rep_candidate.reason_category: Support or opposition based on party identity, ideological labels, or general alignment (e.g., liberal/conservative).
like_rep_candidate.text:
I’ll be plain with you. If I end up voting for him, it’s because I line up more with that side of the aisle than I do with the Democrats right now. I call myself independent, but when I look at how the parties carry themselves, the Republicans are closer to how I think things ought to be run—more order, more accountability, less of this everything-goes attitude.

The Democratic side, to me, feels too loose these days. Too much bending over backwards, too much talking in circles. I don’t recognize it the way I used to. And I’m not being funny, I’m being factual—that kind of direction makes me uneasy.

So it’s less about him as a personality and more about which team is at least speaking a language I recognize. Right now, that tends to be the Republican side more than the other.

dislike_rep_candidate.reason_category: Comments about temperament, honesty, likability, morality, or personal behavior.
dislike_rep_candidate.text:
Yes. I’m going to say it plain: I don’t like the way that man carries himself.

I was raised, and I raised my kids, that your word and your behavior matter. You don’t just say anything, you don’t just tear people down because you can, and then act like it’s strength. That’s not strength to me, that’s a lack of discipline. And I’ve watched him over the years—how he talks about people, how he doubles down instead of owning anything—and it doesn’t sit right in my spirit. I’m not being funny, I’m being factual.

On our side, I expect a certain level of respect and steadiness, even when things get heated. I don’t need perfect, but I do need someone who at least tries to act like they understand the weight of the role. With him, too often it feels like ego first, country second. And I can’t get comfortable lining up behind that, no matter what else is going on.

like_dem_party.reason_category: Mentions of LGBTQ rights, gender identity, transgender issues, or related cultural debates.
like_dem_party.text:
I’ll tell you straight—I do respect that they’ve taken a firm stand on how people live their personal lives, especially around all this LGBTQ and gender identity conversation. They’re very clear about it, and they don’t waffle. If someone says, “This is who I am,” the Democratic Party is going to back that up, no hesitation. That kind of consistency—I can recognize it.

Now, I don’t agree with how far they push it, not the way it’s being brought into schools and everyday life, especially with children. That’s where I part ways, strongly. But I can still say this: on that issue, they are unified, they’re not confused, and they don’t leave their people guessing where they stand. Compared to the other side sometimes stumbling over their words or trying to please everybody, Democrats are locked in on that, and I notice it.

dislike_dem_party.reason_category: Mentions of LGBTQ rights, gender identity, transgender issues, or related cultural debates.
dislike_dem_party.text:
I’ll tell you straight, and I’m not being funny, I’m being factual—the way the Democratic Party handles all this gender identity and transgender conversation, it doesn’t sit right with me at all.

It feels like they’ve decided everyone else just has to go along with it, no questions, no hesitation, or you’re labeled something ugly. And I don’t respond well to that. I spent years in a school office—children need clarity, structure, and adults who are steady. What I see from that side is a lot of confusion being introduced into places that should be very clear for kids. You’ve got grown folks arguing over basic definitions, and then expecting teachers and parents to just adjust overnight like it’s nothing. That’s not how real life works.

And I’ll say this too—there’s a tone to it. It’s not just “this is what we believe,” it’s “if you don’t agree exactly, you’re the problem.” I don’t deal well with being talked down to like that. You can’t build trust that way, especially not with people like me who’ve been navigating systems our whole lives and know when something feels forced.

So yes, that’s my main issue with them. It feels like they’ve gone too far in that direction, and they’re not leaving room for regular people to ask reasonable questions without getting shut down.

like_rep_party.text:
Yes, I’ll say this plain. What I tend to like is the way Republicans *carry themselves*—there’s more of a sense that your word should mean something, that you show up, you handle your business, you don’t make a whole performance out of every feeling. I’m not being funny, I’m being factual. It comes across as more grounded to me, like people who believe in saying what they mean and standing on it, even if it’s not popular.

And compared to the other side, which can feel… slippery, like the message changes depending on who’s in the room, Republicans read to me as more straightforward in their character. You may not like what they say, but you generally know where they stand. I have more respect for that kind of consistency than all this shifting and soft language that sounds nice but doesn’t hold up when you look twice.

dislike_dem_candidate.reason_category: Non-specific praise or criticism (e.g., 'good,' 'bad,' 'for the people') without clear issue or trait content.
dislike_dem_candidate.text:
I’ll be plain about it: she just doesn’t sit right with me in a general, gut-level way. When I listen to her, I don’t feel steadiness—I feel a kind of… I don’t know, polish without much underneath it. And that matters to me more than people think. At my age, after dealing with banks, contracts, doctors, all of it, you get a feel for who you trust to carry responsibility and who you don’t.

So if I’m voting, I’m not parsing every line item. I’m asking myself, “Do I feel comfortable putting this person in charge of something that affects my land, my tenants, my day-to-day life?” And with her, I hesitate. It feels like more of the same from that side of the aisle—very packaged, very practiced, and I don’t quite believe it.

I’m not saying she’s terrible, and I’m not interested in tearing her down for sport. But I do think people on her side sometimes assume we’ll just go along because the messaging sounds right. I’m not allergic to change, I’m allergic to chaos—and she doesn’t give me a sense of calm, reliable leadership. That alone is enough to make me consider voting the other way.

dislike_rep_candidate.reason_category: Evaluations of a candidate’s qualifications, preparedness, track record, or ability to govern.
dislike_rep_candidate.text:
Well… yes, and for me it comes down to whether someone can actually run the government in a steady, competent way. I’m not looking for a show, I’m looking for management. And when I watched his time in office, what I saw—over and over—was chaos where there should have been process. Staff turning over constantly, agencies not aligned, decisions announced before they were thought through… that’s not how you run anything that has real consequences.

I spent years keeping a vineyard afloat, and before that in a dental office where you follow procedure because people get hurt if you don’t. Government is that same principle at a larger scale. You need someone who respects systems, who hires qualified people and keeps them in place long enough to do their jobs, and who can carry a plan from start to finish without it unraveling halfway through.

From my side of the aisle, I expect a baseline of competence and continuity. Not perfection, but steadiness. And I just didn’t see that with him. It felt improvised, and “improvised” is not what you want when you’re responsible for a whole country.

So it’s less about personality for me and more about: can this person govern in a way that holds together under pressure? And based on what I’ve already seen, I don’t have confidence there.

like_dem_party.reason_category: Views on immigration levels, border security, undocumented immigrants, or asylum policies.
like_dem_party.text:
Well… yes, but I’ll be plain about what it is, because I don’t like pretending I’m enthusiastic about everything they do.

What I appreciate about Democrats—*our side*, I suppose—is that they start from the idea that immigration is about people first, not just lines on a map. I’ve lived long enough, and in enough different places, to know that newcomers aren’t some abstract threat. In San Francisco especially, you saw families building a life from nothing, working jobs nobody else wanted, sending their kids to school, trying to follow the rules even when the rules weren’t built for them. I have a hard time with the way Republicans talk about it sometimes—it gets very sharp, very dismissive, like the human part is an inconvenience.

Now, that doesn’t mean I think Democrats always handle it well. Sometimes it feels… loose, or like they’re avoiding the hard edges of the problem. And I’m not allergic to having a border or expecting people to come in through a system. Of course we need that. I’m not interested in chaos—never have been.

But if I’m choosing which side I’m more comfortable standing with, it’s the one that at least tries to keep some decency in the conversation. The tone matters to me. I want a country that can enforce rules without turning hard-hearted about it. And Democrats, for all their messiness, are closer to that than the other side right now.

like_rep_party.reason_category: Support or opposition based on party identity, ideological labels, or general alignment (e.g., liberal/conservative).
like_rep_party.text:
I’ll answer you plainly: yes, there is something I like, and it’s the general philosophy. I’ve always had a soft spot for the Republican instinct toward self-reliance and not having everything run through a central system. That idea—that people and communities should carry real responsibility, not just wait for solutions to come from above—that resonates with me. It probably comes from how my husband and I built the vineyard. Nobody was coming to rescue us in 1983. If we didn’t keep the books straight or make the next payment, that was on us.

So when Republicans talk about independence, about not overextending government into every corner of life, I understand the appeal. It feels grounded in a kind of realism about limits—financial, human, practical. I don’t hear that as “you’re on your own,” I hear it as “you’re accountable,” and I think there’s value in that. We can be kind and still be exact about responsibility.

Now, do I agree with them across the board? No, not even close. Sometimes that philosophy gets pushed so far it forgets people actually need support structures. But the core identity—the emphasis on personal responsibility and a certain skepticism of big, sweeping systems—that’s something I don’t dismiss. I recognize myself in that more than I’d probably admit in a church coffee hour.

I’ll answer you plainly, but I’m not interested in turning it into a shouting match.

What I don’t like is the overall direction of the party’s ideology—the way it’s moved further to the right in a way that feels rigid and, frankly, a bit suspicious of anything that isn’t its own camp. I’m not allergic to conservative ideas; I’ve lived long enough to see value in caution and tradition. But this version of the Republican Party… it leans into a kind of “we’re right, they’re wrong, and that’s the end of it” thinking that doesn’t sit well with me.

I’ve always thought of myself as someone who looks at what works, not which team gets credit. And the Republican Party, as it presents itself now, feels very much like a team identity first—like you’re expected to sign on to a whole package of beliefs and attitudes. That kind of ideological bundle makes me uneasy. Life isn’t that tidy. Running a household, managing land, working with a co-op—you don’t get to say “I’m only going to deal with problems that fit my side.” You take what’s useful and you adjust.

So when I watch them, I don’t feel invited into a conversation. I feel like I’m being asked to pick a side and stay there, and I don’t operate that way. That’s the part I don’t like—it feels narrower than it used to, and I don’t trust narrow thinking, no matter who it comes from.

Yes. She’s actually held multiple roles where you have to make decisions with real consequences, not just talk about them. Attorney general, U.S. senator, vice president—that’s a ladder where you learn how systems actually function, where they break, and what you can and can’t move quickly. That matters to me more than speeches. In my world, if you don’t understand how a system behaves on a bad day, you make things worse fast.

I’ve sat in enough discharge meetings where someone with a nice plan has zero sense of what insurance will approve or how long anything takes, and it falls apart by Friday. I don’t get that vibe from her. She’s worked inside big, messy institutions and still gotten things done, which tells me she knows where the real levers are. I’m not looking for perfect, I’m looking for someone who won’t need a year just to figure out how the machinery works. That’s the reason.

dislike_dem_candidate.reason_category: Positions on abortion, Roe v. Wade, reproductive freedom, or pro-life/pro-choice issues.
dislike_dem_candidate.text:
Yes. If she backed off strong access to abortion in any real way, that would push me against her, full stop.

I’m in a hospital every day. I’ve sat with women who are already hanging on by a thread—financially, medically, emotionally—and then you layer in a pregnancy that isn’t viable or isn’t safe, and suddenly it’s not a talking point, it’s a clock. You don’t have the luxury of “we’ll figure it out next week.” You need a legal, straightforward path to care, period. When that’s restricted or fuzzy, what actually happens is delays, worse outcomes, and families scrambling to cross state lines like they’re doing something wrong when they’re just trying to get basic medical care.

So for me it’s not abstract rights language. It’s: can a woman get the care her doctor says she needs without jumping through hoops that don’t make medical sense? If I got the sense that Harris would compromise that—soften it, carve exceptions that look neat on paper but fall apart in real life—that’s a dealbreaker. I don’t have patience for politicians who say they support women and then get slippery when it comes to the actual access piece. That’s a “maybe,” not a plan.

dislike_rep_candidate.reason_category: Concerns about democratic norms, elections, corruption, legal issues, or threats to democracy.
dislike_rep_candidate.text:
Yes. The way he handled the 2020 election is a hard stop for me.

I can work with a lot of policy differences, I really can, but I need someone to respect the basic rules of the system. When you lose, you concede, you don’t spend months telling people the vote was stolen without solid proof, and you don’t lean on officials to “find” votes. That’s not abstract to me—it’s about whether the process holds when it’s inconvenient. In my job, if people don’t trust the system, everything falls apart fast. Discharge plans, consent, safety—none of it works if folks think the rules are optional.

And I watched that period pretty closely, not as a hobby, just trying to understand what was actually happening. Courts looked at those claims, over and over, and they didn’t hold up. At some point you either accept the outcome or you’re telling the public the whole structure is illegitimate. That’s not a small thing you just move past.

So for me it’s not personality or tone or any of that. It’s that line: if you won’t respect an election result, then I don’t trust you with the job that depends on honoring it.

like_dem_party.reason_category: Mentions of the economy, jobs, inflation, wages, taxation, government spending, or overall financial management.
like_dem_party.text:
Yes. When they’re serious about it, they at least try to put a floor under people financially—things like expanding the Child Tax Credit and pushing for higher minimum wages. I’m not talking theory, I mean actual dollars showing up or not showing up in a household.

During COVID, I had patients who got that expanded tax credit, and you could see the difference immediately. Rent got paid on time, utilities stayed on, people weren’t choosing between insulin and groceries that week. It didn’t fix their lives, but it bought stability, which is half the battle. That matters more to me than any speech.

Now, do they overspend sometimes? Yes. Do they pretend money is infinite? Also yes. I’m not blind to that. But if you’re asking what I *like*, it’s that they at least aim policy at wages and household cash flow instead of just assuming the market will sort it out eventually. Because from where I sit, “eventually” is where people lose their apartments.

dislike_dem_party.reason_category: Non-specific praise or criticism (e.g., 'good,' 'bad,' 'for the people') without clear issue or trait content.
dislike_dem_party.text: Yeah—I don’t like how they talk. It’s too polished and too vague at the same time, which is a weird combination, but that’s how it lands. I’ll watch a press conference or read a statement and think, “Say it in plain English—what are you actually going to do on Monday?” It comes off like a lot of careful wording and not enough straight answers, and that makes it hard to trust, even when I probably agree with the underlying idea. In my world, if I explain a discharge plan that way, a family is going to be confused or think I’m hiding something. I need the next step, not the whole saga, and I don’t hear that from them a lot. It feels like they’re trying to cover every angle instead of just being clear and direct. And after a while, you tune it out because it starts to sound like background noise instead of something you can act on.

like_rep_party.reason_category: Views on immigration levels, border security, undocumented immigrants, or asylum policies.
like_rep_party.text:
Yes. They’re more willing to say out loud that borders actually matter and then act like it’s their job to manage them.

Here’s what I mean in plain English: I spend my days dealing with what happens when systems pretend capacity is unlimited. It isn’t. Housing isn’t. Clinics aren’t. Transportation definitely isn’t. When you have a steady flow of people coming in without a clear, enforceable process—who’s allowed in, how many, how they’re supported—you don’t get compassion, you get chaos. And chaos lands on regular people and on front-line systems like mine.

I’ve had patients where language barriers, no primary care, no stable address, and unclear eligibility all stack up at once. That’s not a moral judgment on them—it’s a logistics failure. And when policy folks act like any enforcement is cruelty, it drives me up the wall. Because what I need is a system that says: here’s the line, here’s the process, here’s what happens if you don’t follow it. That’s how you make anything function.

Republicans, at least in rhetoric and sometimes in policy, are more comfortable saying “you can’t just come in and figure it out later.” That basic stance—secure the border, set limits, enforce them—makes sense to me. It’s the difference between a “maybe” and a plan.
