like_dem_candidate.reason_category: Evaluations of a candidate’s qualifications, preparedness, track record, or ability to govern.
like_dem_candidate.text:
I’ll be straight with you, if I’m voting for her it’s not because I suddenly love everything with a D next to it—it’s because I think she can actually sit in that chair and run the place without it turning into a circus.

What I’m looking at is: has she been in rooms where decisions matter, or is she just talking? She’s been a prosecutor, ran a big state office, sat in the Senate, and then four years as Vice President. That’s not theory—that’s time spent inside the machine, seeing how the gears actually grind. You don’t get to fake it in those jobs. You’re dealing with budgets, agencies, people who don’t agree with you, and you still have to land the plane.

And look, I run a business—small compared to the federal government, obviously—but I can tell you this: there’s a difference between someone who’s read about pressure and someone who’s had three things breaking at once and still has to make payroll Friday. I want the second kind of person. From where I sit, she’s at least been under that kind of pressure in government. The other side, a lot of it feels like performance and slogans, and I don’t have patience for that. I need somebody who knows where the levers are and what happens when you pull them.

So yeah, it comes down to that for me—she looks like someone who’s already been doing the job at a high level, not somebody who’s going to need on-the-job training while the rest of us are trying to keep our own houses in order.

dislike_dem_candidate.reason_category: References to crime, policing, public safety, gun rights or gun control.
dislike_dem_candidate.text:
Yeah. For me it comes down to law and order, plain and simple, and I don’t think her side of the aisle takes it seriously the way they should.

I’m out on rural properties all week, half the time folks aren’t even home when I’m working their system. They’re trusting that their place, their tools, their fuel tank, all of it, isn’t gonna walk off. And I’ve had customers tell me straight up they’ve had equipment stolen, copper stripped, sheds busted into. That stuff isn’t some headline—it’s money out of their pocket and time they don’t get back. Same goes for me. I’ve had to start locking things down tighter than I ever used to, and even then you’re thinking about it at night.

What I hear from her crowd—Democrats, that whole camp—it sounds like they’re always bending over backward for the person doing the crime instead of the one dealing with it. Talking about cutting policing, softening things up, acting like enforcement is the problem. That dog won’t hunt where I live. You ease up on consequences, people notice real quick.

I’m not saying everything has to be heavy-handed or unfair, but there’s got to be a baseline: you break the law, there’s a consequence, and folks feel protected in their own place. I just don’t trust her side to hold that line. And when you don’t feel like someone’s gonna back basic public safety, that’s enough for me to vote the other way.

like_rep_candidate.reason_category: Views on international relations, war, defense, global leadership, or national security.
like_rep_candidate.text:
Yeah, I’ll tell you straight—on the world stage, I think Trump acts like somebody who understands that other countries are not your buddies, they’re competitors, sometimes outright adversaries. That matters to me more than all the speechmaking. I don’t need a president trying to win a popularity contest in Europe or get applause at some conference. I want somebody the other side has to think twice about before they start pushing.

From where I sit, the Democrats tend to come off like if we just say the right words and show we’re “reasonable,” everybody else will fall in line. That dog won’t hunt. Russia, China, Iran—they’re not sitting around waiting to be impressed by our tone. They’re watching for weakness, for hesitation, for places they can test us. And I think Trump, for all his rough edges, doesn’t give them that easy read. He’s unpredictable in a way that makes other leaders cautious. I’d rather have them guessing than getting comfortable.

Here’s the part people miss: peace isn’t just about being nice or even being right, it’s about leverage. It’s about whether the other guy thinks you’ll actually act if lines get crossed. When I’m on a job and a piece of equipment starts acting up, I don’t negotiate with it—I fix it or shut it down before it wrecks the whole system. Same idea. You need somebody who treats threats like real things, not talking points.

So yeah, if I’m checking a box for him, it’s because I think he projects strength in a way the other side doesn’t, and I think that keeps us out of worse trouble. You can call it blunt, you can call it ugly, but I’ll take a leader other countries are wary of over one they think they can play.

like_dem_party.reason_category: References to race, gender, religion, or symbolic representation of social groups.
like_dem_party.text:
Yeah, I’ll give them this, and I don’t mind saying it straight: they’re the party that actually makes a point of putting different kinds of people up front—Black folks, women, immigrants, people who don’t usually get handed the microphone in this country. That matters whether people like to admit it or not. You can call it optics if you want, but I’ve been on enough job sites to know who gets talked over and who doesn’t.

I’ve watched their conventions or speeches here and there, and it’s a different mix of faces and voices than what you see on the Republican side, no question. They lean into that, like “this is who we are,” and I can respect the clarity of it even if I don’t agree with them half the time. It sends a message about who they think belongs at the table.

Now, sometimes I think they overplay it or turn it into a checklist, and that’s where I start grinding my teeth a bit. But compared to the other side acting like everything’s already fair and settled, Democrats at least acknowledge that race and background still shape how people move through the world. That part, I don’t think they’re wrong about.

So yeah, if you’re asking what I *like* about them—it's that they don’t pretend everybody’s starting from the same place, and they actually elevate people who’ve been on the outside of things. Whether they always handle it right is another story, but they don’t ignore it.

dislike_dem_party.reason_category: Support or opposition based on party identity, ideological labels, or general alignment (e.g., liberal/conservative).
dislike_dem_party.text:
Yeah, I’ll be straight with you—I don’t like where that party sits, ideologically. They’ve drifted so far into that big-government, top-down way of thinking that it just rubs me wrong at a gut level. It feels like their default answer to anything is more control, more programs, more somebody somewhere telling you how you ought to run your own life, and that’s just not how I’m wired.

I’ve always been more of a “leave me some room to handle my own business” kind of person, and when I listen to Democratic folks talk, it sounds like they trust systems and institutions way more than they trust individuals. That’s the part that loses me. I don’t see myself in that crowd. I see a group that’s comfortable handing decisions upward, and I’ve spent my whole life trying not to be dependent on somebody else’s decisions.

So yeah, it’s not one little issue—it’s the overall mindset. That whole left-leaning, government-first approach… that dog won’t hunt for me.

dislike_rep_party.reason_category: Mentions of the economy, jobs, inflation, wages, taxation, government spending, or overall financial management.
dislike_rep_party.text:
Yeah, I’ve got one, and it’s not small. For a party that talks like it understands money, they sure as hell don’t act like it when they’re the ones holding the pen.

Here’s what bugs me: Republicans sell this idea that they’re the adults in the room on spending, debt, all that. That’s the pitch, right? Keep government lean, don’t saddle people with nonsense, let folks like me actually keep what we earn. But then you watch what they do when they’re in charge, and it turns into the same story with a different accent—spending goes up, deficits go up, and somehow we’re all supposed to pretend that math doesn’t count this time because it’s “our side.”

I remember when I got laid off, it wasn’t just income—it was health insurance, routine, all of it gone at once. That gap is where people get buried. So when Democrats push for funding those bridges—keeping people afloat while they get back on their feet—that tracks with reality to me.

So when Democrats sound like “just be more open” without matching it with “and here’s exactly how we control, document, and process this cleanly,” it loses me. I can work with reality, but I need a system that looks like a system, not a suggestion.

Here’s what I can tell you, because I’ve had to live it: the past few years, everything in my budget feels like it crept up at the same time—groceries, utilities, even basic stuff like paper towels. I sit at my kitchen counter with my bills and it’s like, okay, what gives this month? And nothing really “gives,” it just stacks. I’m working at the clinic, picking up remote shifts when I can, renting out two rooms in my own place, and I’m still doing that mental math constantly.

What makes me lean toward him is the focus on bringing prices down and pushing for a stronger economy in a way that, at least how he says it, is supposed to hit everyday costs—energy, fuel, that trickle-down stuff that affects everything else. Because when gas jumps, my grocery bill jumps, everything follows. And I know people argue about whether it actually plays out that way, I’m not an economist, but I know what it feels like when things are tighter versus when they’re not.

So it’s not about speeches for me. It’s that I want to feel like I’m not one surprise car repair away from scrambling. If someone sounds like they’re prioritizing getting costs under control and making it easier to stay ahead instead of constantly catching up, that gets my attention.

I’m not trying to be dramatic, I’m trying to be accurate. If you’re asking people to trust the system when you win, you kind of have to respect it when you lose, or at least play it out in a way that feels grounded in evidence, not just volume. From where I sit, it started to feel like the rules only count if they go your way, and that makes everything shakier than it already is.

And look, I’m not someone who trusts the federal government easily—I listen to plenty of people who question it—but there’s a difference between healthy skepticism and just telling people the whole thing is broken beyond repair. I see what happens at the clinic when people don’t trust basic systems—appointments, insurance, records—and everything turns into chaos real fast. Scale that up to elections, and it’s not a small thing.

So yeah, that’s the piece that would make me hesitate or even vote against him. Not policy stuff—this is more about whether the basic structure holds if people don’t like the outcome.

And in my head I’m like… okay, but I’m already juggling bills, insurance nonsense, and paperwork just to live a normal life. I don’t hear “help,” I hear “more layers.”

Here’s what I can tell you, because I’ve had to live it: even something as simple as dealing with insurance at the clinic can turn into a whole back-and-forth because of rules and approvals. And that’s with systems we already have. So when I hear Democrats pushing for bigger, more centralized solutions, my reaction is, “How is that not just going to multiply that headache?”

I deal with people all day at the clinic—people in pain, people stressed about bills—and if I talked to them the way some politicians talk, I’d get complaints by lunch.

I remember during one of the election cycles—I think it was around 2020—I had it on while I was doing dishes, and the host was just tearing into people, not even disagreeing, just mocking. So yeah, that’s my thing. I don’t need perfect manners, but I do think basic decency matters. If the message is solid, you shouldn’t have to deliver it like you’re trying to humiliate someone.

Now, I don’t agree with them on how they package it half the time—gets preachy, gets one-size-fits-all—but at least they’re starting from the fact that land and water have limits. The other side, too often, turns it into a culture fight or acts like paying attention to environmental change is some kind of weakness. I’ve got no patience for that. You can’t manage a watershed if your first move is to deny what’s happening in it.

dislike_dem_candidate.reason_category: References to healthcare, Medicare/Medicaid, prescription costs, social safety nets, or aid to disadvantaged groups.
dislike_dem_candidate.text:
Yeah… I mean, I’ll just say it straight, it’s the way that whole side of the aisle handles healthcare money. That’s the thing that sticks in my craw.

Because look, I’m not sitting here against helping people. I needed that system when my wife got pneumonia, and I’m telling you, that hospital kept her breathing. I’m grateful for that part. But the way Democrats—her included—talk about expanding all these programs, more coverage, more this, more that… it always sounds nice until the bill shows up somewhere down the line, and it lands on folks like me who are already hanging on.

Alright, here’s what happened, and I’m not exaggerating. We got hit with those hospital bills, and I’m on the phone trying to make sense of it, and half of it might as well have been written in another language. Then you hear politicians say, “we’re gonna make it better, we’re gonna expand access,” and I’m thinking—okay, but who’s actually straightening out the mess? Or are we just pouring more money into the same broken setup and calling it compassion?

To me, their side treats it like bigger is automatically better. More programs, more spending, more layers. And I’m over here doing porch-math, trying to figure out if I can pay the mortgage and keep the lights on after one bad illness. I don’t trust that crowd to fix costs—they just seem to grow the system and tell you it’s helping.

So yeah, that’s the piece that would push me against her. Not the idea of care—I believe in that—but the way her side goes about it. Feels like you end up more dependent and still just as squeezed.

like_dem_party.reason_category: References to healthcare, Medicare/Medicaid, prescription costs, social safety nets, or aid to disadvantaged groups.
like_dem_party.text:
Yeah—look, I’ll just say it plain: at least the Democrats act like people getting sick or broke actually matters. That’s the part I can’t ignore.

I’ve lived the other side of that, alright? My wife in that hospital, bills stacking up like somebody’s playing a joke on you, and you’re sitting there thinking, “So what, we just lose the house now?” And I’m supposed to hear folks say, what, “figure it out yourself”? Nah. That don’t sit right with me. Whatever else you want to argue about them, Democrats are the only ones consistently talking like regular people shouldn’t get wiped out over a hospital stay or the price of pills.

I’m not saying they’ve got it all neat and clean—half the time it still turns into a mess—but at least they’re pushing in the direction of helping with costs, keeping Medicare solid, not yanking the rug out from under people who are already hanging on. That matters more to me than all the noise.

Because I’ll tell you straight: when you’ve had to refinance your own home just to pay medical bills, you stop caring about slogans real quick. You start caring about who’s even trying to keep folks like you from going under. And in my experience, that’s been them more than the other side, no question.

dislike_rep_party.reason_category: Support or opposition based on party identity, ideological labels, or general alignment (e.g., liberal/conservative).
dislike_rep_party.text:
Yeah… I mean, I’ll just say it plain—I don’t feel like I fit with them people, not really. It’s that whole hard-line conservative identity thing, like you gotta check every box or you’re out. I’ve been around enough folks waving that banner, and it starts to feel like if you don’t talk the same, vote the same, think the same on everything, you’re suspect. I don’t like that.

I’m more of a leave-people-alone type, and a lot of what I hear out of Republicans these days, it’s got this edge to it—like they’ve decided who’s “good” and who ain’t, and I don’t have the patience for that kind of sorting. Feels like a club where you gotta prove you belong, and I’m not interested in joining anybody’s club like that.

I guess what bugs me is the whole identity of it—too rigid, too “this is who we are and that’s that.” Life ain’t that neat, not where I’m sitting. So yeah… I keep my distance from that label more than anything.

dislike_dem_candidate.reason_category: Concerns about democratic norms, elections, corruption, legal issues, or threats to democracy.
dislike_dem_candidate.text:
Yeah… for me it comes down to trust around elections and how power gets handled. I’ve watched some of the stuff she’s said over the years about voting laws and court fights, and it feels like there’s always a little wiggle room in there about what’s fair versus what helps your side win. That bothers me.

Look, in my world, if I send an invoice or sign a contract, it’s got to be clean. No games, no “well technically.” Same thing with elections. I don’t need perfect, I need straight. And when I hear politicians—her included—talk in a way that sounds like they’re okay bending the process if it gets them the result, I check out. That’s a hard line for me.

like_rep_candidate.reason_category: Positions on abortion, Roe v. Wade, reproductive freedom, or pro-life/pro-choice issues.
like_rep_candidate.text:
Yeah—if I’m being straight, it’s the abortion issue. That’s the one that sticks with me more than anything else with him.

I’m not trying to get into a whole speech about it, but for me it’s pretty simple: I believe life starts early, and I don’t see it as something you just treat like a scheduling decision. That’s shaped by my faith, yeah, but also just how I look at responsibility. You don’t get to undo certain things because they’re inconvenient. That’s how I see it.

When Roe got overturned, I remember thinking—finally, somebody actually moved the needle instead of just talking about it every election cycle. I’ve heard politicians say the same lines for years and nothing changes. That, at least, was action. You can argue about how it was done, sure, but it wasn’t just talk.

And look, I’m not blind—there are hard cases, and I’m not sitting here pretending every situation is clean and simple. Life’s messy. But at a baseline, I lean toward protecting that life, not expanding ways to end it. That’s just where I land.

So yeah, if you’re asking what would push me toward him specifically, it’s that. Everything else, I’ve got mixed feelings. But on that one, it’s pretty clear to me.

I’m not saying they get it right—half the time I look at it and go, “that’s a paper answer, who’s actually making sure it works?”—but the instinct behind it, that government should be more active in people’s lives, that’s their lane. That’s the piece I’d point to.

dislike_dem_party.reason_category: Mentions of climate change, environmental policy, energy production, or sustainability.
dislike_dem_party.text:
Yeah—honestly, the way they handle energy and climate stuff drives me nuts.

It always feels like it’s written by people who’ve never been on a roof in August with units tripping out because the grid’s strained and everything’s running flat out. They’ll push these big transitions or restrictions like it’s just flipping a switch, but down here, power and cooling isn’t theoretical—it’s survival. I’ve had days where we’re trying to keep a small office or a clinic up and running, and you start stacking rules or squeezing supply, and it shows up real fast in cost, delays, or equipment availability.

I’m not saying ignore the environment—I’ve put in higher-efficiency systems for years, that’s just good work. But the way it comes across sometimes… it’s a paper answer. Like, okay, sounds nice—does it keep the air on when it’s 100 degrees and the whole city’s pulling power? That’s where I think they lose me.

I’ve had a couple customers over the years—commercial guys, one was a vet—came back not right, couldn’t keep steady work. You hear their stories, and then you watch politicians talk like it’s just moving pieces on a board. That doesn’t sit right with me. I’m not saying don’t defend the country—of course you do—but if you’re gonna send people, have a real plan for how it ends and who’s accountable when it drags.

Otherwise it feels like a paper answer, you know? Big talk, not enough follow-through.

From where I sit as a small business owner, when the system is loose or selective, it creates weird incentives. You’ve got people doing things the right way—paperwork, licensing, insurance—and then others operating half in the shadows, cutting prices because they’re not carrying the same costs. That shows up in trades more than people want to admit. I’m not even talking politically, I’m talking about bids I lose to guys who can undercut because they’re not playing by the same rules. That’s not a level field.

So when Republicans talk about tighter borders and stricter enforcement, what I hear is: define the rules clearly, apply them evenly, and don’t just shrug when they’re ignored. If you want people coming in—and we obviously need labor in certain areas—then set a system that’s actually usable and enforced. Don’t make it this gray zone where everyone kind of knows what’s happening but nobody says it out loud.

I’m not anti-immigrant—half the crews I’ve worked with over the years are immigrants—but I am anti-BS. If there’s a line, it should be a real line. If there’s a process, it should work. Right now it feels like neither is true, and that spills into business in a very real way.

They act like it’s optional. Like people aren’t one bad diagnosis away from getting wrecked. I’ve seen what that looks like up close—bills, meds, follow-ups—it doesn’t care how hard you work. Feels like they don’t really give a damn about that part.

like_dem_candidate.reason_category: References to crime, policing, public safety, gun rights or gun control.
like_dem_candidate.text:
If I’m being honest, the one lane where I’d even pause and look twice at her is around crime and how she approaches policing, just because of her background as a prosecutor. That at least signals to me she’s had to deal with real-world consequences, not just talk about it in slogans. I’m not someone who thinks everything is falling apart, but I do think safety is one of those basic promises—if you call for help, someone competent shows up, and there’s follow-through.

Running an online resale business, I deal with a lighter version of that all the time—fraudulent buyers, chargebacks, people testing boundaries. It trains you real quick that rules only matter if somebody actually enforces them. I had a situation last year where a buyer claimed an item arrived broken, sent back a completely different piece, and the platform just auto-refunded them. No real investigation, just “policy.” That kind of thing makes you pay attention to who believes in accountability versus who just smooths things over.

So with her, the potential “for” would be: does she actually believe in enforcing the law in a consistent, not-selective way? Not performative crackdowns, not tying officers’ hands to the point they stop doing their job, but also not looking the other way when things go wrong. I’m not arguing, I’m just reading the fine print out loud—that’s what I’d be listening for. If I felt like she was serious about steady, boring, reliable public safety—not ideological swings—I could respect that enough to consider it.

dislike_dem_candidate.reason_category: References to healthcare, Medicare/Medicaid, prescription costs, social safety nets, or aid to disadvantaged groups.
dislike_dem_candidate.text:
Yeah—if I had to name one thing, it’s how she talks about expanding federal healthcare programs without being very clear on what that does on the ground, especially for costs and access.

I’m not against help. I’ve used telehealth a ton since 2020, and I’ve had months where getting a quick, affordable prescription without sitting in a waiting room was the difference between handling something early or letting it drag. So I’m not coming at this like, “everybody’s on their own.” But when I hear broad promises about expanding Medicare or increasing subsidies, my first thought is: okay, who’s actually paying, and what happens to pricing behavior once that money is flowing?

I’ve watched this in a smaller way with insurance and prescriptions. A basic medication that should be straightforward suddenly has three different prices depending on how it’s billed, and nobody can explain it cleanly. You get on a call and it’s like talking to a script. That’s not a theory—that’s me sitting at my kitchen counter with my laptop open, trying to figure out why one refill is $18 and the next quote is $64 for the same thing.

So when she leans into expanding those systems, I don’t hear enough about fixing that underlying mess—pricing transparency, incentives, who’s accountable when costs creep. It sounds like more money into a system that already doesn’t behave honestly. And I’m not arguing, I’m just reading the fine print out loud: if you don’t tighten how that system works, you can end up with higher costs dressed up as “coverage,” and regular people are still stuck doing detective work just to understand their own bills.

That’s the piece that would make me hesitate and probably lean against her—not the idea of helping people, but the way it’s structured and what it actually turns into once it’s real.

like_rep_candidate.reason_category: Evaluations of a candidate’s qualifications, preparedness, track record, or ability to govern.
like_rep_candidate.text:
If I’m being straight, the thing that would push me toward him is that he’s already done the job once, so you’re not guessing how he governs—you’ve got a record to look at.

I’m a “read the fine print” person by habit, and with most candidates it’s all promises and vibes. With him, I can actually go back and look at what decisions he made, how he handled pressure, what changed and what didn’t. You can argue about whether you liked those outcomes, sure, but it’s not theoretical. That matters to me more than a polished campaign plan because plans are easy to say and hard to execute.

I remember during his term, I’d follow along not just through headlines but by watching what actually filtered down to small businesses like mine—shipping costs, platform rules, just the general business climate. My experience is small-scale, but it’s real, and it teaches you fast whether leadership decisions have ripple effects or not.

So for me it comes down to: I’ve seen him in the role already, under real conditions, not just on a debate stage. That makes it easier to judge competence in a concrete way instead of trying to read intentions off a script. I’m open to being wrong, I just trust a track record more than a pitch.

like_dem_party.reason_category: Non-specific praise or criticism (e.g., 'good,' 'bad,' 'for the people') without clear issue or trait content.
like_dem_party.text:
Yeah, I can give you one thing, and it’s pretty simple: I like that they at least *sound like* they’re trying to look out for regular people.

Now, I’m not saying they always get it right—half the time I’m sitting there thinking, “okay, but who actually thought this through?”—but the tone matters to me more than people admit. When I listen to Democrats talk, I hear a kind of baseline message of, “we’re supposed to make things a little easier, a little fairer, not just tell people to tough it out.” And I don’t hate that. Life already has enough friction built in.

I think that lands with me because of my own path. When I had to leave university, it wasn’t because I didn’t care or didn’t try. It was money, plain and simple. So when I hear someone at least acknowledge that pressure exists—that people can get squeezed even when they’re doing what they’re supposed to—I notice that. It feels closer to reality than pretending everybody’s starting from the same spot.

Now, do I trust them to execute that well every time? No. I’m not that easy. But if you’re asking what I *like*, it’s that general posture of trying to be “for people,” even if sometimes it comes out more like a slogan than a plan.

like_rep_party.reason_category: Non-specific praise or criticism (e.g., 'good,' 'bad,' 'for the people') without clear issue or trait content.
like_rep_party.text:
Yeah—if I had to put a finger on it, what I tend to like is they *feel more straightforward to me*. Not perfect, not always right, but more like what you see is what you get.

And I know that’s a little squishy, but I’m basing it on how it comes across in real life, not a party brochure. The tone is usually less polished, less “focus-grouped,” and I have an easier time figuring out where someone actually stands without decoding ten layers of messaging. In my world—running a business, dealing with contracts, shipping issues, all that—I have a pretty low tolerance for anything that feels overly packaged or slippery.

It reminds me of dealing with buyers online, honestly. I’d rather someone just say, “Hey, this arrived damaged, what can we do?” than send a long, carefully worded message that’s trying to steer me into a refund without saying it outright. One feels direct, the other feels like I’m being managed.

So yeah, it’s that sense of plainness, I guess. Even when I don’t fully agree, I can at least track it without wondering what’s being hidden in the fine print. And you know me—I’m not arguing, I’m just reading the fine print out loud.

dislike_rep_party.reason_category: Positions on abortion, Roe v. Wade, reproductive freedom, or pro-life/pro-choice issues.
dislike_rep_party.text:
Yeah—this is the one place I tend to part ways with them, and it’s not abstract for me, it’s pretty practical. It’s how hard-line they’ve gotten on abortion, especially after Roe got overturned.

I’m a churchgoing person, so I’m not casual about it. I do believe it’s serious. But the way some Republican laws are written now, it feels like they’ve taken a complicated, real-life situation and flattened it into a slogan. And real life is messier than that. I’ve known women—not close family, but close enough through church and work—who were dealing with pregnancies that were not straightforward. Health issues, timing, situations where you’re sitting there thinking, “Okay, what actually keeps this person safe?” And the laws don’t leave much room for that kind of judgment.

What bothers me is when it turns into “no exceptions, no discussion, and we’ll sort out the consequences later.” That’s a lot of confidence for not a lot of evidence about how those situations actually play out on the ground. Doctors get nervous, women get stuck waiting, and suddenly it’s not about values in the abstract, it’s about who’s allowed to make a call in a moment that can go sideways fast.

I’m not asking for anything extreme. I’m asking for some acknowledgment that these decisions sometimes need to stay between a woman, her doctor, and her conscience—maybe her pastor too, depending on the person. When the party line sounds like it doesn’t trust women to handle that at all, that’s where I step back. It just doesn’t match what I’ve seen in real life.

dislike_dem_candidate.reason_category: Evaluations of a candidate’s qualifications, preparedness, track record, or ability to govern.
dislike_dem_candidate.text:
Yeah. For me it comes down to whether someone looks like they can actually run the machine, not just talk about it.

When I watch her in interviews or hear her explain policy, I don’t hear a clear plan with steps, tradeoffs, and what it costs. It feels like broad points without the “how this gets done Monday morning” piece. In my world, if a property manager asks how you’re going to keep a system online, you better be able to walk it: what equipment, what schedule, what it runs, what happens if it fails. I haven’t seen that level of operational detail from her, and that makes me uneasy about putting her in charge of something as big as the federal government.

Now, I don’t agree with how far they take it half the time, and I don’t like the top-down way it gets pushed. But at the core, that idea that the working guy shouldn’t get run over just because he doesn’t have leverage—I can respect that, even if I land on the Republican side most days.

I’ve watched too many people lately act like elections only count if they win. That doesn’t sit right with me. You start treating the whole system like it’s optional, like rules are just suggestions, you’re asking for trouble down the line. I grew up with the idea that you don’t like the result, you come back next round and do better—you don’t try to bend the scoreboard after the game.

So with her, what I’d be voting for is keeping the basic machinery intact. Courts, elections, the idea that when it’s settled, it’s settled. I don’t need a politician I agree with on everything—I need one who’s not going to play games with whether the whole thing even works.

dislike_dem_candidate.text:
Yeah—straight up, it’s the political lane she’s in. I’m not a party guy, but I’ve been around long enough to know what comes bundled with that label. When I hear her talk, it lines up with a more liberal, top-down way of running things, and that’s where I start pulling back.

I run a business where every extra rule, fee, or “initiative” lands on me first and gets passed to my customers next. So when someone is tied to a political approach that leans heavier on government direction, more programs, more oversight—I don’t hear “help,” I hear “here comes another layer I’ve gotta work around.” That’s just how it’s played out in my world.

So it’s not about her personality or anything like that. It’s that she represents a side of the aisle that, in my experience, tends to make my day-to-day harder, not easier. And I vote based on what keeps my operation clean and predictable.

like_rep_party.reason_category: References to crime, policing, public safety, gun rights or gun control.
like_rep_party.text:
Yeah—if I strip everything else away, the thing I consistently like is they take crime and day-to-day safety seriously, at least in how they talk about it and what they push for.

I’m in and out of people’s homes all over the Detroit area, every kind of neighborhood, all hours. I’ve had vans broken into, had tools walk off a job, had customers nervous to even open the door after dark. That stuff isn’t theoretical to me—it’s money, time, and honestly just whether people feel okay in their own house. When someone says “we’re going to back the police, we’re going to prosecute, we’re not just going to shrug at theft,” that lines up with what I deal with.

And it’s not about being hard for the sake of it. It’s consistency. If a guy steals copper out of a basement or strips a job site, there should be a predictable outcome. Right now it can feel like a coin toss—one place cracks down, another place acts like it’s minor. That uncertainty bleeds into everything: insurance, pricing, even how I schedule jobs. I’ve had to think about which streets I’ll park on and how fast I can get in and out, which is ridiculous when you’re just trying to fix someone’s heat.

So yeah, that’s the piece I connect with. Keep it basic: protect people, protect property, and don’t make it optional depending on the week. You want communities stable? Start there.

like_rep_candidate.reason_category: References to crime, policing, public safety, gun rights or gun control.
like_rep_candidate.text:
I’ll be honest, the main thing that would push me toward voting for Trump is how he talks about law and order and actually backing the police. That’s not abstract to me. I live in a regular suburban area where people are just trying to keep their homes stable, and when crime creeps up—even a little—it changes how you live. You start double-checking locks, thinking about when you go out, watching who’s around your street. I’ve had neighbors deal with car break-ins, and one of my tenants had their catalytic converter stolen a couple years ago. It’s not dramatic headline stuff, but it wears on you.

What I hear from Republicans, and especially from Trump, is a clearer line: you enforce the rules, you support the people who are actually out there dealing with crime, and you don’t make excuses for it. The other side—I’m sorry, but a lot of Democrats sound like they’re more worried about explaining the criminal than protecting the person who’s just trying to live their life. That doesn’t sit right with me. I’m not doing chaos today, and I don’t think most people are.

And I’ll add this—when leadership sounds uncertain or starts hedging around basic safety, it filters down. People notice. If you give the impression that rules are optional, you get more of that behavior. Trump, whatever else you think about him, doesn’t hedge on that. It’s very much “this is where we stand, and we’re backing it.” For me, that clarity matters more than polished language or trying to sound balanced. I want to feel like someone’s actually on the side of people who follow the rules.

dislike_rep_candidate.reason_category: Comments about temperament, honesty, likability, morality, or personal behavior.
dislike_rep_candidate.text:
Yeah… there is, and it’s not a small thing for me. It’s his behavior, the way he carries himself. I don’t expect a politician to be a saint, I’m realistic about that, but I do expect a basic level of steadiness and straight dealing, especially from someone representing our side. With him, it often feels like you’re getting a different version of the story depending on the day, and that wears on me.

I spent years in billing where if something doesn’t line up, you fix it or you own it. You don’t just talk over it or spin it and hope people move on. And with Trump, I’ve had too many moments where I’m thinking, “That’s a headline, not an answer,” or “that doesn’t quite add up.” It makes it harder to trust what’s coming out next, even if I agree with the general direction politically.

And the tone—just the constant friction, the name-calling, the drama—I’m not doing chaos today, you know? I’ve got enough real-world things to manage without feeling like the person on our side is stirring the pot on purpose. I want someone who can represent conservative instincts without making it feel like a reality show every week.

So yeah, for me it comes down to trust and temperament. If I feel like I have to constantly filter what someone says or brace for the next blow-up, that’s when I start thinking about voting against them, even if they’re technically on “my team.”

like_dem_party.reason_category: Views on immigration levels, border security, undocumented immigrants, or asylum policies.
like_dem_party.text:
I’ll be honest, I don’t line up with Democrats on most things, but if I’m sticking to one area where I at least understand what they’re trying to do, it’s immigration—specifically the part about not treating every single person coming here like they’re automatically a problem.

Now, I don’t mean open doors, no rules—because that’s where I part ways pretty fast. You need a border, you need a process, you need to know who’s coming in. I’m not doing chaos today, and that includes national policy. But I do think Democrats, as a group, tend to come at it from a more human angle, like these are people first, not just numbers or threats. And I can respect that instinct, even if I think they sometimes take it too far or don’t think through the follow-through.

In my world—medical billing, front desks, paperwork—you see real-life versions of this. You’ve got patients who don’t have perfect documentation, English isn’t their first language, and if you just shut the door on them, it doesn’t make the problem disappear, it just pushes it somewhere messier and more expensive later. So I get why Democrats lean toward “let’s figure out a way to handle people already here,” instead of pretending it’s all clean and simple.

Where I get off the train is when it feels like they’re more focused on signaling compassion than actually enforcing a workable system. Because if you don’t have clear rules and consequences, the people who do follow the rules end up carrying the load—and I’ve seen that pattern too many times to ignore it.

So, short version: I can respect that Democrats, as a side, emphasize the human side of immigration. I just think they lose the plot when it comes to structure and limits.

dislike_dem_party.reason_category: Concerns about democratic norms, elections, corruption, legal issues, or threats to democracy.
dislike_dem_party.text:
I’ll be straight with you, the biggest thing that bothers me about the Democratic Party is how loose they seem with the rules when it suits them, especially around elections and process. I’m not talking about slogans—I mean the nuts and bolts stuff. From where I sit, if you want people to trust the outcome, you keep the procedures tight, consistent, and transparent. Instead, it often feels like they’re comfortable changing rules midstream or brushing off concerns as if regular people are too dumb to notice. That’s a problem.

In my world—billing, claims, documentation—if you start bending process because it’s “easier” or “for a good reason,” you open the door to mistakes at best and abuse at worst. Same principle. And when people ask questions about elections or oversight, the response from that side too often comes off as dismissive, like “just trust us.” That’s a headline, not an answer. Trust comes from showing your work, not from telling people they’re wrong to ask.

I’m not saying every Democrat is doing something shady. I’m saying the party, as a whole, doesn’t seem as committed as I’d like to keeping the system tight and above suspicion. And once people lose confidence in that, it’s very hard to get back. Show me the numbers, show me the safeguards, show me the consistency—then we can talk.

like_rep_party.reason_category: Mentions of the economy, jobs, inflation, wages, taxation, government spending, or overall financial management.
like_rep_party.text:
I don’t usually wear a jersey, but if I’m being honest, what I *like* about Republicans is they at least talk like money is real and not just something you can keep stretching. That matters to me. I’ve spent my whole adult life making sure numbers line up—at work with claims, at home with a mortgage and bills—and I can tell you, if you run things sloppy, it catches up. Every time.

From my side of the fence, Republicans sound more grounded on that. Lower taxes, tighter spending, not acting like there’s an endless pot somewhere—that just lines up with how I’ve had to live. I mean, after my divorce, there was no safety net fairy that showed up. It was “cut costs, track everything, rebuild.” So when I hear Democrats talk, a lot of it sounds like wishful math to me. That’s a headline, not an answer.

I’m not saying Republicans get it perfect—nobody does—but I feel like they’re at least speaking the language of budgets, consequences, and limits. And to me, that’s just basic adulthood. You can’t run a household, a clinic, or a country pretending the numbers don’t matter. Show me the numbers, then we’ll talk—that’s where I land, and that’s why I lean their way.

like_dem_party.reason_category: References to healthcare, Medicare/Medicaid, prescription costs, social safety nets, or aid to disadvantaged groups.
like_dem_party.text:
Yeah—if I had to pick one thing, it’s how they handle the basic safety net around healthcare.

I’m not someone who loves dealing with the medical system—I avoid it more than I should—but I’ve had just enough real moments, like when I had pneumonia a few years back, to see how quickly costs and logistics can get out of hand. What I like is the push, at least in principle, to keep things like Medicare, Medicaid, and prescription costs from becoming completely unmanageable for people. Not in a theoretical way—I mean making sure there’s a floor so one bad health event doesn’t just wipe someone out.

I don’t think they execute it cleanly all the time—the system is still a maze—but the intent to make healthcare at least somewhat accessible and bounded, I think that matters. That’s probably the clearest place where I align.

dislike_dem_party.reason_category: Mentions of the economy, jobs, inflation, wages, taxation, government spending, or overall financial management.
dislike_dem_party.text:
Yeah—if I had to pick one thing, it’s how loose they can be around spending and the downstream impact on costs.

I’m not allergic to government spending in principle, but I’ve sat in enough budget and operations meetings to know that if you don’t tie spending to clear outcomes and accountability, it drifts. And when it drifts, you feel it as inflation, higher taxes, or just less discipline overall. That shows up in real life—housing costs, services, everything creeping up—and I don’t always hear Democrats talk about the tradeoffs in a grounded way.

The short version is: I want the same level of financial rigor from government that I expect inside a business unit—what are we solving for, how are we measuring it, and when do we stop if it’s not working. I don’t consistently see that.

Yeah. For me it’s pretty simple — they at least try to make sure people can actually get medical care without it wrecking their finances.

I’ve got a coworker who’s on insulin, and before things got adjusted a few years back, he was stretching doses and doing math every month like it was a utility bill he might not be able to pay. That’s not a “lifestyle choice,” that’s life or death. Democrats are the only ones I see consistently pushing to cap that kind of cost and keep programs like Medicare and Medicaid solid. The other side talks a lot about the market, but the market doesn’t keep you alive if you can’t afford the prescription.

I’m pro-choice, I vote that way, and I don’t trust Republicans on it at all—they’ve shown what they’ll do. Yeah—if you want my honest answer, it’s the way Republicans talk about the border and keeping some order to it. I don’t mean chest-thumping, I mean the basic idea that you can’t just run a system with no clear entry points and expect it to hold.

We had a situation a while back where a small system near us got overwhelmed—not immigration, just seasonal population swings—and you could watch the pressure drop because nobody planned for the load. That’s what it reminds me of. If you don’t know who’s coming in and at what pace, the system strains and the people already on it feel it first.

I’m still a Democrat, no question. But on that piece, Republicans at least sound like they’re talking about the load on the system, not pretending it’ll sort itself out.

like_dem_candidate.reason_category: Positions on abortion, Roe v. Wade, reproductive freedom, or pro-life/pro-choice issues.
like_dem_candidate.text:
Yeah—this one’s pretty straightforward for me. It’s her stance on abortion, specifically keeping it legal and not turning it into some patchwork where your zip code decides what kind of care you can get.

Look, I’m not out here treating it like a casual decision. It’s serious. But I’ve also been around enough real-life situations—family, people at church, people who don’t advertise their business—to know it’s not clean or theoretical. Complications, timing, finances, stuff people don’t want to say out loud. When that hits, the last thing you need is the government stepping in like it understands your situation better than you or your doctor.

The way I see it, this is a medical decision that should stay between a woman, her family, and a doctor who actually knows what’s going on. Same way I think about my primary care doctor—someone who knows your history, who you can call, who’s accountable. You start putting blanket bans or heavy restrictions on top of that, you’re basically saying, “We don’t trust you or your doctor, we’re going to decide from a distance.” That doesn’t sit right with me.

And the state-by-state thing? That’s where it really breaks down. I work in logistics—I think in systems. If the rule changes every time you cross a border, people fall through cracks. Now you’ve got people driving hours, scrambling, delaying care. That’s how problems get worse, not better.

So yeah, if I’m naming one thing that would push me toward her, it’s that she’s clearly on the side of keeping that decision legal and accessible, instead of tightening it down and pretending that solves anything.

like_rep_candidate.reason_category: Mentions of the economy, jobs, inflation, wages, taxation, government spending, or overall financial management.
like_rep_candidate.text:
Yeah. For me it’s the cost side of life, straight up.

When he was in, I remember fuel, groceries, basic stuff—you could plan a month without feeling like every line item was creeping up on you. Now it’s like every time I run the numbers—gas for the commute, food for a family, utilities—something’s higher, and it doesn’t let up. In my world, that matters more than speeches. If I can’t predict expenses, everything else gets shaky.

What I’d give him credit for is pushing hard on keeping business costs down—taxes, regulations, that whole environment—because that flows through to jobs and prices whether people like it or not. I see it on the logistics side: when operating costs go up, it doesn’t stay in a spreadsheet, it hits shipping rates, then shelves, then my grocery bill.

I’m not doing a whole personality debate. I’m looking at, can I keep my household steady without playing defense every month? If I think one guy’s policies make that easier—lower pressure on costs, more predictable—that’s the lane I’m in.

dislike_rep_candidate.reason_category: Non-specific praise or criticism (e.g., 'good,' 'bad,' 'for the people') without clear issue or trait content.
dislike_rep_candidate.text:
Yeah—his overall way of carrying himself. It just feels… off to me. Not steady, not disciplined, more like everything turns into a show. And I don’t have patience for that.

I spend my whole day making sure things run clean—people know their role, communication is tight, no unnecessary drama. When the person at the top feels like the opposite of that, it bothers me. I don’t need perfect, I’m not expecting some saint, but I do expect a baseline of being put-together and not constantly stirring the pot.

It’s hard to trust someone with real responsibility when the vibe you get is chaos wrapped in confidence. That alone is enough to make me step back and say, yeah, I’m probably not going that direction.

dislike_dem_party.reason_category: Views on immigration levels, border security, undocumented immigrants, or asylum policies.
dislike_dem_party.text:
Yeah. The way they handle immigration and the border—honestly, it feels disconnected from how things actually work on the ground.

I’m not coming at this as some abstract policy debate. I deal with systems that break when you lose track of who’s responsible for what. Ports, warehouses, trucking—if you don’t know what’s coming in, who’s handling it, and where it’s going, everything backs up and people start cutting corners. So when I hear messaging that sounds like, “we’ll figure it out, be compassionate first,” I’m thinking, okay… but who’s tracking intake, who’s accountable for placement, what’s the limit before local systems get overloaded?

And the part that really bothers me is how loose it can sound around enforcement. You can’t just say rules matter in one area of life and then treat them like suggestions at the border. That doesn’t hold up. I’ve got kids, I’ve got my mother in my house—I don’t run my home like that, and I wouldn’t run a team like that either.

I’m not against immigration. My church is full of immigrants, my own family story is tied into that. But there’s a difference between a process that’s clear, enforced, and fair—and one that feels like it’s reacting after the fact. When it’s the second one, regular communities end up absorbing the strain, and nobody wants to admit where the breaking point is.

So yeah, that’s the main thing. It comes across like they don’t want to draw hard lines, and in any system I’ve ever worked in, if you don’t draw the lines, the system eventually draws them for you—and it’s usually messy.

like_rep_party.reason_category: Mentions of the economy, jobs, inflation, wages, taxation, government spending, or overall financial management.
like_rep_party.text:
Yeah—when they’re serious about spending and not just talking, I like that. I’m not interested in slogans about “growth,” I’m looking at whether someone is actually trying to keep costs from running away on regular families.

I’ll put it in real terms. In my house, if property taxes jump, insurance goes up, groceries creep up every month, that’s not abstract. That’s me reworking the budget at the kitchen table and deciding what gets pushed. Same at work—fuel costs spike, port fees shift, suddenly every shipment is tighter and people feel it down the line. So when Republicans focus on limiting government spending and not adding new layers of cost that eventually show up in taxes or inflation, that tracks for me. It’s the same mindset I use running operations: if you don’t control inputs, everything downstream gets sloppy and expensive.

I’m not saying they always execute it cleanly—sometimes they talk a big game and still spend—but the instinct to keep a lid on costs and not treat money like it’s theoretical, that’s the part I respect. Because in real life, somebody always pays for it, and it’s usually the families trying to keep things steady.

I’ll give you one real example—my daughter’s school had that deal last year where a biological boy was in girls’ locker room rotation, and parents were basically told “get over it.” That didn’t sit right with me. If she came out and said, plain as day, “we’re gonna draw some lines here, protect girls’ spaces, and stop pretending there aren’t differences,” and actually meant it—not lawyer talk, not activist talk—just common sense… that’d be the first time I’d even consider it.

But as it stands, that’s her team’s thing, not ours. And I’m not crossing over for more of that.

The whole “first this, first that”—woman, Black, whatever—gets pushed like it’s the main qualification, and that rubs me wrong. I don’t hire a tech because of what box he checks, I hire him because he can fix the machine without making it worse. When I hear her talk, it feels like our side is being told we’re supposed to vote based on identity, and if we don’t, we’re the problem. That’s not how I was raised. One thing that stuck with me—I remember her speech where she kept coming back to being the first woman of color in the role and telling people to see themselves in her; that’s fine as a footnote, but it sounded like the headline. I’m not voting for a symbol, I’m voting for someone to run the country, and that whole approach pushes me the other way.

like_rep_candidate.text:
Yeah. I’ll keep it simple—when things get sideways overseas, I want somebody in charge who other countries think twice about testing. Not somebody they think they can push around while we hold a meeting about it.

I remember when Iran was acting up a few years back and then that general—Soleimani—got taken out. After that, it got real quiet, real fast. That’s the kind of message I’m talking about. You don’t have to like the guy’s personality, but our side needs someone the rest of the world reads as, “don’t screw with them unless you’re ready for it.” Republicans get that. Democrats spend too much time trying to be liked. I don’t care about liked—I care about nobody messing with us or dragging us into something because they think we won’t hit back.

like_dem_party.reason_category: Views on international relations, war, defense, global leadership, or national security.
like_dem_party.text:
Yeah—when they’re at their best, I think Democrats are a little less eager to swing the hammer first overseas. I’ll give you that straight.

Look, I’ve had a kid from our area—worked summers doing irrigation installs—come back from a deployment not the same guy. That sticks with you. So when I hear Democrats pushing for slowing things down, working angles with other countries before we start lighting stuff up, I don’t hate that. War isn’t some cable news graphic, it’s people you know getting chewed up and families dealing with it after.

I’m not saying they always get it right—don’t twist that—but if you’re asking what I like, it’s that instinct to not treat every problem like it needs a military answer on day one. That’s it.

dislike_dem_party.reason_category: Mentions of the economy, jobs, inflation, wages, taxation, government spending, or overall financial management.
dislike_dem_party.text:
Yeah. Money. Straight up—how they handle money.

I don’t care what the speech sounds like, I watch what it costs me to live. Last couple years, everything that actually matters in my house—groceries, fuel, parts, even basic shop supplies—went up, and it didn’t creep, it jumped. We had a stretch where hydraulic fluid and filters we use all the time were up enough that I had to go back through jobs and double-check we weren’t eating the difference. That’s real, day-to-day stuff, not some talking point.

And when I hear Democrats talk, it always sounds like more spending, more programs, more “we’ll cover it,” like money just shows up. It doesn’t. It comes out of people like me, or it shows up as higher prices somewhere down the line. You can dress it up however you want, but I’m the one standing at the parts counter doing the math, and it’s not working in our favor.

like_rep_party.reason_category: Views on immigration levels, border security, undocumented immigrants, or asylum policies.
like_rep_party.text:
Yeah. Border and immigration, straight up. That’s the one.

Look, I’m not arguing, I’m diagnosing: a place either controls who comes in or it doesn’t. And I like that Republicans at least talk and act like it matters. Out here it’s not some abstract debate—we had a guy bring a combine in last fall, broke down mid-harvest, and he’s telling me he can’t find enough legal help he can count on, paperwork’s a mess, and meanwhile you’ve got people coming in sideways and nobody can tell me who’s responsible for what. That kind of chaos costs real money and time. Two weeks waiting on parts is bad enough—two weeks of not knowing your labor situation? That’ll bury you.

I’m fine with people coming here. Always have been. But do it straight, do it legal, and have a system that means something. Republicans, at least on this, are on the side of “lock it down, then sort it out,” and Democrats feel like “let it slide and we’ll figure it out later.” I don’t trust “later.” That’s it.

dislike_rep_party.reason_category: Concerns about democratic norms, elections, corruption, legal issues, or threats to democracy.
dislike_rep_party.text:
Yeah. I’ll tell you straight.

I don’t like when our side starts playing games with elections or acting loose with the rules just because we think the other side does it. That stuff matters. If people stop trusting the vote, you don’t have much left holding it together. I don’t care if it’s Democrats or Republicans doing it—if we look like we’re trying to bend outcomes or ignore results we don’t like, that’s a problem.

I remember 2020, sitting in the shop after hours, guys arguing about what was real and what wasn’t, and it got to the point where nobody trusted anything—courts, counts, nothing. That’s not strength, that’s rot. You can’t run a country like that. You lose, you figure out why, and you come back better. You don’t torch the whole system because you’re mad about the result.

I’ll be honest with you, the sticking point for me is how the border and immigration have been handled, and what I’ve heard from her on that. It feels too loose, and I don’t mean that in a slogan way, I mean in a day-to-day, practical sense of whether there are clear rules and whether they’re actually enforced.

Here’s what I’ve seen with my own eyes, or at least close enough to count. Through our church pantry, we’ve had more families come through the last few years who are in very uncertain situations—no paperwork settled, children not in stable schooling yet, parents afraid to sign up for anything official. And don’t misunderstand me, we feed them, we help them, we treat them with dignity. That’s the job. But it also shows me what happens when the system itself isn’t orderly. People end up in limbo, and local communities are the ones trying to patch it together.

What I don’t hear from her—at least not clearly—is a sense of structure. Who comes in, how, how long they stay, and what happens if they don’t qualify. In a classroom, if you don’t have clear expectations, the kindest kids and the most troubled kids both end up struggling. That’s not cruelty, that’s structure. And I apply that same thinking here.

So it’s not that I’m against immigration. Goodness, no—this country runs on it. But I want it to be orderly and fair, and I’m not convinced she sees the need for firmness the same way I do. And that hesitation is enough to make me pause, because once something gets out of hand at that scale, it’s very hard to tidy it up later.

I’ll be honest with you, the one thing that pulls me toward him—really the only thing strong enough to make me consider it—is abortion. I don’t talk about that lightly, and I don’t talk about it like a slogan. For me it’s tied up with what I believe happens before any of us ever get our act together in this world.

I’ve spent a lifetime around teenagers, including girls who were scared, pregnant, and trying to figure out what came next. I never once thought, “this is simple.” It isn’t. But I also never got comfortable with the idea that ending a pregnancy is just another decision you slot in alongside the rest. That always sat heavy with me, like a sentence that doesn’t quite read right no matter how many times you revise it.

In church, we don’t debate it in abstract terms. We bring meals, we help people through what comes after, we try—imperfectly—to stand with both the mother and the child. And I suppose over time that’s shaped me into believing the law ought to lean toward protecting that life, even when the situation is complicated.

So when I look at Trump, it’s not his personality—Lord knows I have my issues there—it’s that he moved things in a direction where abortion is more restricted. That matters to me. It feels, for lack of a better phrase, closer to what I can live with when I lay my head down at night.

Now, I know reasonable people see that very differently, and I’ve taught enough students to know there’s real pain on all sides of it. But if you’re asking for the specific reason that might push me his way, that’s it. Everything else, I can argue with myself about. That one, I don’t shake off so easily.

like_dem_party.reason_category: Mentions of climate change, environmental policy, energy production, or sustainability.

I’ll be honest with you, the thing I consistently appreciate about Democrats is that they treat environmental issues—climate, land use, clean energy—as something you plan for, not something you argue about forever. That matters to me more than people expect.

I remember a unit I taught years ago where we read essays about stewardship—just basic care for the world you’ve been given—and those kids, even the ones who didn’t care about grades, understood the idea that you don’t foul your own nest. Fast forward to now, and I watch these hotter summers, the storms getting stranger, and I think: this isn’t theoretical anymore. When Democrats talk about investing in cleaner energy or protecting water and land, it sounds like long-term thinking to me. Not perfect, not always tidy, but at least it’s forward-facing.

And I’ll tell you, at my church pantry during COVID, we had weeks where supply chains were off and certain foods just weren’t coming in like usual. It doesn’t take a scientist to see how fragile some of these systems are. So when a party says, “Let’s build something more stable and less dependent on burning through everything,” I hear a kind of practicality in that.

Now, read that back to yourself—because I’m not saying they get every detail right. But on that one issue, they are at least writing the full paragraph instead of pretending the topic isn’t assigned.

It’s not the compassion piece—I’m right there on that—but the label, the package of it. There’s a tone sometimes, like if you don’t line up neatly under “Democrat,” then you must be on the wrong side of things entirely, and that dog won’t hunt in my classroom or anywhere else.

I’ve always thought of myself as someone who agrees with a lot of liberal ideas because of what I saw teaching—kids who needed protection, fairness, a chance to be seen—but I never felt comfortable wrapping myself in the party label itself. It feels too… prescribed, I suppose. Like you’re signing onto a whole identity, not just a set of positions. And I’ve spent too many years teaching students to think for themselves to then turn around and say, “Well, I belong to this side, so I take all of it.”

So when Democrats present themselves as the home for “people like me,” I bristle a little. I don’t like being told what box I’m in. I’d rather choose piece by piece, even if that makes my ballot look inconsistent to someone else.

I’m not saying every single person in the party is the same, but as a whole, it just doesn’t feel like a place where my values fit.

like_dem_candidate.reason_category: References to race, gender, religion, or symbolic representation of social groups.
like_dem_candidate.text: I’ll be honest, the main thing that lands with me is seeing a woman of color in that position and not treating it like it’s unusual. So when I see someone like her up there, it signals—at least a little—that the door isn’t just cracked for one kind of person anymore. That matters to me in a practical way, not symbolic for show, but in who gets listened to and who gets taken seriously. It doesn’t decide everything for me, but it’s the piece that would pull me toward her.

dislike_dem_candidate.reason_category: Views on immigration levels, border security, undocumented immigrants, or asylum policies.
dislike_dem_candidate.text:
Yeah. It’s the border piece for me, plain and simple.

From where I sit, it feels like the rules aren’t steady or clear, and that makes everything harder on the ground. I spend my days dealing with systems that already bend under paperwork and capacity, and when immigration policy sounds like it’s shifting or not enforced consistently, I don’t trust how that plays out locally. It’s not about being against people coming here—I’ve worked with plenty of patients who are just trying to get by—but I think you have to have a process you can point to and say, “this is how it works,” and actually mean it.

Right now, when I listen to her talk about it, I don’t hear that kind of clarity or follow-through. And for me, if I can’t see how a policy holds up in real life, I’m not comfortable backing it.

like_rep_candidate.reason_category: Evaluations of a candidate’s qualifications, preparedness, track record, or ability to govern.
like_rep_candidate.text: If I’m sticking to one concrete thing, it’s that he’s already been in that job and I’ve seen how he actually governs, not just how he campaigns. I don’t have to guess what his decision-making looks like under pressure or how his administration runs day to day, because we’ve lived through a full term of it. For me, that matters more than promises — I’ve spent too many hours dealing with systems that sound good on paper and fall apart in practice. So having a known track record, even if I don’t agree with all of it, carries weight.

dislike_rep_candidate.reason_category: Mentions of the economy, jobs, inflation, wages, taxation, government spending, or overall financial management.
dislike_rep_candidate.text:
Yeah. For me it comes down to how his approach to tariffs and trade would hit prices where I live.

Out here, when costs jump, there’s no cushion. Gas is already a long drive expense, groceries come from farther out, and a lot of folks are on fixed or tight incomes. When you start talking broad tariffs, I hear “prices go up on the ground,” whether it’s parts, food, or anything that moves through a supply chain. I lived through 2008 in a bank—I don’t take chances on policies that feel like they might spike costs and then sort it out later. I need something steadier than that.

like_dem_party.reason_category: Support or opposition based on party identity, ideological labels, or general alignment (e.g., liberal/conservative).
like_dem_party.text:
Yeah—if I’m keeping it simple, I like that Democrats generally lean toward expanding public healthcare coverage. That whole “government should help make sure people can actually get care” idea lines up more with how I see things.  

I’m not saying they always get it right, but that basic stance—that access to care isn’t just a personal problem to solve on your own—that’s something I agree with more than the alternative.

dislike_dem_party.reason_category: Views on immigration levels, border security, undocumented immigrants, or asylum policies.
dislike_dem_party.text:
Yeah. The piece that doesn’t sit right with me is how loose and abstract the immigration talk can get, especially around the border. It’s a lot of “we should be humane” — and I agree with that — but not enough “here’s exactly how this works day to day, here’s who’s responsible, here’s the timeline.”

In my world, if you don’t have clear steps and clear limits, the system jams up and the people at the bottom deal with it. I’ve watched what happens when rules are unclear or keep shifting — you get backlogs, missed follow-ups, people stuck in limbo. And then the folks actually trying to follow the rules, or the communities trying to absorb new demand, are left guessing.

I’m not saying shut the door. I’m saying pick a process and run it clean. Who gets in, how long it takes, what happens if they don’t qualify — write it down, stick to it, and make it doable. Right now it feels like a long drive for a maybe, and I don’t have much patience for systems built like that.

like_rep_party.reason_category: References to crime, policing, public safety, gun rights or gun control.
like_rep_party.text:
Yeah—if I had to point to one thing, it’s that they tend to take public safety seriously, especially around the reality of being out here where you’re on your own more than not.

Where I live, if something goes sideways, you’re not getting a patrol car in two minutes. It might be twenty, it might be longer. So the idea that people can protect themselves, that law enforcement is backed up and not second-guessed into inaction—that matters to me in a very practical way. It’s not political theory, it’s “what happens if someone pulls into your driveway at night and it’s not good.”

I don’t need speeches about it. I just pay attention to who seems to understand that distance and response time aren’t the same everywhere, and Republicans, more often than not, talk about that in a way that lines up with real life out here.

dislike_rep_party.reason_category: Mentions of climate change, environmental policy, energy production, or sustainability.
dislike_rep_party.text:
Yeah. The part that sticks with me is how a lot of Republican leadership talks about energy and the environment like it’s all just an inconvenience to business, instead of something you have to live with long-term.

Out here, land isn’t abstract. It’s where your water comes from, it’s what your family’s been on for generations. When I hear them push hard for more drilling or rollbacks without much talk about cleanup, water safety, or what happens ten years down the line, it feels short-sighted. I’m not against using resources—we all rely on it—but I want to hear a plan that doesn’t leave the mess sitting with local people.

Right now it often sounds like, “we’ll deal with that later,” and from what I’ve seen, “later” usually means the folks on the ground are the ones dealing with it. That’s the part I don’t like.

like_dem_candidate.reason_category: Support or opposition based on party identity, ideological labels, or general alignment (e.g., liberal/conservative).
like_dem_candidate.text:
Mire, yo se lo digo sin tanta vuelta… para mí pesa el lado del partido. Yo ya tengo años inclinándome con los demócratas, no porque crea que son perfectos, sino porque del otro lado —los republicanos— muchas veces siento que no van con la gente como yo, de trabajo, de manos, que no tuvimos palancas ni estudios largos.

Entonces cuando veo a Kamala Harris, la veo dentro de ese equipo. Y para mí es eso: es de “este lado” y no del otro. Yo no estoy buscando un salvador, estoy viendo con quién me alineo más, quién no me va a dejar colgado. Y aunque a veces no me encante todo lo que dicen los demócratas, siento que ese grupo entiende mejor a familias como la mía que el otro lado que se la pasa hablando como si todos tuviéramos negocio propio o dinero guardado.

No me platiques la novela, dame el clavo: es partido contra partido. Y yo, por lo que he vivido, jalo más para ese lado.

dislike_dem_candidate.reason_category: Views on immigration levels, border security, undocumented immigrants, or asylum policies.
dislike_dem_candidate.text:
Mire, yo he votado demócrata muchas veces, pero con Kamala y ese grupo siento que se les va la mano con la frontera, y eso sí me hace ruido, fuerte. Yo soy inmigrante, yo sé lo que es batallar y hacer las cosas derechas, y no me gusta cuando parece que el partido se inclina a abrir sin tener control. No es falta de corazón, es que sin orden se hace un desorden que termina pegándole a la gente que ya está aquí trabajando, incluyendo a familias como la mía.  

Los demócratas hablan mucho de compasión —y está bien— pero a veces se les olvida la otra mitad: reglas claras y consecuencias. Y cuando escucho a Kamala en ese tema, siento que está más del lado de “dejen pasar” que de “vamos a ordenar esto bien”. Yo no soy de pleito, pero ahí sí digo: nuestro lado tiene que ser responsable también, no nada más bien intencionado.

dislike_rep_candidate.reason_category: Comments about temperament, honesty, likability, morality, or personal behavior.
dislike_rep_candidate.text:
Mire, yo no soy de pleito, pero… a mí lo que me brinca de ese hombre es cómo se comporta. No es cosa de políticas, es de carácter. Yo crecí con la idea de que si vas a estar al frente, te conduces con respeto, con palabra, aunque estés enojado. Y este señor, perdón, pero parece que siempre está buscando pleito, diciendo cosas que no medirías ni en una mesa con tu familia.

En mi lado —la gente con la que yo me junto, en la iglesia, en el barrio— valoramos la decencia básica. No tienes que ser perfecto, nadie lo es, pero sí tener cierto freno. Y él no lo tiene. Se burla, insulta, cambia lo que dijo… eso a mí no me cuadra. Yo puedo trabajar con alguien duro, claro que sí, pero no con alguien que no sabes si mañana va a respetar su propia palabra.

Entonces sí, eso me empuja en contra. No es que el otro lado sea santo, ni de chiste, pero al menos uno espera un mínimo de comportamiento. Aquí siento que es puro show y pleito, y yo ya a esta edad no estoy para andar siguiendo a alguien así.

like_dem_party.reason_category: Concerns about democratic norms, elections, corruption, legal issues, or threats to democracy.
like_dem_party.text:
Mire, yo no soy de andar con banderas todo el día, pero le voy a decir claro: lo que sí me gusta de los demócratas es que, en general, respetan el juego como debe ser. Las elecciones son elecciones, se ganan o se pierden, y ya. A mí me enseñaron en la obra que si la medida no te gusta, no cambias la cinta, corriges el corte. Pues aquí igual.

Cuando veo al otro lado queriendo poner en duda todo, como si cada resultado fuera trampa nomás porque no les conviene, eso no me entra. Eso es peligroso. Una cosa es pelear ideas, otra es querer voltear la mesa. Y yo ya viví suficiente para saber que cuando se rompe esa confianza, luego nadie respeta nada.

Yo puedo no estar de acuerdo en muchas cosas con los demócratas, claro que sí. Pero en eso… en cuidar que el proceso sea limpio, que la ley aplique parejo y que no andemos inventando cuentos cuando perdemos… ahí me quedo más tranquilo con ellos que con los otros. Porque sin eso, todo lo demás se cae. Así de simple.

dislike_dem_party.reason_category: Views on immigration levels, border security, undocumented immigrants, or asylum policies.
dislike_dem_party.text:

Siento que dentro del partido hay muchos que hablan como si no hubiera límite, como si nomás fuera “que pase quien sea y luego vemos”. Y no es así en la vida real. Aquí en las comunidades es donde cae todo: escuelas, rentas, trabajo… y nadie te explica cómo se va a sostener eso. Y cuando uno lo dice, luego luego te quieren poner como si fueras anti-inmigrante, y no, tampoco.

Mira, yo no soy de pleito, pero mi problema con ellos es ese: parecen más preocupados por verse bien diciendo “sí a todo” que por poner orden con respeto. Y sin orden, la cosa se rompe. Yo he visto cómo cuesta hacer las cosas aunque sea medio bien, entonces cuando oigo ese discurso tan abierto, no me cuadra.

like_rep_party.reason_category: Positions on abortion, Roe v. Wade, reproductive freedom, or pro-life/pro-choice issues.

Y siento que los demócratas lo tratan muy ligero, como si fuera puro “derecho” y ya, sin hablar de la responsabilidad que viene junto. A mí eso no me cuadra, no con mi fe ni con cómo me enseñaron a hacerme cargo de las cosas. Yo no soy perfecto, pero sí creo que hay líneas que uno no debe cruzar.

Los republicanos, con todos sus defectos, por lo menos se paran más firmes en eso. Y yo respeto eso, porque no es cosa pequeña. Es vida.

Veo recortes, veo que aprietan por un lado, pero a los de arriba siempre les encuentran cómo acomodarles las cosas.

Yo le hablo como lo viví, no como lo vi en internet. Y con ellos, muchas veces siento que es al revés: dicen que si a los de arriba les va bien, a nosotros nos cae algo… pero pues yo sigo viendo que todo sube y el cheque no estira.

No me platiques la novela, dame el clavo: si después de toda una vida trabajando, todavía estás batallando para cubrir lo básico, entonces ese manejo no está jalando para nosotros. Por eso ahí es donde me pierden.
