Yeah. For me it comes down to the border, plain and simple.

I grew up in a place where if a fence is down, you fix the fence. Not because you hate the neighbor, but because lines matter or the whole thing turns into a mess you can’t manage. That’s how I look at the country. I don’t have anything against people wanting a better life, but the way Democrats talk about it, like the rules don’t really count or you can just sort it out later, that doesn’t sit right with me.

Trump, whatever else people say about him, he talks like the border actually needs to be controlled, like it’s not optional. That lands with me. I’ve spent my whole life keeping systems running—vehicles, schedules, even my own health after that surgery—and if you let basic order slip, you pay for it later.

So yeah, that’s the piece that would push me his way. Republicans at least sound like they take that seriously, and Democrats don’t, not in a way I trust.

dislike_rep_candidate.reason_category: Comments about temperament, honesty, likability, morality, or personal behavior.

Yeah, there is, and it’s not about policy for me. It’s how he carries himself.

I was raised—and the Army just hammered it in—that if you’re in charge, you keep your mouth under control and you don’t make everything about yourself. With him, it always feels like the opposite. Too much talking, too quick to throw names around, too much heat where a steady hand would do better. If we’re the party that says we stand for order and law, then we don’t get to pick and choose when that applies.

Yeah—if I’m being straight, it’s immigration and border handling. That’s the one thing that can actually push me into “do I sit this out or vote differently,” which I don’t say lightly.

I’m from El Paso, so this isn’t abstract to me. I’ve seen what it looks like when policy is either too loose or too performative. You get real people—families, kids—showing up in situations we are not actually set up to handle with dignity, and then local systems just absorb the chaos. And then everybody argues on TV while the border communities and places like here in Aurora deal with the fallout later—housing, jobs, clinic demand, all of it.

So here’s my issue with her specifically: I don’t feel a clear, grounded plan that balances compassion with actual control of the system. It can sound like, “we’re humane,” which I agree with, but then what does that look like on Tuesday morning at a processing center? Who’s staffing it? How fast are cases moving? What happens when numbers spike? Because when that part is fuzzy, what I’ve seen—both back home and even with some of our patients here—is people get stuck in limbo, or cities get overwhelmed, and then the politics gets uglier.

like_dem_candidate.text:
I’ll give you my plain answer: it would come down to whether I believed she could actually run the place competently day to day—keep things steady, make decisions, and not let everything drift.

And right now, I don’t see that. I haven’t watched her operate in a way that says, “this person has a firm hand on the wheel.” It feels more like messaging than management. In my world, you can’t just talk about the system—you’ve got to understand where the pressure builds and what fails at 2 a.m. when nobody’s watching. I haven’t seen that kind of command from her.

So is there something that would make me vote for her? Yes—if I were convinced she had that operational competence. But I’m not there.

dislike_dem_candidate.reason_category: Views on immigration levels, border security, undocumented immigrants, or asylum policies.
dislike_dem_candidate.text:
Yeah—border policy, plain and simple. I don’t like how loose it’s been handled, and from what I’ve seen and heard, she’s been right in the middle of that approach. You can dress it up with language about compassion and processing, but if the system can’t control who’s coming in and how fast, then it’s not a system—it’s pressure building with no relief valve.

I’m not saying shut the door and throw away the key. I’m saying have a process that actually holds. Know who’s coming in, enforce it consistently, and don’t create incentives for people to bypass the legal path. Right now it feels like we’re signaling, “Get here first, we’ll sort it out later,” and that’s exactly backwards.

I’ve spent my life looking at systems that fail when you overload them. Water lines, treatment plants—doesn’t matter. You exceed capacity long enough, something gives. Same idea here. And when I hear her talk, I don’t hear a plan that respects limits. I hear a lot of talk that sounds nice but doesn’t answer, “Show me where it fails.”

So yeah, that’s the sticking point for me. If you can’t or won’t get a handle on the border, I’m not going to be comfortable putting you in charge of anything that depends on order.

like_rep_candidate.reason_category: Evaluations of a candidate’s qualifications, preparedness, track record, or ability to govern.
like_rep_candidate.text:
I’ll give you the plain version: he’s actually been in the chair and made the thing run, at least for a stretch. That matters to me more than speeches. You can argue about style all day, but when I look at it like a system, I ask, did the system function under his watch in a way that people could live with—jobs, costs, basic stability—and for a period there, it did.

I’m not saying it was perfect, nothing is. But I’ve seen plenty of people who sound polished and then can’t execute when it counts. In engineering terms, talk is cheap—show me the system under load. He’s been under load. That’s the piece that makes me even consider voting for him again.

like_dem_party.reason_category: Support or opposition based on party identity, ideological labels, or general alignment (e.g., liberal/conservative).
like_dem_party.text:
I’ll give you my plain answer, and it’s not a long list. What I can say I *like*—or at least respect—is that the Democratic Party is consistently on the side of a stronger central government. I don’t agree with where they take that most of the time, but I’ll give them this: they’re not confused about it.

They believe problems get solved by building out federal programs, more oversight, more coordination from the top. There’s a kind of internal consistency there. You know what you’re getting. It’s not bouncing around depending on the day or the headline.

Now, for me, that same instinct is exactly where I part ways. I’ve spent too many years watching decisions get worse the farther they are from the ground. But I can at least respect a party that says, “This is our philosophy,” and sticks to it, instead of pretending to be something else.

dislike_dem_party.reason_category: Concerns about democratic norms, elections, corruption, legal issues, or threats to democracy.
dislike_dem_party.text:
I’ll give you my plain answer: I don’t like the way parts of the Democratic Party get loose around the idea of fair process in elections, then act like that concern itself is out of bounds.

And I’m not talking in slogans, I mean the nuts and bolts. When you start changing rules close to an election, expanding things quickly without clear guardrails, or brushing off questions about how ballots are handled and verified—well, that’s where my engineer brain kicks in. Show me where it fails. Because every system fails somewhere if you don’t design it tight.

What bothers me is the attitude that if you even ask about it, you’re treated like you’re trying to undermine democracy. No—checking the system is part of protecting it. In water work, if somebody said “don’t question the pressure readings, just trust the system,” I’d say you’re asking for a break.

So it’s that tone and that approach: moving pieces around and then discouraging scrutiny. That doesn’t sit right with me. You don’t build trust that way—you erode it. And once trust goes, good luck getting it back.

dislike_dem_candidate.reason_category: Support or opposition based on party identity, ideological labels, or general alignment (e.g., liberal/conservative).
dislike_dem_candidate.text:
Yeah. For me it’s pretty straightforward — she’s on the Democratic side of the aisle in a way that’s not moderate anymore, and that’s a problem for me.

I’m not looking at this like a personality contest. I’m looking at alignment. And when I hear her talk or look at where she’s lined up politically, it tracks with a more progressive, government-heavy approach that I just don’t agree with. Bigger federal role, more top-down decision-making — that whole lane. That’s not how I think things should be run, and it doesn’t match how I’ve seen things actually work on the ground.

So it’s less about her as an individual and more about what she represents politically. That’s enough for me to vote against her.

like_rep_candidate.reason_category: Mentions of climate change, environmental policy, energy production, or sustainability.
like_rep_candidate.text:
Yeah—energy policy, specifically how aggressive he is about domestic production. From my perspective, when we clamp down too hard on oil and gas here in the name of climate policy, we don’t actually reduce demand, we just outsource production to countries with lower standards. That feels like a PR move, not a real solution. I’d rather see the U.S. produce what we’re already going to use, with tighter controls and actual accountability, than pretend we’re “clean” while importing it.

Also, practically, energy costs bleed into everything—hospital operations, supply chains, travel, all of it. When prices spike, it’s not abstract, it shows up in how systems cut corners or delay purchases. So a candidate who prioritizes energy independence and stable pricing gets my attention more than someone selling a broad climate narrative without a clear, workable mechanism. Don’t sell me a brochure—show me how it functions on a Tuesday when the grid’s under pressure and costs are up.

like_dem_party.reason_category: References to healthcare, Medicare/Medicaid, prescription costs, social safety nets, or aid to disadvantaged groups.
like_dem_party.text:
Yeah—prescription drug costs. That’s the one area where I’ll give Democrats credit, full stop.

I’m in and out of endocrinology offices, I’ve done the lab cycles, I’ve dealt with the “this is covered, this isn’t, surprise bill” routine, and it’s a mess. When they push on capping insulin or letting Medicare negotiate prices, that’s not abstract to me—that’s Tuesday. It’s the difference between people actually staying on their meds versus stretching doses or just dropping off.

And from the hospital side, I see how pricing games ripple through everything—formularies, what gets approved, what patients can realistically access after discharge. So anything that puts real pressure on drug pricing instead of just issuing a press release, I’m for. Don’t sell me a brochure—if it lowers what patients pay at the pharmacy counter, that’s a win.

dislike_dem_party.reason_category: Support or opposition based on party identity, ideological labels, or general alignment (e.g., liberal/conservative).
dislike_dem_party.text:
Yeah. The big thing for me is how far left the party has moved ideologically, especially on social issues. It feels like the baseline now is a very progressive worldview, and if you don’t line up with that, you’re treated like you’re the problem. I’m not talking about fringe Twitter stuff—I mean what I actually hear in mainstream messaging.  

From where I sit, it’s like the party assumes a certain set of beliefs about identity, gender, and culture that I just don’t share, and there’s not a lot of room to disagree without being labeled. I’m a practicing Catholic, so I’m going to see some of that differently, and I don’t think that automatically makes me backwards or uninformed.  

So yeah, it’s less one specific bill and more that the party’s ideological center has shifted in a way that doesn’t match how I see the world.

like_rep_party.reason_category: Mentions of climate change, environmental policy, energy production, or sustainability.
like_rep_party.text:
Yeah—energy policy, specifically how they approach domestic production.

I like the focus on actually producing energy here instead of pretending we can run a modern economy on wishful thinking. In my world, hospitals don’t get to “opt out” of power demand—ORs, imaging, manufacturing for devices—it all depends on stable, affordable energy. When policy leans into shutting down fossil fuel production too fast without a real replacement that can carry the load, costs go up and reliability gets shaky, and that hits everything downstream.

What I see from Republicans is more willingness to say, “Okay, we can work on emissions, but we’re not going to kneecap our own energy supply in the process.” That balance—keep things running, keep costs somewhat predictable—that makes sense to me in a real, Tuesday-morning operations kind of way.

like_dem_candidate.reason_category: Concerns about democratic norms, elections, corruption, legal issues, or threats to democracy.
like_dem_candidate.text:
If I’m being very specific, it’s this: I want someone who will respect the mechanics of elections and the peaceful handoff of power, even when it doesn’t go their way.

I watched 2020 from inside a system that was already under strain, and what bothered me wasn’t disagreement—it was how quickly people were willing to cast doubt on the process itself without evidence that held up. You can’t run a country if half the population thinks the scoreboard is rigged every time they lose. That’s not a policy disagreement, that’s a systems failure.

I’m not looking for somebody who sounds nice on TV, I’m looking at what happened when he was already in the chair. And whether people like him or not, we’ve seen him do the job once. That matters to me.

I’m not big on these career politicians who’ve been in there 30, 40 years and still act like everything’s somebody else’s fault. That’s both parties sometimes, but especially the Democrats lately, just a lot of talking and not a lot of straight answers. With Trump, you may not like how he says it, but at least you’ve got a record you can point to and say, “Alright, this is what he did, this is how he handled it.”

I’m a “show me” kind of guy. Same as at the hardware store—some new kid comes in telling me a tool’s the best thing ever, I don’t care what the box says, I want to know who’s actually used it and what happened after six months. Trump’s already been “used,” so to speak. We’ve seen how he operates under pressure, how he deals with other countries, how he runs the whole machine.

So yeah, if I’m voting for him, that’s the core of it. He’s already been tested in the job. To me, that carries more weight than somebody promising they’ll figure it out once they get there.

Let me tell it to you the way my toolbox sees it: if you treat every bolt like it’s stripped, you’re gonna wreck a lot of good threads. Same idea. Now, does that mean I’m jumping ship and voting the other way? That’s a whole different question. Because on our side, we’re looking at the bigger picture, and I don’t trust their side any further than I can throw a toolbox. She’s had to make decisions with legal, political, and operational consequences all at once, and defend them under pressure. I care about that because governing isn’t a TED Talk, it’s a series of calls where half your options are bad and you still have to pick one and own it. I’d rather have someone who’s been stress-tested in real systems than someone learning on the fly.

dislike_dem_candidate.reason_category: Support or opposition based on party identity, ideological labels, or general alignment (e.g., liberal/conservative).
dislike_dem_candidate.text:
Yeah—if she suddenly started drifting toward the center in a way that felt like she was sanding down core Democratic positions just to look “electable,” that would lose me fast.

I’m not interested in a Democrat who starts acting like a cautious moderate Republican in nicer packaging. If I wanted that, I’d just vote for the actual thing. I want someone who’s clearly operating from a liberal framework and not apologizing for it every five minutes. The second it feels like she’s trying to split the difference on basic party values, I’m out.

like_rep_candidate.reason_category: Views on immigration levels, border security, undocumented immigrants, or asylum policies.
like_rep_candidate.text:
Yeah. Border control, full stop.

I spend my days inside hospital systems that are already stretched, and I’m not interested in pretending capacity is unlimited because it sounds nice. When people come in without documentation, they still get treated — ethically, that’s correct — but it doesn’t magically come with funding, staffing, or planning. It just lands on the system and everyone scrambles.

What I hear from Trump, whether you like how he says it or not, is at least an acknowledgment that you need a controlled front door. Clear rules, enforced. Not this half-open, half-denied mess where we act surprised every six months that intake is higher than what the infrastructure can handle. I can work with a system that has rules. I’m not here for a free-for-all followed by hand-wringing.

dislike_rep_candidate.reason_category: Comments about temperament, honesty, likability, morality, or personal behavior.
dislike_rep_candidate.text:
Yeah. His relationship with the truth.

I’m not here for a mystery, and with him everything feels like a moving target. He’ll say something, then say he didn’t, then say you misunderstood, and expect everyone to just keep up. I work in rooms where if you misstate one detail, you lose the contract. So watching someone operate at that level with that kind of casual dishonesty—it’s a hard no for me.

dislike_dem_party.reason_category: References to healthcare, Medicare/Medicaid, prescription costs, social safety nets, or aid to disadvantaged groups.
dislike_dem_party.text:
Yeah. They overpromise on healthcare access and then hand you something that doesn’t actually function on the ground.

I’m in hospitals all week. I see what “coverage” looks like when a patient still can’t afford the prescription, or Medicaid reimbursement is so low providers quietly limit how many of those patients they take. You get people technically insured and still bouncing between gaps—delayed care, denied devices, paperwork ping-pong. Don’t tell me we “fixed access” if the person can’t get the medication or the clinic won’t schedule them.

If you want my honest take, it’s this: stop selling big, shiny coverage numbers and fund the unglamorous parts—reimbursement rates, formularies that make sense, and actually paying for preventive care so people aren’t showing up in crisis. I’m not here for a headline. I’m here for something that works.

dislike_rep_party.reason_category: References to crime, policing, public safety, gun rights or gun control.
dislike_rep_party.text:
Yeah. The way they handle guns and public safety.

I’m in and out of hospitals and city neighborhoods all week, and I watch what happens when unstable situations meet easy access to firearms. It’s not abstract to me. And the Republican line—every time—is basically “more guns, fewer restrictions, don’t touch it.” That’s not a plan, that’s a refusal to deal with reality.

I’m not asking for anything exotic. Background checks that actually catch people, some accountability for how weapons move around, basic guardrails. Instead it turns into this absolutist thing, like any limit is tyranny. Meanwhile, the rest of us are supposed to just absorb the risk and call it freedom. I’m not here for that.

like_dem_candidate.text: Honestly? I don’t get a straight, grounded vibe off her. Feels polished in a way that makes me wonder what’s real and what’s just talking points. If I can’t read someone, I don’t trust ’em, simple as that.

like_dem_candidate.reason_category: Positions on abortion, Roe v. Wade, reproductive freedom, or pro-life/pro-choice issues.
like_dem_candidate.text:
Yeah—honestly, the cleanest, most decisive reason for me is where she is on abortion access. That’s the one that actually cuts through all my usual “it depends” instincts.

I’m not coming at it from a slogan place. I’m thinking about it as a system that either gives people options or traps them when something goes sideways. After my dad’s stroke and just being more embedded in healthcare logistics in general, I’ve seen how quickly “edge cases” become real life. Pregnancy is not some neat, predictable process where you can write one rule and call it done. There are timing issues, risk tradeoffs, medical judgment calls—like, you need room for a doctor and a patient to make a decision without wondering if they’re crossing some legal tripwire.

And I’ve got a daughter now. That changes the math in a way that’s hard to overstate. I don’t want her growing up in a system where her options depend on her ZIP code or where the default is “wait until it’s worse” because that’s what the law allows. That just feels like bad design. It introduces friction at exactly the moment you want clarity and speed.

What I associate with Harris—fairly or not—is a pretty unambiguous stance that those decisions stay with the individual and their doctor, not the state. And for me, that’s one of those areas where I don’t want a half-step or a clever compromise. I want it to be boringly clear what the rule is so people can act without second-guessing the consequences.

So yeah, if I strip everything else away, that’s the lever that actually moves me toward voting for her.

dislike_rep_candidate.reason_category: Concerns about democratic norms, elections, corruption, legal issues, or threats to democracy.
dislike_rep_candidate.text:
Yeah—if I boil it down to one thing, it’s how he treats the basic rules of elections and losing. That’s the line for me.

I can live with policy disagreements, I can even live with leaders who are a little chaotic if the system holds. But the 2020 aftermath—pushing claims he couldn’t substantiate, leaning on officials to “find” votes, and then not cleanly conceding—that breaks the mechanism that everything else sits on. I keep coming back to this like a systems problem: if the scoreboard itself is up for grabs, then every downstream decision is suspect. You can’t run a country on “whoever yells loudest about fraud wins.”

And I’m not saying elections have to be perfect—there’s always some noise—but there’s a difference between tightening processes and trying to delegitimize the outcome when you lose. That’s where I get off. It’s not about liking or disliking him personally; it’s that I don’t trust someone who treats the transfer of power as negotiable. For me, that’s disqualifying.

like_dem_party.reason_category: Support or opposition based on party identity, ideological labels, or general alignment (e.g., liberal/conservative).
like_dem_party.text:
Yeah—honestly the thing I keep coming back to is that, identity-wise, Democrats are the party that at least tries to build in a baseline of fairness. And I know that sounds a little abstract, but I don’t mean it in a “everyone hold hands” way. I mean it pretty concretely: the idea that if you get sick, lose a job, hit some bad luck, the system shouldn’t just let you fall straight through.

That maps pretty directly to how I see the world. I’m not anti-business—I work in it, I believe in it—but I don’t think markets by themselves produce outcomes most people would recognize as acceptable without some guardrails. So when I say I “like” the Democratic Party, it’s really that alignment: they’re the side that, at least in intent, is trying to smooth out the worst-case scenarios for regular people rather than just saying “that’s how it goes.”

And yeah, I get frustrated with how they execute sometimes—like, a lot—but at the identity level, if I have to pick a team, that’s the one that’s closest to my instinct of what a functional, fair system should look like.

dislike_dem_party.reason_category: References to healthcare, Medicare/Medicaid, prescription costs, social safety nets, or aid to disadvantaged groups.
dislike_dem_party.text:
Yeah—this is the one that actually gets under my skin a bit. I think Democrats are directionally right on healthcare and the safety net, but the way they execute it feels… half-built and then declared finished.

Like, I went through this pretty directly with my dad after his stroke. What you want in that moment is a system that behaves like a system—clear handoffs, someone accountable, costs that don’t feel like random number generation. Instead it’s Medicare rules here, supplemental coverage there, rehab approvals that seem to reset every few weeks, prescription pricing that depends on which layer you’re looking at. And everyone you talk to is trying, but no one actually owns the full picture.

And I look at Democrats and I’m like—this is your home turf. You say you want healthcare to be a right, you want to protect people from getting crushed by bad luck. I agree with that. But then the policies end up as these stacked patches: subsidies on top of private plans, negotiation on a subset of drugs but not others, eligibility cliffs that make no intuitive sense unless you’ve read the fine print. It’s not that the intent is wrong—it’s that the system design never gets cleaned up.

Let’s not make it mystical: if I can’t explain to my mom, in five minutes, what something will cost and who’s responsible for her care, the system is failing at the point of use. And I don’t see Democrats pushing hard enough for that kind of clarity. It’s a lot of “we expanded coverage” or “we capped this category,” which matters, but from the inside it still feels like you’re navigating a maze when you’re already stressed and vulnerable.

So yeah, that’s the frustration. I’m on their side in terms of goals, but I don’t think they finish the job in a way that actually makes the experience sane for normal people.

dislike_rep_party.reason_category: Mentions of LGBTQ rights, gender identity, transgender issues, or related cultural debates.
dislike_rep_party.text:
Yeah—if I strip it down to the one thing that consistently puts me off, it’s how the party handles LGBTQ issues, especially around gender identity. I’m not coming at this from some abstract culture-war angle; it just feels like they’ve locked into a posture that treats a pretty small, already vulnerable group as a political lever, and I don’t think that’s a great look or a great outcome.

Like, I get that there are real questions to work through—sports, medical decisions for minors, all of that. Those aren’t fake issues. But the tone and the policy direction often feel way out of proportion to the actual problem size. It’s very… sweeping. Bans, restrictions, hard lines, not a lot of “let’s define edge cases carefully and build something that actually works in practice.” And that’s where I get uncomfortable, because I’m always asking, okay, what’s the system we’re building here, who does it help, and what are the second-order effects?

I have friends and coworkers who are gay or trans, and they’re just… normal people trying to have stable lives, same as anyone. When the rhetoric turns into “we need to crack down on this whole category,” it feels like we’re solving for signaling rather than outcomes. I’m allergic to that kind of drama math—where the narrative is louder than the actual data.

So yeah, that’s the piece where I kind of disengage. I’m open to policy debates if they’re grounded and specific, but what I usually see from Republicans on this topic feels blunt and overcorrecting in a way that doesn’t line up with my baseline of fairness.

Yo he visto a los demócratas prometer mucho, pero luego todo se vuelve más caro o más enredado. Y uno acá no tiene tiempo para andar peleando con seguros o papeleo cuando lo que quiere es que lo atiendan y ya. Con los republicanos, y con Trump en particular, yo siento —aunque no sea perfecto— que al menos no traen esa idea de meterse en todo y complicarlo más.

No le digo que sea santo ni nada de eso. Pero cuando uno ya pasó por un susto del corazón, cambia la cosa. Yo no quiero que me “administren” la vida desde lejos. Quiero poder pagar, entrar, que me vean, y salir con lo que necesito. Y en eso… pues sí, me inclino más para ese lado.

I’ve seen this movie before with elections—sometimes you’re not picking a person you feel strongly about, you’re picking the direction you think is steadier. dislike_dem_candidate.reason_category: Mentions of the economy, jobs, inflation, wages, taxation, government spending, or overall financial management.
dislike_dem_candidate.text:
I’m going to say this plain, because that’s how I look at these things now—my concern would be how she handles the economy, especially spending. I’ve lived long enough to know that when Washington starts talking big about new programs and big packages, that money has to come from somewhere, and it trickles down in ways people don’t always admit. I feel it at the grocery store more than anywhere else. I used to be able to fill a cart for my household without doing math in my head the whole time, and now I catch myself putting things back.

And look, I’m not an economist, that’s not my lane. But I do pay my bills, I still have a mortgage at 71, and I’m helping support my daughter and grandson. So when I hear messaging that sounds like more spending without a very clear, grounded plan for keeping everyday costs steady, that gives me pause. I’ve seen this movie before—good intentions, but regular people end up tightening their belts while officials say everything is improving.

I don’t need a big speech about long-term growth. I need to know: are my utilities, my groceries, my basic life going to stay manageable? If I don’t feel confident about that, that alone is enough to make me hesitate and possibly vote the other way.

like_dem_party.reason_category: Support or opposition based on party identity, ideological labels, or general alignment (e.g., liberal/conservative).
like_dem_party.text:
Well… I wouldn’t say I’m “with” them, but I do like that they lean more toward a government that actually steps in and helps people, instead of just saying everyone ought to figure it out on their own. That whole “we’re in this together” mindset—more on the liberal side of things—sits better with me than the every-man-for-himself approach. I’ve seen too many families over the years who were working hard and still needed a little structure or support to stay afloat, and that philosophy at least acknowledges that.

Now, I don’t agree with everything they attach to that, not by a long shot, but that general direction—more community-minded, more willing to use government as a tool—that part I can respect.

dislike_dem_party.reason_category: Mentions of LGBTQ rights, gender identity, transgender issues, or related cultural debates.
dislike_dem_party.text:
I’m going to say this carefully, because I don’t have any interest in being unkind. But what sits wrong with me is how the Democratic Party handles gender identity issues, especially with children and schools. It feels like it moved very fast, and now you’re expected to go along with things that, in a real school on a real Tuesday, are not simple at all.

I’ve sat with middle schoolers who can’t decide who their friends are from one week to the next, and now we’re asking them to make very big identity decisions and expecting every adult to affirm it immediately, no questions. I’ve seen situations where teachers were afraid to say the wrong thing, parents felt pushed out, and the child was still clearly struggling underneath all of it. That doesn’t feel like good care to me, it feels like adults trying to keep up with a policy.

I’m not against treating people with dignity — of course not. But I don’t like the sense that if you ask a practical question or say “slow down,” you’re treated like you’ve done something wrong. I’ve seen this movie before in schools: when adults get rigid about a position instead of paying attention to the child in front of them, somebody gets missed. That’s the part I don’t like.

dislike_dem_candidate.reason_category: Evaluations of a candidate’s qualifications, preparedness, track record, or ability to govern.
dislike_dem_candidate.text:
I’ll say it straight: I’m not convinced by her track record actually running something complicated end-to-end. That’s the piece that matters to me. It’s one thing to speak well, to show up in hearings, to take positions—I’m not dismissing that—but I look for, “What did you take responsibility for, where were the failure points, and how did you fix them when it got messy?”

In my world, a clinic looks fine on paper until denials spike, staff starts cutting corners, and suddenly cash flow drops two months later. The person I trust is the one who can walk into that, trace the problem, and stabilize it without creating three new problems. When I look at her career, I don’t see enough of that kind of operational ownership where the results are clear and tested under pressure.

Maybe she has done more behind the scenes—I’m open to that—but I’m not seeing the kind of “this worked, here’s how, here’s the before and after” that makes me feel steady. And at this stage, I vote like I manage: show me the outcome, not just the intention.

like_dem_party.reason_category: Mentions of the economy, jobs, inflation, wages, taxation, government spending, or overall financial management.
like_dem_party.text:
I’ll give you one thing, and it’s the main thing for me: when they talk about wages and the cost side of life, it at least sounds like they understand the pressure people are under month to month. Not theory—rent, groceries, payroll, insurance premiums. That layer.

I’m in billing, so I see numbers all day, and I run a household with three generations. When prices jump even a little, it’s not abstract. It’s “okay, what gets delayed, what gets cut, who covers the gap.” What I tend to like from Democrats—when they’re being practical, not performative—is the push for higher wages and some guardrails so everything doesn’t just float up while pay stays stuck. Because I’ve watched clinics where staff are working hard, collections are tight, and still the front desk can’t keep up with their own bills. That gap is real.

Now, I’m not saying they always execute it cleanly—sometimes the math doesn’t survive a Tuesday, and small businesses feel it—but the focus on wages and cost pressure, that part I don’t argue with. It matches what I actually see.

like_rep_party.reason_category: Support or opposition based on party identity, ideological labels, or general alignment (e.g., liberal/conservative).
like_rep_party.text:
I’ll be honest, the one thing I do find myself agreeing with them on is that more conservative, smaller-government mindset. Not in a slogan way, I mean in the sense that decisions should stay closer to the ground instead of everything being pushed from some national level that doesn’t see the day-to-day reality.

I’ve spent too many years in clinics watching top-down rules come in that look clean on paper and then just… break in real life. Staff gets squeezed, patients get confused, and nobody at the top feels it. So when Republicans talk about limiting how much the federal side controls and leaving more to states or local systems, that part makes sense to me. It lines up with how I already think: if it can’t survive a Tuesday in a busy clinic, it’s not a good plan.

Now, do they always execute that well? No. But that basic idea—less centralized control, more local decision-making—that’s the piece where I nod a little.

like_dem_candidate.reason_category: References to race, gender, religion, or symbolic representation of social groups.
like_dem_candidate.text: I’ll be straight with you—about the only thing that would make me pause is that she’s a daughter of immigrants and a minority woman who made it all the way up there. I remember what it felt like coming here in the 80s, being the odd one out, having to prove yourself twice just to be seen as competent. So when I see someone with that kind of background reach that level, there’s a part of me that respects it, like, okay, you didn’t just get handed a clean path. I like that Democrats at least talk like regular people should be able to see a doctor without it turning into a whole financial crisis.  

When I got diagnosed with hypertension, that wasn’t optional for me—I needed meds, checkups, all of that. So anything that makes that more reachable, I’m paying attention to.  

It’s not perfect, I’m not saying that. But yeah… I lean that way because it feels closer to “people should be able to get care and not fall apart over it.” That matters to me, for real.

dislike_dem_party.reason_category: Assessments of whether a candidate or party is effective, organized, cooperative, or able to govern.
dislike_dem_party.text:
Yeah… I don’t trust them to run things clean and steady. That’s my issue.

From where I sit, it feels like a lot of talking, a lot of promises, and then when it’s time to actually execute, it turns into confusion, delays, or finger-pointing. And I’m not watching this like a sport—I’m dealing with systems every week. Insurance approvals, clinic coordination, billing codes… when leadership isn’t tight and predictable, that mess rolls downhill fast. People like me feel it first.

I’m not trying to be political about it, I’m trying to be practical. I need a party that can organize, make a decision, and follow it through without everything getting tangled up. With Democrats, it often feels like too many voices pulling in different directions, and nobody really in charge of the outcome.

I’m not saying the other side is perfect. But if you’re asking me what I don’t like—it’s that sense that things aren’t being run in a way you can count on. And I can’t build a stable life on “we’ll figure it out later.”

like_dem_candidate.reason_category: Comments about temperament, honesty, likability, morality, or personal behavior.
like_dem_candidate.text:
From what I’ve seen, it comes down to whether I trust how a person carries themselves when nobody’s grading them. With her, I get more of a steady, grown-up presence. Not perfect—nobody is—but she doesn’t come off like she’s trying to win a shouting match every five minutes. That matters to me more than people think.

I pay attention to tone. How someone talks about people they disagree with, how they handle pressure, whether they sound like they’ve got some basic respect in them. With her, I hear someone who at least tries to stay within the lines, if that makes sense. I’ve listened to enough political talk—on both sides—to know when somebody’s just stirring the pot to keep attention on themselves. That kind of behavior, I’m done with it. We’ve had enough of that.

I’m a Democrat, so I’m already leaning that direction, I’m not going to pretend otherwise. But even inside that, I’m not buying a miracle, and I don’t fall in line just because there’s a D next to a name. I still ask, “Would I trust this person in a room where decisions actually affect my family?” And with her, I’m closer to yes than I am with the other side right now.

I’ll put it this way—if you dropped her into a church meeting or a hospital family consult, I think she’d know how to listen, keep it respectful, not make it about herself. That counts with me. Character shows up in small behaviors, not speeches.

dislike_dem_candidate.reason_category: Comments about temperament, honesty, likability, morality, or personal behavior.
dislike_dem_candidate.text:
I’ll be straight with you—what would push me away from Kamala Harris isn’t some checklist of policies, it’s how she comes across as a person. From what I’ve seen and heard, I don’t always trust the way she talks. It feels… practiced in a way that doesn’t sit right with me, like she’s saying what the room wants instead of just saying what she believes and standing on it.

And look, I’m on the Democrat side more often than not, so this isn’t me cheering for the other team. This is me looking at one of ours and thinking, “Are you real with me or not?” Because if I’m going to back somebody, especially at that level, I need to feel like their word holds when the cameras are off too. I’ve worked around enough people—supervisors, plant managers, even pastors—to know the difference between someone who’s steady and someone who shifts depending on who’s listening.

There’ve been moments—I can’t quote you exact dates, but you hear her in interviews or speeches—and I catch myself squinting a little, like, “Alright, what are you really saying here?” That’s a bad sign for me. If I have to do that much decoding, I start to pull back.

So yeah, it comes down to that gut-level read of her character. If I don’t feel like I can take her at her word, then I’m not going to be comfortable standing behind her, even if she’s on “my side.” I’m not buying a miracle just because it’s wearing my team’s jersey.

like_dem_party.reason_category: References to healthcare, Medicare/Medicaid, prescription costs, social safety nets, or aid to disadvantaged groups.
like_dem_party.text:
From what I’ve seen, the main thing I like is they at least *try* to keep regular people from getting crushed by medical costs. I’m not saying they do it perfectly—far from it—but they’re the only side I hear consistently talking like healthcare is something you should be able to use without risking your house.

When my wife had her stroke in 2021, that was the moment everything got real. You’re not thinking politics in the ER, but a few weeks later when the bills start showing up, you start paying attention real quick. Rehab, medications, follow-ups—it stacks up fast, and half the time you’re arguing over codes and what’s “covered.” What I noticed is Democrats, at least the ones I listen to, talk like that situation is a problem that needs fixing. The other side, a lot of times, it sounds like, “Well, that’s just how it is,” or like you should’ve planned better. That doesn’t sit right with me, because you can plan all you want and still get blindsided.

I’m not buying a miracle from either party, but if I’ve got to pick who’s closer to understanding what that feels like—being stuck on the phone, trying to keep your spouse at home, watching every prescription price—it’s them. They talk more about lowering drug costs, keeping Medicare solid, making sure people don’t fall through the cracks. That matters to me more than slogans. I’ve lived the alternative, and it’s not theoretical—it’s a stack of envelopes on your kitchen table.

dislike_dem_party.reason_category: Concerns about democratic norms, elections, corruption, legal issues, or threats to democracy.
dislike_dem_party.text:
I’ll be straight with you—what bothers me about our side is when it feels like we bend the rules we claim to defend. I’m talking about elections, process, basic fairness stuff. You can’t spend all day saying “protect democracy” and then act casual about how primaries are handled, who gets pushed out, or how decisions get made behind closed doors. From where I sit, that starts to look like insider control, not trust in voters, and that’s a problem.

I remember back in a local race—nothing national, just county level—but there was a candidate folks around here actually liked, showed up, shook hands, did the work. Then party people kind of steered things toward someone else, more “approved,” and it left a bad taste. Nobody said anything illegal out loud, but it felt managed. And once you feel that, it’s hard to un-feel it.

Look, I’m not saying the other side is cleaner—they’re not. But if we’re going to say we’re the ones defending the system, then we have to be tighter than that. No shortcuts, no “it’s okay when we do it.” Otherwise regular people like me start wondering if anybody at the top actually trusts us to choose. And once that trust cracks, it’s real hard to fix—like a bad weld, you can grind it down, but it’s never quite the same.

like_rep_party.reason_category: References to crime, policing, public safety, gun rights or gun control.
like_rep_party.text:
From what I’ve seen, yeah—when it comes to public safety, I tend to line up more with Republicans than Democrats, and I don’t mind saying that plain.

I’m a working guy, still out in parking lots at night sometimes, back entrances of churches, small businesses, places where the lights aren’t great and you’re by yourself with a toolbox. I’ve had a couple moments—not headline stuff, just real life—where somebody’s hanging around too long, watching what you’re doing, and you feel that switch flip in your head like, “Alright, stay sharp.” In those moments, I don’t want a long debate about theory. I want to know the law’s on the side of regular people protecting themselves and that police aren’t being second-guessed into hesitation.

I listen to both sides, but Democrats, they can sound like they’re talking around the problem. Republicans—at least the ones I pay attention to—they talk more straight about crime being a problem, about backing law enforcement, about not making it harder for people to defend themselves if something goes sideways. That lands with me. Maybe it’s how I’m wired, maybe it’s just years of being the guy responsible for locking up and making sure things are secure.

I’m not looking for trouble, and I don’t walk around scared, but I believe in being prepared. And between the two sides, Republicans sound more like they respect that instinct instead of trying to explain it away. That matters to me more than people think.

dislike_rep_party.reason_category: Support or opposition based on party identity, ideological labels, or general alignment (e.g., liberal/conservative).
dislike_rep_party.text:
I’ll be straight with you—what rubs me the wrong way about the Republican Party is the whole direction it’s taken, the identity of it. It leans hard into a kind of conservatism that doesn’t line up with how I see people or community. I don’t mean that in a fancy political theory way, I mean in a day-to-day, who’s included and who’s not kind of way.

From where I stand, Democrats—imperfect as they are—at least try to build outward, like you’re widening the circle. Republicans, the way they come across to me now, it feels like narrowing the circle, deciding who counts and who doesn’t. And I’ve never been comfortable with that. I grew up being the kid who had to figure out how to belong, you know? So when a party’s tone feels like “protect what’s ours” instead of “make room,” I’m already halfway out the door.

I’m not saying every Republican voter is like that. I’ve got neighbors, church folks, good people, they vote that way. But the party identity itself, the brand of it, it doesn’t sit right with me. It feels like it’s speaking a different moral language than I am.

If you want the simple version: I lean Democrat because that side, at least in spirit, lines up more with how I think people ought to be treated. The Republican side, as it stands now, doesn’t. That’s the core of it for me.

I’ve sat in executive meetings where diversity was a slide deck and nothing else, and then watched how differently people behaved the minute a woman of color was the one with authority—suddenly the room recalibrates. That’s not symbolism, that’s power dynamics.

dislike_dem_candidate.reason_category: Views on immigration levels, border security, undocumented immigrants, or asylum policies.

Yeah. The border.

I don’t do slogans on this. I’m talking about the actual handling of it — the mixed signals, the “we’re tough / we’re compassionate” press lines while the system on the ground is jammed and inconsistent. That’s a liability wearing lipstick. You can’t have asylum claims backlogged for years, cities scrambling, and then act like it’s under control because the messaging sounds humane.

I’ve spent a career watching what happens when leadership avoids clear rules and enforcement: the burden drops to the lowest level and people get hurt or ignored. Same pattern here. If you can’t show me a clean process — who gets in, how fast claims are decided, how you handle people who don’t qualify — then don’t sell me a sunset about “orderly and humane.” Show me the paper trail. Until then, yeah, that’s the kind of thing that makes me hesitate.

I’ve spent twenty years dealing with executives who rewrite events to protect themselves, and it poisons everything—investigations, accountability, basic trust. Same pattern.  

I don’t vote for people I wouldn’t trust to sit across from me in a closed-door conversation and tell me what actually happened. With him, I’d be taking notes assuming half of it’s fiction. That’s disqualifying for me.

Yeah. The purity tests. That whole “you’re not the right kind of Democrat unless you check every ideological box this week” thing—can’t stand it.

I’ve sat in rooms, literal rooms, where people who agree on 90% of policy will tear each other apart over wording, over tone, over who’s “evolved enough.” It’s exhausting and it’s stupid. Politics is supposed to be about outcomes—labor protections, healthcare access, voting rights—but instead you get this internal policing of identity like it’s a club with a dress code.

I’m very liberal, don’t get it twisted. But I’m not performing it for anyone, and I don’t trust a party that sometimes seems more interested in proving who belongs than actually getting anything done. It starts to feel like ideology as theater, and I’m not buying a ticket.

Yeah. Their obsession with controlling women’s reproductive decisions. That’s the one that does it for me, full stop.

I watched Roe get gutted and all I could think was, here we go, politicians who will never sit in an exam room making medical calls for other people’s bodies. Don’t sell me a sunset about “protecting life” when what it turns into is forced pregnancy, delayed care, doctors hesitating because they’re worried about getting charged. That’s not abstract to me—I’ve worked around healthcare long enough to know exactly how fast “policy” turns into real harm when providers get scared.

You can call it values, you can call it states’ rights, whatever. I call it taking decision-making away from the person actually living in that body and handing it to a legislature. I don’t trust that, and I don’t respect it.

When I hear her talk, it sounds like more programs, more spending, more “we’ll take care of it,” and I’m the one sitting here thinking, with what money? I’m not new. I lived through the recession, I refinanced, I stretched things out just to stay afloat. You don’t forget that. So when someone talks big without being very plain about what it’s going to cost month-to-month for people like me, I don’t trust it. I don’t need speeches. Yes. The way he talks about people who aren’t like him—especially immigrants and Muslims—turns my stomach, and I don’t say that lightly.

I spent over 30 years in a public school classroom. You don’t get to pick which children walk through your door. I had kids from Mexico, from Vietnam, from families that spoke barely any English at home, and you know what? They were just kids. Nervous, trying, wanting to belong. When I hear a grown man, a president no less, talk about whole groups of people like they’re a problem or something to be feared, I think about those children sitting at their desks looking up at me. I can still see their faces clear as day.

And I’m Catholic. We’re not supposed to sort people into “us” and “them” like that. You help who’s in front of you. You don’t label them and push them out. So when he leans into that kind of language—like certain groups are less welcome here—it hits me as wrong in my bones. Not political-wrong. Moral-wrong.

I’m not new. I’ve heard tough talk before from politicians. This isn’t that. This is talking about whole kinds of people like they don’t belong, and I won’t put my name next to that.

like_dem_party.reason_category: References to race, gender, religion, or symbolic representation of social groups.

I spent my whole career in classrooms where half my kids were Hispanic, some Black, some from immigrant families, and you could see plain as day that the system didn’t treat them the same. Mira, una cosa es tener reglas claras y otra es como que todo se vuelve puro mensaje bonito y ya. Y mientras, las ciudades, las escuelas, los servicios… se saturan. Eso no es teoría, eso se ve. Luego ellos hablan como si poner orden fuera algo malo, y pues no, compa, sin orden no funciona nada.

Yo lo veo así: nosotros acá tratando de cumplir, pagar, no meternos en problemas… y del otro lado el partido empujando algo que se siente sin control. No me gusta eso. No es odio ni nada, es más bien que así como ellos lo manejan, no es realista.

Mira, yo soy inmigrante, yo sé lo que es venir a trabajar y hacerte tu lugar. Pero una cosa es eso y otra que todo sea como sin control, como que cualquiera entra y ya. Eso no es justo ni para el que siguió el camino, ni para la gente que ya está aquí tratando de pagar renta, comida, todo subiendo.

Tampoco estoy con lo de “sacar a todos”, eso se me hace puro coraje hablando… pero sí me gusta que de ese lado al menos empujan más la idea de reglas, de quién entra y cómo. Del otro lado a veces se siente como que quieren quedar bien con todos y no dicen nada firme. Y eso, la verdad, no ayuda.

I’m going to be honest, for me it comes down to money and how this country is being run financially. I don’t feel like people on her side respect how tight things are for regular households. Prices go up, groceries, gas, even thread and fabric for me—it all creeps up—and then they talk like it’s just numbers on a chart. It’s not numbers to me. It’s whether I can keep the house steady without juggling bills.

When my husband passed and then that 2009 mess hit, I learned real quick what happens when people in charge get sloppy with money. The ones at the top make decisions, and people like me sit at the kitchen table figuring out which bill waits. I remember calling about the mortgage, feeling like I had to beg just to keep paying what I already owed. That stays with you.

So when I look at her and the Democrats, I see more spending, more promises, and I don’t see enough respect for limits. People talk big until it’s their money on the line. For me, I stick with Republicans because at least they talk like the money matters. I’m not saying they’re perfect, but I trust them more to not turn everything into a bigger, more expensive situation.

It’s more like, okay, this person at least sees people like us and isn’t embarrassed by us.

And around here, that counts for a lot. People I know, church folks, neighbors, they feel the same way — like he’s standing with us, not trying to turn us into something else. I’m not saying he’s perfect, nobody is, but I don’t need perfect. I need someone who feels like they’re pulling in the same direction we are.

The other side… it just feels like they don’t respect us, plain and simple. And after a while, you stop listening to people who make you feel small. So yeah, that’s the main thing for me.

I’ll be honest, I’m a Republican first, that’s where I’m planted. But if you’re asking me to say something fair, I do think Democrats sometimes have a steadier hand when it comes to dealing with other countries. Not always, but enough that I notice it.

What I mean is, they seem more willing to sit down, talk things through, keep things from escalating into something bigger and more dangerous. I grew up around military life, and after my husband passed, I paid a lot more attention to what gets our country pulled into conflict. War is not some abstract thing to me. And I’ll be honest, this is where I part ways very firmly with Republicans who still support him. We’re not just disagreeing on taxes or spending priorities—we’re disagreeing on whether it’s acceptable for a leader to single people out in ways that make them feel less safe or less American because of who they are. For me, that’s not something I can shrug off or “balance” against other considerations.

What wears on me is how they talk about the economy like people are spreadsheets instead of people with grocery lists. I’m sitting at my kitchen table with two piles of mail—what I call “safe” and “not today”—and the “not today” pile has gotten thicker the past few years. Property taxes nudged up, insurance nudged up, groceries are just… higher, in a way you feel every single week. And then I’ll hear national Democrats say, in effect, “things are strong,” and I think, well, for who? That may be true on paper, but it doesn’t match what it feels like to live on one income and try to keep a house.

At the library desk, you hear it in a very plain way. People aren’t talking ideology, they’re asking if we still waive certain small fees, or if there’s a cheaper way to print something, or they’re using the computers to check benefits because something got tighter. That’s the ground level. And I don’t think our side always listens to that without immediately explaining it away.

I’m not saying the other side has some magic fix—I don’t believe that for a second. But Democrats can come across as a little too congratulatory about the big picture and not attentive enough to the monthly math people are doing. I’m not trying to start a whole thing, I’m trying to keep the wheels on. If your message doesn’t line up with what people are actually paying for gas, food, and keeping the lights on, you lose trust, even from people like me who are inclined to be on your side.

I come out of a worldview that leans toward collective responsibility, public institutions, and a kind of shared social floor, and the Republican identity—at least as it presents now—leans much more toward individualism and a smaller view of government than I’m comfortable with.

That said, if I’m trying to answer your question in good faith, what I can respect is that there is a clear ideological spine there. People who identify as Republican tend to know what they believe about government being limited, about personal responsibility, about skepticism of centralized power. Even when I disagree with where that leads, I understand the internal logic of it. As an English teacher, I always appreciated when an argument was at least coherent—“that’s a real sentence,” you know—versus something that shifts depending on the audience.

But emotionally and politically, I don’t feel “at home” there. My instincts line up with the Democratic side of the aisle, especially around the role of public systems and protecting access for people who don’t have a lot of cushion. So anything I “like” is more about recognizing a consistent conservative identity, not a sense that it’s where I belong.

If something’s off, he doesn’t give me a polished speech, he just tells me straight and calls me back when he says he will. That’s trust.

dislike_rep_party.text: Yeah… I mean, straight up, they’re just not my lane ideologically. The whole mindset doesn’t line up with how I see things, so I don’t really trust it.

dislike_dem_candidate.reason_category: Mentions of climate change, environmental policy, energy production, or sustainability.
dislike_dem_candidate.text:
Yeah, for me it comes down to the energy and climate stuff, plain and simple. The Democratic side, and she’s right in the middle of that, talks like we can just flip a switch off fossil fuels and everything will keep humming. That’s not how the real world works, not where I stand.

I’ve spent my whole life around systems that have to run every day, no excuses. Warehouses don’t care about good intentions—if the power’s unstable or fuel costs jump, everything downstream feels it. Food prices, trucking, jobs, all of it. So when I hear big pushes that sound like they’re coming out of a brochure instead of a shop floor, I’m not buying it.

I’m not against cleaner energy—let’s not make it weirder than it is. If something works and doesn’t wreck reliability or jack up costs, I’m all for it. But the way her side talks, it feels rushed and disconnected from how dependent we still are on oil and gas to keep things moving.

So yeah, that’s a big reason I’d lean against her. It just doesn’t line up with how I see things actually functioning day to day.

like_rep_candidate.reason_category: Views on immigration levels, border security, undocumented immigrants, or asylum policies.
like_rep_candidate.text:
Yeah. For me it comes down to the border, plain and simple.

I’m not looking at it like a talking point, I’m looking at it like basic control of your own house. If you don’t know who’s coming in, how many, and what the process is, then the whole thing starts to feel like nobody’s actually in charge. And when Democrats talk about it, half the time it sounds like if you even bring it up, you’re the problem. I’m not buying that. There’s a difference between treating people decently and just letting the system turn into a free-for-all.

What I get from Trump — and yeah, I know he’s rough around the edges — is that Republicans at least act like the border is something you’re supposed to secure first, then figure out the rest. That matches how I think about problems. You don’t leave the door wide open and then argue about house rules.

So it’s not complicated for me. On that issue, it feels like our side is at least trying to control the situation, and their side either won’t or pretends it’s not really a problem. That’s enough to push me toward voting for him.

dislike_rep_candidate.reason_category: Comments about temperament, honesty, likability, morality, or personal behavior.
dislike_rep_candidate.text:
Yeah… I mean, I’m still gonna vote Republican, that’s just where I land, but I’ll tell you straight—Trump makes that harder than it ought to be sometimes.

It’s not policy stuff for me, it’s how he carries himself. There’s a way to fight the other side without acting like every single day is a personal grudge match. After a while it just feels… messy. Like he can’t leave anything alone, always poking, always escalating. I sit there watching and think, “Alright, are we trying to win people over or just keep the pot stirred?”

And the honesty piece—look, I don’t expect a saint, I’m not buying the brochure version of any politician. But I can live with “I don’t know” more than I can live with somebody who sounds like they’re just saying whatever fits the moment. With him, sometimes I’m sitting there going, “Alright, which version is the real one today?”

So yeah, it’s that. The personality, the tone, the constant noise. Doesn’t line up with how I think a leader ought to act, even if I’m still on the same team overall.

like_dem_party.reason_category: Support or opposition based on party identity, ideological labels, or general alignment (e.g., liberal/conservative).
like_dem_party.text:
I mean, I’ll give them this—at least they’re clear about who they are. The Democratic Party leans into that whole liberal, big-government approach, and they don’t really pretend otherwise. I don’t agree with most of it, but I can respect knowing what team you’re on and what you’re gonna push for.

With Republicans, sometimes I feel like we say one thing and then govern another way depending on who’s in charge. Democrats, for better or worse, tend to stick closer to that liberal playbook. I’m not buying the brochure version of it, but I’d rather deal with somebody who’s upfront about their ideology than someone trying to blur it.

dislike_dem_party.reason_category: Mentions of LGBTQ rights, gender identity, transgender issues, or related cultural debates.
dislike_dem_party.text:
Yeah, I’ll tell you straight, the thing that bugs me most about Democrats is how they handle all the gender identity and transgender stuff. It feels like they’ve decided there’s only one acceptable way to talk about it, and if you even slow down and ask a basic question, you’re treated like you’ve got bad intentions.

I’m not out here trying to make anybody’s life harder. If someone’s going through something, fine, treat people decent. I’ve got no interest in bullying anybody. But the way our side sees it, Democrats keep pushing it like it’s settled, no discussion needed, and that just doesn’t match how regular people actually think or talk about it.

At work, guys aren’t sitting around hating anybody, but they’ll say, “Hold on, explain how this is supposed to work in real life.” And instead of getting a straight answer, it turns into labels and lectures. That’s where I check out. I can live with “I don’t know yet, we’re figuring it out.” I’m not buying the brochure version where it’s all perfectly solved and you’re the problem if you don’t nod along.

So yeah, that’s the big one for me. It’s not the existence of the issue — it’s how Democrats handle it, like it’s above debate. That just pushes people like me further away.

like_rep_party.reason_category: Mentions of the economy, jobs, inflation, wages, taxation, government spending, or overall financial management.
like_rep_party.text:
Yeah. For me it comes down to money and how it’s handled, plain and simple.

I’ve lived through what it feels like when things get tight—2008, then my own credit card mess after the divorce—and I pay attention to who at least talks like they understand that you can’t just keep spending like there’s no end to it. The Republican side, in my experience, is closer to how I run my own house: you bring in X, you don’t pretend you’ve got 2X, and if you do, it catches up with you. I’m not saying they’re perfect—nobody in Washington is—but when I’m choosing, I’m going with the group that at least sounds like they’re trying to keep the books from going completely sideways.

And look, I see it day-to-day. Prices go up, groceries cost more, gas jumps around—you feel that stuff immediately. So when one side seems more comfortable with big spending and the other at least makes noise about reining it in, I lean that direction. I’m not buying the brochure version from either party, but on the money side, that’s where I land.

dislike_rep_party.reason_category: Comments about temperament, honesty, likability, morality, or personal behavior.
dislike_rep_party.text:
Yeah, I’ll tell you straight—sometimes our side acts like being right is more important than acting right. And that bugs me.

I don’t expect politicians to be saints, but there’s a tone thing. You’ll hear people on our side come off mean, or like they’re enjoying the fight a little too much, and I think it turns regular people off. Feels less like we’re trying to win folks over and more like we’re trying to score points. I’m not buying the brochure version where that’s supposed to be “strength.” Strength, to me, is saying your piece without acting like a jerk about it.

And look, I’m still on this side, no question. I just think we’d do better if we remembered people are watching how we carry ourselves, not just what team we’re on.

Yeah—border and immigration policy is the big one for me. From what I’ve seen, it feels too loose, like we’re not actually in control of who’s coming in or how fast, and that spills over into real stuff—housing pressure, job competition, and just strain on systems that regular people rely on.

I’m not anti-immigrant at all—I work with guys from all backgrounds—but I think it’s got to be orderly and enforced, or it’s not fair to the people doing it the right way or to families here trying to stay afloat. If it doesn’t look controlled, I’m out.

dislike_rep_candidate.text:
Yeah—if I felt like he wasn’t actually running the job in a disciplined way, that would push me off.

From my side of the fence, the presidency is operations at scale: priorities, follow-through, the right people in the right seats, and not constant churn. When I see turnover, mixed signals, or plans that change midstream, that reads like a job site where nobody’s clearly in charge. I can’t run my business like that—techs get confused, customers get mad, costs go up.

So if I think he’s not executing cleanly—too much chaos, not enough steady management—that’s the kind of thing that would make me vote against him.

When there’s pressure to keep drug prices from getting stupid or to make sure families aren’t choosing between treatment and groceries, I’m for that. I’m not trying to win a debate on it—I’ve just seen the math, and when that side leans into lowering those costs, I respect it.

I’ll be straight with you—I haven’t seen anything from her that makes me think she’d handle the money side better than my side would. That’s the lane I care about. I’m looking at grocery bills, material costs, insurance, the mortgage sitting on my chest every month, and I don’t see Democrats, her included, easing that pressure. If anything, it feels like more spending, more layers, and somehow it still lands back on guys like me trying to keep a household afloat.

I’m not arguing theory here—I’m looking at receipts. What I pay now versus a few years ago, what my clients are willing to spend, how tight people are getting on renovations. If I thought she and her party were going to steady that, bring costs down, make it easier to keep cash flowing, I’d at least consider it. But right now, from where I sit, it looks like more of the same direction, and that’s not working for me.

dislike_dem_candidate.reason_category: Mentions of climate change, environmental policy, energy production, or sustainability.
dislike_dem_candidate.text:
Yeah, I’ll be straight with you—it’s the climate and energy push from her side of the aisle. That whole approach just doesn’t line up with how the real world runs, at least not where I’m standing.

Out here, Phoenix doesn’t forgive. You shut down reliable energy or make it more expensive in the name of big national climate goals, people feel it immediately—AC bills, material costs, everything tied to building and living. I’ve watched what happens when fuel and supply costs jump—suddenly a job that penciled out doesn’t, and somebody eats that, usually the homeowner or the guy like me trying to finish the work.

It feels like her side treats energy like a switch you can flip overnight—go all in on their version of clean, push everything else out—and I don’t buy that. Not because I’m against being responsible, but because I’ve spent 40 years dealing with what actually holds up under pressure. You don’t tear out a load-bearing wall without a plan that works the next day, not ten years from now.

So yeah, that’s the thing that pushes me away. It comes off like theory over reality, and I live in reality every day.

like_rep_candidate.reason_category: Views on international relations, war, defense, global leadership, or national security.
like_rep_candidate.text:
Yeah—if I’m being straight, it’s how he handles the world stage. I don’t need a president to be polite at a dinner party; I need someone other countries don’t try to push around. When Trump was in, it felt like our side—Republicans, people who still think strength matters—had someone who’d actually stare down China, Iran, whoever, without immediately softening the stance to keep everyone comfortable.

I watch how Democrats talk about this stuff, and it sounds like they’re always trying to manage feelings first and leverage second. That makes me uneasy. The world’s not a yoga class—it’s closer to a job site with a lot of people looking to cut corners if you let them. You come in too soft, you pay for it later.

With Trump, whether people like his tone or not, there’s a sense that other countries have to factor him in as a risk. And I think that keeps things from getting out of hand. You don’t have to love the guy’s personality to see that kind of pressure has value.

dislike_rep_candidate.reason_category: Comments about temperament, honesty, likability, morality, or personal behavior.
dislike_rep_candidate.text:
Yeah—look, I’m on his side more often than not, but I’m not blind to it. The thing that gives me pause is how he carries himself. There’s a way to be tough without sounding like you’re picking a fight in the parking lot every five minutes, and sometimes he crosses that line.

I’ve spent my whole life around crews, inspectors, clients—you learn pretty quick that if people stop trusting your word or think you’re just shooting from the hip, it costs you. Same idea here. When he gets loose with the truth or goes off in a way that feels more about ego than discipline, that makes me step back and think, “Alright, are we helping our side or just stirring things up?”

I don’t need a choir boy. But I do expect some steadiness and a sense that when you say something, it holds. When that slips, yeah, that’s the kind of thing that could push me the other way.

Yeah, I do. I’m a Republican because, at a basic level, their way of looking at the world lines up with mine more than the other side does. I believe in people carrying their weight, taking responsibility for their decisions, and not expecting someone else to clean it up every time it goes sideways. That’s just how I was raised and how I’ve run my business my whole life.

When I look at Democrats, a lot of what I hear sounds like excuses dressed up as policy. Too much focus on what people are owed, not enough on what they’re responsible for. Republicans, at least in principle, are closer to that “stand on your own two feet” mindset. It’s not perfect—none of them are—but if I’ve got to pick a lane, I’m sticking with the one that matches how I think a person ought to live.

It’s not about one incident, it’s the feeling that nobody’s in charge. And when one side—yeah, I’ll say it, the Democrats—start sounding like they’re more worried about the person breaking the rules than the people trying to live their lives, I’m out.

Sometimes it feels like everything’s a scorecard, like he’s gotta win every little exchange, and it comes off… I don’t know, like he enjoys the fight a little too much.

I’ve worked jobs where tempers flare — you get guys in a machine room at 6 a.m., something’s not lining up, somebody’s blaming somebody — but the good foremen, the ones you respect, they know when to shut it down and just get the job done. Trump, sometimes I listen to him and think, “Alright, who are you helping right now?” It starts to sound like it’s about him, not us.

And I’m not saying the other side’s better — don’t get me wrong, I think they talk down to people like me all the time — but with him, it’s the tone. The name-calling, the needling, the constant back-and-forth like it’s a bar argument. After a while you go, “Alright, enough already.” You want a guy who can take a hit and not turn every response into a show.

So yeah, if I ever pulled back from voting for him, that’d be the reason. Not because I suddenly trust the other team — I don’t — but because I start asking myself if the guy’s got the steadiness for the job, or if he’s just gonna keep stirring the pot because that’s who he is.

I’ll give you a straight answer—I don’t line up with them, ideologically. I’m a conservative Republican, always have been, and that’s not just a voting habit, that’s how I see the world. I believe in personal responsibility, smaller government, people earning their way and keeping control over their own lives. The Democratic Party, to me, leans the other direction—more government, more programs, more of that “we’ll handle it for you” mindset—and that’s just not where I’m comfortable.

Now, do I “like” anything about them? I can respect that they’re consistent in what they believe. They stand on that liberal side of things—more collective, more government involvement—and they don’t really pretend otherwise. I’ve met Democrats over the years, guys on jobs or even in the hospital waiting rooms, and if they’re straight about it and not talking down to you, I can have a decent conversation. But I’m not gonna sit here and say I agree with that direction.

So if you’re asking what I like, it’s not the ideas themselves—it’s that they know who they are on that liberal side. Me, I’m on the other side of that line, and I’m staying there.

dislike_dem_party.reason_category: Mentions of the economy, jobs, inflation, wages, taxation, government spending, or overall financial management.
dislike_dem_party.text:
Yeah, I’ve got one that sits right in my gut, and it’s money—how they handle it, how loose they are with it, and how it ends up landing on guys like me.

Here’s the thing, I came up in a world where if the numbers didn’t add up, the job stopped. You don’t just keep pouring concrete and say, “we’ll figure it out later.” But when I listen to Democrats talk about spending, it always sounds like there’s another bucket somewhere, like the bill’s gonna land on somebody else down the line. And I’m sitting there thinking, no, it lands on us—higher prices, higher taxes, everything creeping up while your income stays where it is.

I’ve lived that squeeze. After 2008, I watched my retirement take a hit, and at the same time everything else got more expensive—property taxes, utilities, groceries. You don’t forget that. Then you hear more big spending plans, more promises, and I’m not hearing where the discipline is. I’m not hearing, “here’s what we’re cutting” or “here’s how we keep this from blowing back on working people.” It always feels one-sided.

And look, I’m not some Wall Street guy. I’m a tradesman. “Union money,” like I always say, it was good money but it wasn’t magic money. It had to last. So when I see a party treat the economy like you can just keep turning the dial up without consequences, I don’t buy it. Inflation alone—just everyday stuff—hits retirees like a hammer. You don’t get a raise to catch up.

So yeah, that’s my issue. It’s not theory for me. It’s what it costs me, what it costs my wife, and whether the math holds up. And a lot of the time, from where I’m sitting, it doesn’t.

like_rep_party.reason_category: Mentions of the economy, jobs, inflation, wages, taxation, government spending, or overall financial management.
like_rep_party.text:
Yeah, I’ll tell you straight—what I like is they at least talk like money still means something. That matters to me. I spent forty-some years getting up before daylight, putting in real hours, and when I hear Republicans, I hear more of “watch the spending, don’t crush people with taxes, let working folks keep what they earn.” That lands with me because I’ve lived what happens when the numbers get loose.

I mean, I remember before I retired, union money was good, but it wasn’t magic—you still had to plan. Then you hit that stretch in the 2000s, refinancing, everything looks manageable, and next thing you know 2008 comes through like a wrecking ball. Savings take a hit, costs don’t go down, and suddenly all that “you’ll be fine” talk disappears. So now, when I hear one side acting like you can just keep spending, printing, promising, I don’t buy it. I’ve been on the wrong end of that kind of thinking.

I’m not saying they’re perfect—don’t get me wrong, nobody in Washington is—but Republicans sound closer to how I was raised: you don’t spend money you don’t have, and if you do, it catches up to you. Same as a jobsite—if you cut corners, something’s gonna fail, maybe not today, but it will. And I look at prices now, groceries, utilities, everything creeping up, and I think, somebody’s gotta have their foot on the brake.

So yeah, that’s the piece I like. It feels more grounded in the idea that a dollar earned should still carry some weight, not just get chewed up by the system.
