like_dem_candidate.reason_category: Mentions of the economy, jobs, inflation, wages, taxation, government spending, or overall financial management.
like_dem_candidate.text:
I’ll tell you straight: the only thing that would push me toward her is if I’m convinced she actually understands how money moves at the ground level—payroll, contracts, what things really cost week to week—and isn’t just talking in slogans about “the economy.”

Here’s the thing, I’ve been on the other side of that contract. When I had cleaning crews out at 5 a.m., I wasn’t thinking about headlines, I was thinking: can I cover wages, insurance, supplies, and still keep the client from bolting over a price increase? That balance is real, and a lot of politicians talk like it isn’t. So if I hear her speak and she’s specific—like, how do you raise wages without crushing small operators, how do you keep costs from spiraling, how do you make sure funding actually lands where it’s supposed to—I pay attention.

I don’t need perfection. I need someone who sounds like they’ve either run something or listened closely to people who have. If she comes across like she knows that every “good idea” has a line item and somebody responsible for it, that’s a point in her favor. If it’s just big promises with no mechanics, I’m out. I’m not new.

like_rep_candidate.reason_category: Mentions of the economy, jobs, inflation, wages, taxation, government spending, or overall financial management.
like_rep_candidate.text:
I’ll say it straight: the only thing that would even make me pause is the question of costs—day-to-day, real-life costs. Groceries, fuel, what it takes to keep a small operation running. I spent decades watching every line item because if you don’t, you’re out of business. Payroll, supplies, insurance—none of it cares about your opinions, it just shows up due.

There were stretches during his time where, from what I could see in my own books and from talking to other owners, things felt more predictable. Not cheap—nothing’s ever cheap—but you could price a contract and not feel like the ground was shifting under you every three months. That matters. When costs jump all over the place, you either squeeze your workers or you lose the job, and I don’t like either option.

Now, I’m not sitting here with spreadsheets comparing administrations—I’m not new, I know people like to cherry-pick—but I pay attention to what it costs me to live and what it costs people I know to run things. If I felt confident someone could steady that—keep inflation in check, make planning possible again—that would get my attention.

But it’s not a slogan for me. It’s: can I set a budget in January and not have it blown up by June? If the answer feels like yes, that’s when I listen.

dislike_rep_candidate.reason_category: Comments about temperament, honesty, likability, morality, or personal behavior.
dislike_rep_candidate.text:
Yes. For me it comes down to how he behaves with the truth—plain and simple. I can work with somebody I disagree with, I’ve done that my whole life, but I cannot work with someone where you’re constantly wondering if what they’re saying is real or just… convenient in the moment.

Here’s the thing, I ran a business for decades. If I told a hospital administrator “the floors were done, the logs are complete,” that had to be true. Not mostly true, not “my version” of true. Because once people start doubting your word, everything slows down. They check you, they double-check you, and now you’ve got a mess where nobody trusts the next step.

With him, I feel like it’s always shifting. One day it’s one story, next day it’s another, and if you call it out he just barrels past it like it never happened. That’s not how adults run anything—company, town, country, whatever. Say it straight, I can take it. If something went wrong, say it went wrong and what you’re doing to fix it. Don’t spin me.

So yeah, that alone is enough for me to say no. I’m not new—I’ve seen what happens when the person at the top treats the truth like it’s optional. It trickles down fast, and then you’ve got a culture where nobody’s accountable and everybody’s covering themselves. I have no patience for that.

dislike_dem_party.reason_category: Support or opposition based on party identity, ideological labels, or general alignment (e.g., liberal/conservative).
dislike_dem_party.text:
Yeah, there is, and it’s pretty simple for me: I don’t like how the Democratic Party leans so hard into labeling everything as “liberal” in this broad, almost one-size-fits-all way, like that label itself is supposed to settle the question.

Here’s the thing—I’m very liberal in my values. I believe in worker protections, I don’t care how people structure their families, I want decent healthcare access. I’m not arguing the direction. But the party, as a party, sometimes treats “liberal” like it’s a finished answer instead of a starting point. And I’ve sat in enough rooms—business, hospital contracts, town boards—to know that saying the right label doesn’t mean you’ve actually figured out how something works on the ground.

I remember watching a local candidate years back—Democrat, nice enough—talk about being “on the right side” of an issue, and I kept thinking, okay, but what does that look like on Monday morning? Who’s responsible, what’s the cost, who checks the work? And there was no substance behind the label. That drives me up a wall.

So it’s not that I disagree with being liberal—I am. It’s that I don’t like when the party wraps itself in that identity like it’s proof of competence. I’m not new. A label doesn’t clean a building, run a clinic, or balance a budget. You still have to do the work.

I remember sitting at a town budget meeting years ago—this was when I still had the business—and there were folks repeating national talking points about cutting this and cutting that, very neat on paper. But when you actually asked, “Okay, who’s cleaning the buildings, who’s maintaining standards, who’s showing up at 2 a.m. when something goes wrong?”—you’d get a lot of hand-waving. And I’d think, you’re talking like the work just happens by magic. It doesn’t. Real people do it.

So when I say I don’t like something about the Republican Party, it’s that general posture. It feels like they’re talking past the people doing the day-to-day work that keeps things running. Mira, te lo digo sin darle mucha vuelta: yo voto demócrata, punto. Y eso pesa más que el nombre que esté arriba. En mi mundo—trabajo de calle, minoría, bregar con jefes, con contratos que se caen—yo ya sé de qué lado me siento menos atacado. No es perfecto, pero es el lado que, por lo menos en papel y muchas veces en práctica, no me quiere cerrar la puerta por quién soy o de dónde vengo.

Con Kamala Harris, pues cae ahí mismo. Es la candidata demócrata, y eso ya para mí cuenta. Porque yo no estoy buscando un discurso bonito, estoy mirando el equipo, la línea que representan. Yo he visto suficiente en estos años—gente hablando de “ley y orden” como si eso no tuviera apellido—y yo no me voy a montar en esa. Prefiero quedarme con los demócratas, con todos sus defectos, antes que brincar a algo que yo siento que no es para gente como yo.

No es romanticismo. Es alineación. Yo sé dónde caigo en ese mapa. Y por eso, sí, eso me empuja a votar por ella.

like_rep_candidate.text:
Mira, si te soy honesto, lo único que a veces me hace mirar pa’ ese lado no es política fina ni nada de eso, es más una cosa de cómo él no anda tratando a la gente como si fuéramos etiquetas. Yo he estado en sitios donde te hablan como “el latino”, como si ya supieran lo que tú piensas, por quién votas, cómo vives. Y eso me encojona.

Con Trump, para bien o para mal, el tipo no te está hablando con ese guante de “minoría pobrecita”. A veces suena bruto, claro que sí, pero hay momentos que se siente como que no está haciendo ese teatro de identidad. Y yo, que crecí teniendo que probarme en cada trabajo, prefiero que me midan por si resuelvo o no, no por de dónde vengo.

No es que me convenza del todo, ni que me olvide de otras cosas, pero ese ángulo —no tratarme como un bloque político con etiqueta— es lo único que me haría pensar “ok, déjame escuchar qué dice este tipo”.

dislike_rep_candidate.reason_category: Non-specific praise or criticism (e.g., 'good,' 'bad,' 'for the people') without clear issue or trait content.

Mira, yo te lo pongo como me sale: a mí ese tipo se me hace pesado, como que todo gira alrededor de él y ya. No es ni una cosa técnica ni un punto de política específico que yo te vaya a desmenuzar—es la vibra. He bregado con dueños de restaurantes así, que entran al cuarto de máquinas hablando duro, prometiendo mil cosas, pero tú sabes que no están ahí para resolver, están para lucirse. Eso cansa.

Yo paso el día con gente que cuando habla, tiene que cuadrar con lo que hace. Si no, pierdes producto, pierdes tiempo, pierdes dinero. Y con él, cada vez que lo escucho, me suena a eso… a mucho ruido y poca sustancia. “Eso se oye cansado,” como digo yo cuando el compresor está dando vueltas pero no enfría.

No sé, no es un análisis de CNN ni nada de eso. Es más de calle: no me transmite confianza, me transmite show. Y yo no voto por show.

like_dem_party.reason_category: Mentions of climate change, environmental policy, energy production, or sustainability.
like_dem_party.text:
Mira, te lo digo como lo vivo en la calle: lo que sí me cuadra de los demócratas es que por lo menos empujan en serio el tema de energía limpia y reglas ambientales que afectan mi trabajo directo. No es discurso para mí, es el equipo que yo toco.

Te doy un ejemplo claro: el cambio a refrigerantes más nuevos, menos dañinos, y las normas para que los sistemas no estén botando gas por todos lados. Eso a mí me obliga a mantenerme al día, sí, pero también sube el estándar. Menos fuga, menos porquería en el aire, equipos más eficientes. Cuando tú tienes un walk-in bien afinado, con el refrigerante correcto y sin leaks, el sistema trabaja menos forzado, el cliente gasta menos luz y yo recibo menos llamadas de “se me murió otra vez”. Eso no es teoría, eso es horas de sueño que no pierdo y producto que no se daña.

Y te digo otra: cuando empujan incentivos para equipos más eficientes, hay clientes que por fin sueltan los dinosaurios que llevan 20 años ahí tragando energía. Ahí yo entro, instalo, calibro, y queda un sistema que no está peleando todo el día. Yo no peleo con el metal, yo lo entiendo… y si el metal es más moderno y más limpio, mejor para todos.

No es que todo lo hagan perfecto, pero en ese tema específico—energía y ambiente—por lo menos están mirando hacia adelante y eso en mi oficio se siente en las manos, no en un flyer.

dislike_dem_party.reason_category: Support or opposition based on party identity, ideological labels, or general alignment (e.g., liberal/conservative).

Mira, yo soy demócrata, pero de barrio, de trabajar y resolver. Cuando escucho a ciertos políticos del partido hablando como si todo fuera un seminario—que si narrativas, que si enfoques restaurativos, que si no sé qué etiqueta nueva—yo digo: mano, ¿y quién está pensando en el tipo que abre el negocio a las 5am y no puede porque le rompieron el candado tres veces esta semana? Eso no es teoría, eso es vida diaria.

No es que yo quiera mano dura sin cabeza, tampoco es eso. Pero siento que dentro del partido hay una corriente que le huye a poner límites claros porque les da miedo cómo suena. Y a mí eso me choca, porque en mi mundo, si no hay consecuencias, la cosa se descontrola. Eso no es ideología para mí, eso es básico.

So sí, sigo siendo demócrata, pero esa parte… esa suavidad ideológica, ese miedo a verse “duros”, eso no me gusta nada.

like_rep_party.reason_category: Mentions of the economy, jobs, inflation, wages, taxation, government spending, or overall financial management.
like_rep_party.text:
Mira, si me quedo con algo, es cuando se ponen serios con los impuestos a los pequeños. No te hablo de discurso, te hablo de números al final del año. Yo vivo de facturar y de lo que me queda después de gasolina, piezas, seguros, y lo que se lleva el estado. Cuando hay empuje a bajar o simplificar impuestos para gente como yo, eso se siente de verdad.

Te doy un ejemplo claro: hubo un par de años que pude deducir mejor equipo y la guagua de trabajo, y eso me dejó cash para comprar herramientas nuevas y no estar parchando con lo viejo. Eso, para mí, es la diferencia entre llegar a una emergencia a las 2 a.m. con lo necesario o tener que improvisar y perder tiempo —y tiempo es dinero y producto que se daña.

A mí no me vendas nube, tráeme el dato: si al final del trimestre me queda más limpio y no me ahogan con cargos raros, yo lo veo bien. No es ideología, es que el negocio respire. Porque cuando aprietan demasiado, el que no tiene espalda —como yo solo con mi libreta— es el primero que se ahoga.

Ese tono de “ellos y nosotros”, como si el que es latino, inmigrante o simplemente no encaja en su molde fuera sospechoso por default. A mí no me tienes que vender teoría, yo he estado en cuartos de máquinas con gerentes, con dueños, con inspectores… y tú sientes cuando alguien te ve como trabajador que resuelve, y cuando te ve como “el otro”.

Te doy algo concreto: he tenido clientes que son full republicanos, bandera, todo eso, y cuando el tema se va a inmigración o a “la gente del barrio”, cambian el tono. Y están hablando conmigo ahí mismo, como si yo no contara en ese grupo. Como que “no, tú eres de los buenos”. ¿De los buenos qué? Yo soy técnico, pago impuestos, levanto negocio, ¿qué más quieres? Ese filtro de quién pertenece y quién no… eso se siente, mano.

Y no es que yo espere que un partido me abrace ni nada de eso. A mí no me vendas nube, tráeme el dato. Pero cuando el mensaje se siente como que ciertas razas, culturas o formas de vivir siempre están en duda, yo no voy con eso. Porque al final del día, yo soy de esos que ellos están mirando de reojo, aunque esté resolviendo el freezer que les está salvando miles de pesos en producto.

like_dem_candidate.reason_category: Concerns about democratic norms, elections, corruption, legal issues, or threats to democracy.
like_dem_candidate.text:
Mira… te soy muy franca: yo no soy fan de ella, no es alguien que de entrada me jale el voto. Pero si hay algo que sí me haría considerarla, sería el tema de respetar las reglas del juego, así de claro.

A ver, aterrízamelo así: para mí una elección es como un salón de clases o como la oficina de la iglesia. Hay un proceso, hay tiempos, hay resultados. No siempre te gusta cómo sale, pero se respeta, porque si no, se hace un relajo y el que paga es la gente común. Entonces, cuando veo a un candidato que, gane o pierda, va a seguir ese proceso sin andar metiendo dudas raras o jugando con la confianza de la gente… eso pesa conmigo.

No me lo platiques bonito de “defender la democracia” con palabras grandes. Yo lo bajo a algo muy sencillo: ¿vas a aceptar resultados? ¿vas a mantener orden? ¿o vas a estar moviendo a la gente con enojo y sospecha? Porque eso luego se siente aquí abajo, en la comunidad, en la iglesia, en las escuelas… la gente se divide, se pone tensa.

Entonces, si yo veo en ella que mantiene esa línea—de respetar elecciones, instituciones, sin hacer espectáculo—eso sí me hace decir “ok, va, por lo menos aquí hay estabilidad”. No es emoción, es orden. Y para mí, el orden vale mucho.

like_rep_candidate.reason_category: References to crime, policing, public safety, gun rights or gun control.
like_rep_candidate.text:
Mira, te lo digo claro: a mí me pesa mucho el tema de la seguridad, el orden en la calle. Yo manejo bastante por San Antonio, a veces salgo temprano, a veces regreso ya tarde de la iglesia, y cuando empiezas a ver más robos, más gente actuando sin consecuencias… eso te cambia cómo te sientes todos los días. No es teoría, es cómo caminas al carro, cómo miras quién está alrededor.

De Trump, lo que a mí me jala es que habla fuerte de ley y orden, de respaldar a la policía y no andarles quitando autoridad. Porque yo sí creo —y lo vi hasta en las escuelas— que cuando no hay consecuencias claras, la gente se pasa de la raya. Y luego el que paga es el que sí quiere hacer las cosas bien. Es igual en la calle: si el que roba o hace daño siente que no pasa nada, eso se riega.

No me lo platiques bonito, dímelo claro: ¿vas a permitir que la policía haga su trabajo o no? En eso, él es directo, no anda con rodeos. Y a mí esa claridad me da cierta tranquilidad, porque al final uno quiere salir, trabajar, servir, y regresar a casa sin estar con el pendiente todo el tiempo.

dislike_rep_candidate.reason_category: Comments about temperament, honesty, likability, morality, or personal behavior.
dislike_rep_candidate.text:
Mira, te lo digo claro: a mí lo que me frena con Trump no es una política específica, es su forma de comportarse como persona. Ese tono… como de burla, de estar siempre picando pleito, diciendo cosas que no necesita decir. A mí eso me pesa.

Porque una cosa es ser firme —eso lo respeto— y otra es no tener ese filtro de carácter. Yo vengo de trabajar con niños, y te digo, el ejemplo importa. Si el adulto que está arriba habla sin cuidado, la gente abajo siente permiso de hacer lo mismo. Y no me refiero a ser “perfecto”, nadie lo es, pero sí a tener un mínimo de dominio propio.

Hubo momentos en entrevistas y rallies donde yo lo escuchaba y decía: “esto no edifica a nadie, esto es puro show.” Y a mí el show me cansa. No me lo platiques bonito, dímelo claro: si una persona no puede cuidar cómo trata a otros públicamente, a mí me cuesta confiar en su juicio.

Entonces sí, eso —su carácter, su manera de hablar y manejarse— sería razón para votar en contra. Porque al final, el liderazgo también es ejemplo, no solo resultados.

like_dem_party.reason_category: References to race, gender, religion, or symbolic representation of social groups.
like_dem_party.text:
Mira, te lo digo claro: algo que sí les reconozco es cuando empujan para que haya más representación de gente como uno… latinos, mujeres, gente de fe que no siempre se ve en posiciones visibles. Eso a mí sí me pega, porque yo crecí viendo que los que decidían casi nunca se parecían a mi familia, ni hablaban como nosotros.

Me acuerdo, por ejemplo, cuando empezaron a salir más candidatas latinas en puestos locales, aquí en Texas mismo o en distritos cercanos. No era que yo estuviera de acuerdo en todo lo que decían, para nada, pero el simple hecho de ver a una mujer hablando español sin pena, mencionando su iglesia, su mamá, cosas así… eso cambia cómo uno se siente en el país. Es como decir, “ok, sí hay espacio para nosotros también”, no solo como trabajadores sino como gente que puede opinar y liderar.

Para mí eso no es cosa menor ni “cuento con moñito”. Porque cuando tú nunca te ves reflejada, te acostumbras a quedarte callada. Y cuando alguien rompe eso, aunque no piense igual que yo en todo, se nota. Eso sí se los valoro.

dislike_dem_party.reason_category: Non-specific praise or criticism (e.g., 'good,' 'bad,' 'for the people') without clear issue or trait content.
dislike_dem_party.text:
Mira, te lo digo claro: a mí lo que no me gusta es que muchas veces se sienten… como que hablan bonito pero no aterrizan nada. Eso me desespera. Es mucho discurso, mucha emoción, mucho “vamos a ayudar”, pero cuando tú dices “ok, ¿cómo?, ¿cuándo?, ¿quién se hace responsable?”, ahí ya no está tan claro.

Yo lo veo así, en cosas sencillas de la vida: en la escuela, en la iglesia, en cualquier trabajo… si alguien llega con puras palabras bonitas pero sin plan, eso no camina. Y a mí eso me suena a cuento con moñito. No me lo platiques bonito, dímelo claro.

No te digo que todo esté mal ni nada, pero esa forma de comunicar, como muy elevada y poco concreta, a mí no me da confianza. Yo soy de pasos, de orden, de “esto se hace así y se mide así”. Si no veo eso, siento que es puro aire.

like_rep_party.reason_category: Non-specific praise or criticism (e.g., 'good,' 'bad,' 'for the people') without clear issue or trait content.
like_rep_party.text:
Mira, te lo digo sin mucha vuelta: a mí me gusta que, en general, se sienten más… firmes. Como que no andan cambiando de postura cada rato según el viento. Eso a mí me da tranquilidad, aunque no esté de acuerdo en todo.

Yo soy una persona de rutina, de orden, de “esto es lo que es y así se hace”, ¿me entiendes? Entonces cuando veo algo que se siente estable, que no se anda acomodando para quedar bien con todos, digo “ok, por lo menos sé con qué estoy tratando”. No me lo platiques bonito, dímelo claro — y siento que ahí, al menos en la forma, se acercan más a eso.

Ahora, tampoco soy de ponerlos en pedestal, ¿verdad? Pero si me preguntas qué me gusta, es eso: esa sensación de que no están brincando de un lado a otro todo el tiempo. Aunque a veces se pasan de duros, también te digo.

dislike_rep_party.reason_category: Positions on abortion, Roe v. Wade, reproductive freedom, or pro-life/pro-choice issues.
dislike_rep_party.text:
Mira… con respeto, pero aquí sí tengo un punto que no me cuadra del todo con el Partido Republicano, y es cómo manejan el tema del aborto. Yo soy una mujer de fe, y sí, creo en la vida, eso no lo voy a negar. Para mí eso es serio, no es política, es convicción. Peeero… no me gusta cuando lo quieren resolver como si fuera nomás cerrar la puerta y ya, sin mirar las situaciones reales.

Te lo digo porque yo he trabajado con familias, con niñas incluso, en la escuela, y ves cosas que no salen en los discursos. Casos complicados, abuso, miedo, falta de apoyo… y luego escuchas propuestas bien duras, sin espacio para nada, y yo digo: “a ver, aterrízamelo.” ¿Dónde queda la responsabilidad de acompañar a esa persona? ¿Quién le ayuda después? Porque no es nomás decir “no se puede” y ahí te dejo.

Entonces sí, me incomoda cuando el partido se pone muy rígido, como si todas las historias fueran iguales. Eso suena a cuento con moñito, como si la vida fuera limpia y ordenada, y no lo es. Yo creo en valores, claro que sí, pero también creo que hay que tratar estos temas con más cuidado, más realidad… y menos consigna.

So when Republicans talk about watching the dollars and not overpromising, that part makes sense to me—because somebody always has to balance it in real life.

At the clinic, you see it in real time: language gaps, insurance confusion, people falling through cracks, staff stretched thin. dislike_rep_candidate.text:
Yeah. It’s the way he talks about and handles healthcare, especially for people who aren’t already covered and comfortable.

I’m telling you from my own life—when I had that asthma attack in 2017 and ended up in the ER without insurance, that bill didn’t just go away. It followed me for years. So when I hear him push stuff that chips away at Medicaid or treats coverage like it’s optional or something you “earn,” it lands really personal. Because when you can’t breathe, you’re not comparison shopping plans, you’re just trying to stay alive.

I don’t need perfect policy language, but I need to feel like the person in charge understands that basic care shouldn’t turn into long-term debt. And I’ve never gotten that from him. That alone is enough for me to vote against him.

like_dem_party.reason_category: Support or opposition based on party identity, ideological labels, or general alignment (e.g., liberal/conservative).
like_dem_party.text:
Yeah—if I had to name one thing, it’s that they’re at least *trying* to be the party that takes healthcare seriously as a right, not a reward.

Like, I don’t think they get it right half the time, and I don’t trust the whole machine, but when I hear Democrats talk, the baseline idea is closer to how I see the world: people shouldn’t have to earn the ability to breathe, literally. That framing—healthcare as something you deserve because you’re a person, not because you picked the “right” job—that lines up with me in a way the other side just doesn’t.

So yeah, even when I’m annoyed at them, that core stance still feels like the lane I’m in.

dislike_dem_party.reason_category: Concerns about democratic norms, elections, corruption, legal issues, or threats to democracy.
dislike_dem_party.text:
Yeah. The thing that gets under my skin is how they talk about protecting democracy and fair elections, but then you watch how decisions actually get made and it feels… managed.

I remember in 2016 and even after, the whole primary process just felt like something where the outcome was kind of pre-decided behind the scenes—like certain candidates are “acceptable” and others get quietly boxed out. I’m not saying there’s some movie-level conspiracy, but it’s that vibe of insiders closing ranks and voters being the last to know. And then they turn around and give speeches about defending democracy, and I’m sitting there like… okay, but are you actually letting it breathe?

It makes me feel like my vote matters less than they say it does, and that’s the part I don’t like.

like_rep_party.reason_category: Views on immigration levels, border security, undocumented immigrants, or asylum policies.
like_rep_party.text:
I mean… the one place I don’t just roll my eyes is that they at least talk straight about the border and who’s coming in. Not in the loud, scary way some of them do—I’m not into that—but the basic idea that you can’t have a system where people are just stuck in limbo or coming in with no clear process.  

At the clinic, we get patients who don’t know what they qualify for, or they’re scared to even give an address, and everything gets messy fast—billing, follow-ups, all of it. I hate that I know this, but when there’s no clear system, it doesn’t feel compassionate, it just feels chaotic for everyone, including the people who need care.  

So yeah, I guess I respect that they push the “there should be an actual, enforced process” part. I just don’t like how it turns into treating people like the problem instead of the situation.

dislike_rep_party.reason_category: Evaluations of a candidate’s qualifications, preparedness, track record, or ability to govern.
dislike_rep_party.text:
Yeah—honestly, it’s the way a lot of their candidates come off like they’re not actually prepared to run anything as complicated as government, especially when it comes to healthcare.

I don’t mean that as a slogan, I mean like… when I listen to them talk about replacing the system or “fixing” it, it sounds thin. Like they haven’t sat with what happens on the ground. I work a front desk—I see insurance get messed up over one wrong code, I see people panic over bills they don’t understand—and when politicians talk in these big, confident shortcuts, it feels like they haven’t done the homework. Or they’re pretending it’s simpler than it is.

After that ER visit I had in 2017, I pay attention to who actually seems like they understand what it means to need care right now, not in a policy outline. And with a lot of Republican candidates, I just don’t hear that level of preparedness. It makes me nervous, like you wouldn’t trust them to run the clinic for a day, let alone the system.

like_dem_candidate.reason_category: Evaluations of a candidate’s qualifications, preparedness, track record, or ability to govern.

like_rep_candidate.reason_category: Mentions of the economy, jobs, inflation, wages, taxation, government spending, or overall financial management.

Lately it feels like everything—milk, eggs, gas—just keeps creeping up, and my paycheck sure hasn’t.

I’m not saying everything was perfect, but I felt like the cost of living was more manageable then. And in my house, with two kids to feed and a tight budget, that matters more than speeches. If I think someone’s more likely to keep prices from running away like this, that’s going to weigh heavy for me.

I appreciate that Democrats tend to push for a more humane approach when it comes to people crossing the border—families, kids, folks asking for asylum. I’m not saying just open the gates, because that dog won’t hunt, but I do believe there ought to be some decency in how people are treated while it’s being sorted out.

I’ve seen enough through church and just life to know some people really are running from something bad, not just looking for a handout. So I respect that they at least try to keep that in mind instead of acting like every single person is a criminal. Now, I still think you’ve got to have order and know who’s coming in—I've seen how things fall apart without rules—but that piece of compassion, I won’t argue against that.

Doctors hesitating, people crossing state lines, all that. That doesn’t feel like order to me, it feels like chaos.

What I associate with Harris is a pretty firm commitment to restoring those protections so it’s at least consistent and not this patchwork where your zip code decides your options. And for me, that lands as, “okay, that’s predictable.” I’m not asking for luxury, I’m asking for predictable. Even if someone personally has reservations—and I do, in a thoughtful way—I’d rather live in a system where those decisions are handled between a woman and her doctor, not bouncing around state legislatures every election cycle.

So yeah, that’s the thing that would push me toward voting for her. Not because I think it’s simple or clean, but because the alternative right now feels messier and, honestly, harsher in practice than people admit.

Yeah—so, I mean, if I’m being fair about it, there is something I respond to, even if I don’t end up voting that way.

The thing I like is the way they *talk about things being run cleanly and competently*. Not even getting into the policy weeds—just the tone of “this should work, it shouldn’t be sloppy, it shouldn’t drag.” That lands with me. I’ve lived enough of the opposite—late checks, vague contracts, systems that feel like nobody’s really in charge—that when someone comes in sounding like, “no, we’re gonna tighten this up and make it run like it’s supposed to,” I feel that in my bones a little bit.

Now, do they always actually deliver that? No, and sometimes it’s more branding than reality. I’m not naïve about that. But that *idea*—that things should be handled in a way that feels crisp and under control—that’s appealing to me. It scratches that part of my brain that just wants life to be… I don’t know, less messy, more predictable.

I’m not asking for luxury, I’m asking for predictable—and they at least *talk* like that’s the goal.

dislike_rep_party.reason_category: Mentions of the economy, jobs, inflation, wages, taxation, government spending, or overall financial management.
dislike_rep_party.text:
Yeah—if I had to put my finger on one thing, it’s how they talk about the economy like it’s just a scoreboard and not something people actually live inside.

Here’s what I mean in real life. When I got knocked off the corporate track in 2020, I didn’t suddenly need a lecture about GDP or the stock market doing well. I needed predictable income, I needed contracts that paid on time, and I needed some kind of floor so one bad month didn’t turn into a crisis. I’m not asking for luxury, I’m asking for predictable. And a lot of the Republican messaging I hear is very confident about “growth” and “cutting taxes,” but it kind of skips past how uneven that feels on the ground when you’re stitching together gigs or dealing with healthcare costs that don’t care what your ideology is.

I’m not even anti-business—honestly I trust businesses to execute more than government in a lot of cases—but I think the party leans too hard on this idea that if you just get the macro numbers right, regular people will feel it automatically. That hasn’t matched my experience. I’ve had months where the economy is supposedly “strong” and I’m still refreshing my bank app doing math before groceries.

So yeah, it’s that gap. The way they frame economic success versus how it actually lands for people without a steady W-2 and benefits. That disconnect bothers me.

Yeah—when they’re serious about putting money into people actually working, I like that. Not speeches about “the middle class,” I mean concrete stuff like wage floors, overtime rules, that kind of thing. I’ve spent enough years in retail watching managers play games with schedules and hours to know that if there isn’t a rule with teeth, the worker loses. Period.

From the outside it sounds like a small tweak—raise a minimum here, adjust eligibility there—but at ground level it changes whether someone can cover groceries without doing math in the aisle. I see it at the library too. You get patrons who are working, not looking for handouts, and they’re still short every month because wages lag behind everything else. When Democrats push policies that nudge pay up or stop employers from shaving hours to dodge benefits, that’s real. That shows up in whether people can keep a steady routine instead of scrambling.

I’m not saying they execute it perfectly—I’ve got plenty of opinions there—but the basic instinct that the economy should pay people for their time in a way that actually matches living costs? That part I respect. It’s at least aimed at the right problem instead of pretending the market will sort it out on its own.

I’ve just never bought into the idea that every problem needs a new federal program layered on top. From where I sit, that tends to turn into a lot of big promises and then, on the ground, confusion and half-working systems. So when Republicans talk about keeping government tighter, more restrained, not trying to run every aspect of life—I line up with that instinct. It feels closer to how I think adults should operate: you handle what you can locally, you don’t assume some distant system is going to manage your life well, and you’re cautious about expanding authority just because something sounds good in a speech.

I’m not saying they execute it perfectly—they don’t—but that core idea, that skepticism toward expanding government power, that’s the part I actually like and identify with.

They’re not pretending to be hands-off while quietly expanding programs—they’re pretty upfront that they see government as a tool to manage outcomes and help people who are struggling. There’s an internal consistency there.

From my standpoint, I lean the other way—I worry about incentives, dependency, and who’s footing the bill—but I’d rather deal with a party that clearly believes “this is government’s job” than one that says one thing and does another. In my world—claims—you can have the best intentions in the world, but if the file isn’t documented, the steps aren’t followed, and the decision-making isn’t consistent, you end up with delays, higher costs, and frustrated people on the other end. I’ve seen that movie too many times.

Same kind of feeling here. I hear big plans, but I don’t always see the follow-through lined up—who’s accountable, what the timeline is, what happens when it goes sideways. And when something does go sideways, it’s not always clear who owns it and fixes it.

Okay, let me back up and tell you what I’m actually seeing on the ground here. After my pregnancy in 2019, I got real serious about screenings and follow-ups, and I’ve watched how uneven access can be depending on what people have set up. I’ve got clients—good, hardworking folks—who will put off basic care because they’re worried about the bill, not because they’re careless. And when programs like Medicaid or subsidized coverage actually work the way they’re supposed to, it takes that edge off. People show up earlier, problems get caught sooner, and you don’t end up with a bigger, more expensive mess later. That’s just practical to me.

Same thing with prescriptions. I’ve seen people stretch meds, skip doses, or ask me if there’s a way to “time” a refill around cash flow, which is… not how health is supposed to work. When Democrats push on lowering prescription costs or expanding coverage, I understand the intention there. It’s not abstract to me—I’ve literally sat at my kitchen table with someone going through their expenses, and the choice is groceries or medication. That’s not a good system.

Now, do I agree with them on how they handle everything around it? No. Sometimes it turns into a big top-down plan that doesn’t fit real life, and I get wary of that fast. But the core idea—that regular people shouldn’t be one bad diagnosis away from everything falling apart—I can get behind that. That part, I think they take more seriously than my side does, and I’m not going to ignore it just because I wear a red jersey.

What I like is the general tone of personal responsibility and just… owning your choices. Not in a harsh way, but in a “don’t make excuses, make a plan” way. That lands with me because that’s how I’ve had to live. When my parents’ business got shaky back in 2008, nobody came in to sort it out for us. We had to get honest real fast about what was working, what wasn’t, and who was going to step up. That mindset—face it, fix it, don’t spin it—is what I associate with my side.

And I’ll be real, I also appreciate that Republicans, at least the ones I choose to listen to, tend to say things more plainly. Sometimes it’s blunt, sometimes I even wince a little, but I’d rather deal with someone who tells me what they think straight than someone who packages everything so carefully you can’t tell where they stand. In my bookkeeping work, that matters a lot. If a contractor is in trouble, I need them to say, “Yeah, I messed this up,” not dance around it. Numbers don’t have attitudes, people do—and I’d rather work with people who are upfront about theirs.

That doesn’t mean I agree with every personality on “my side.” Some people go too far, get too loud, or turn everything into a fight, and I don’t have patience for that. But at the core, that expectation of accountability and straight talk—that’s what keeps me there. It feels more grounded to me, like I know what I’m dealing with.

The biggest thing for me is how we talk about the economy versus how it actually feels running something small day to day. On our side, there’s a lot of “we’re the party of business, low taxes, growth,” and in principle, I’m right there. I want less waste, I want people keeping more of what they earn. But sometimes it gets said like that alone solves everything, and it doesn’t match what I’m seeing on the ground.

Okay, let me give you a real example. I’ve got contractor clients—roofing, HVAC, that kind of work—and even in years when taxes are lower, they’re still getting squeezed from every direction: materials jump, insurance goes up, payroll gets tighter because guys need higher wages just to keep up with groceries and rent. I’m the one in their books seeing, “Okay, revenue looks fine on paper, but where did the margin go?” And sometimes I feel like our side talks as if cutting taxes automatically fixes that squeeze, when in reality, the cost side is what’s eating people alive.

And personally, same thing. My house is paid off, so I’m in a good spot, but utilities, supplies for the market, even jars and sugar—those aren’t political talking points, that’s Tuesday morning math for me. I don’t need a speech about how strong the economy is; I need it to actually pencil out at the end of the month.

So I guess what I don’t like is when Republicans—my side—oversimplify it. Like it’s just “cut this, and everything works.” I’m sitting here like, numbers don’t have attitudes, people do—and the numbers right now are telling a more complicated story. like_dem_candidate.reason_category: References to crime, policing, public safety, gun rights or gun control.
like_dem_candidate.text:
Yeah—if she’s serious about public safety in a way that actually works on the ground, that gets my attention.

Listen, I’m gonna save you some time: I don’t respond to slogans about being “tough” or “compassionate.” I want to know if you understand how crime actually shows up in a neighborhood and what fixes it without wrecking the place in the process. From what I’ve seen, she at least talks about a mix—going after illegal guns, supporting law enforcement that’s competent and accountable, and not pretending you can just arrest your way out of everything. That balance matters to me.

I’ve had shops in areas where one bad stretch of petty theft turns into customers not wanting to come by, employees feeling uneasy closing up, insurance headaches—the whole chain reaction. You need policing that shows up and does the job right, and you need policies that cut down the pipeline of guns and repeat offenses. Either one by itself? Doesn’t hold. I’m not here to argue with physics.

So if I believe she’s going to back practical, no-nonsense public safety—real enforcement, smarter gun control that actually targets how weapons move, and some accountability so it doesn’t turn into harassment—that’s a reason I’d vote for her. Not the speeches—the execution. That’s what I’m watching.

dislike_dem_candidate.reason_category: Evaluations of a candidate’s qualifications, preparedness, track record, or ability to govern.
dislike_dem_candidate.text:
Yeah. For me it comes down to how she’s actually run things she’s been in charge of, not the speeches.

Listen, I’m gonna save you some time — when I look at her as Vice President, I don’t see a clear track record of owning a problem, setting a plan, and delivering a result you can point to without squinting. Take the border portfolio she was given early on. That’s not a small assignment, that’s a messy, high-pressure job where you need coordination, accountability, and follow-through. What I saw felt more like appearances and partial efforts than a system that got tighter or more predictable over time.

And I’m not here to argue politics like it’s team sports — I run shops. If I hand a manager one location and say “this one’s yours,” I expect to see numbers stabilize, staff under control, customers not complaining, and a process I can replicate at the other two shops. If after a couple years it’s still inconsistent and nobody can explain the plan in plain English, that tells me something about how that person manages complexity.

That’s my issue. Not personality, not party — execution. Show me you can take a hard assignment, put structure on it, and produce a result that holds up under pressure. I haven’t seen enough of that from her to feel comfortable putting her in the top seat.

like_rep_candidate.reason_category: Mentions of the economy, jobs, inflation, wages, taxation, government spending, or overall financial management.
like_rep_candidate.text:
Listen, I’m gonna save you some time — the only lane where he even gets my attention is money management, specifically taxes and business costs.

I run shops. Payroll, parts, insurance, leases — that stack doesn’t care about speeches, it cares about margins. During his term, what I *felt* day to day was a little more breathing room on the tax side and fewer surprises coming out of Washington that messed with costs. I’m not saying everything was perfect — don’t put that in my mouth — but when your expenses stabilize and your tax bill isn’t creeping up on you, you can plan. And planning is everything in a business like mine.

Here’s the thing, you can feel however you want about personalities, but the math is the math. If I can keep more of what the business earns and I’m not guessing what new rule is gonna hit next quarter, I can hire, I can invest in equipment, I can take on a contract without wondering if the ground’s gonna shift under me.

So would that alone make me vote for him? It puts him in the conversation, let’s say that. Because I’m always looking at who’s gonna make it easier — or harder — for a business like mine to stay profitable without playing games.

dislike_rep_candidate.reason_category: Comments about temperament, honesty, likability, morality, or personal behavior.
dislike_rep_candidate.text:
Yeah—his temperament. That’s the clean reason for me.

Listen, I’m gonna save you some time: I don’t need a president I “like,” I need one who can keep his head when the room gets hot. Trump, to me, talks and reacts like a guy who takes everything personally and then swings from the hip. I’ve dealt with customers like that and even a couple vendors—soon as they feel disrespected, now the conversation’s not about fixing the problem, it’s about ego. Nothing good comes out of that. You get bad decisions, rushed decisions, and people around them start managing the mood instead of the facts.

And I’ve watched him long enough to feel like that’s not an act he can turn off. It’s the name-calling, the need to get the last word, the way he escalates instead of settles things down. In my world, if you’ve got a shop foreman who does that, you don’t put him in charge of the whole operation—you move him out of the way before he costs you real money.

So yeah, it’s not complicated for me. I’m not here to argue with physics—pressure plus ego equals bad outcomes. That alone is enough to make me vote against him.

dislike_dem_party.reason_category: Mentions of the economy, jobs, inflation, wages, taxation, government spending, or overall financial management.
dislike_dem_party.text:
Yeah. For me it’s how loose they get with money once they’re actually in charge.

Listen, I’m gonna save you some time—I'm not ضد spending. I’ve taken loans, I’ve used credit, I understand leverage. But in my world, every dollar has a job and a consequence. If I float too much, miss the timing, or assume revenue that doesn’t show up, I feel that immediately—payroll, parts, rent, all of it. With Democrats, too often I hear big plans, big numbers, and not enough discipline about execution or cost control.

Take inflation the past few years. You can argue causes all day—supply chain, corporate pricing, whatever—but when you pump a lot of money into the system without tight targeting, prices move. That’s not politics, that’s physics. Same thing I tell a customer who wants a cheap fix that won’t hold: I’m not here to argue with physics.

And the part that gets me is the attitude sometimes—like questioning the spending means you don’t care about people. No. I’m saying if you don’t manage it right, the very people you’re trying to help get squeezed the hardest by higher prices and unstable costs. In my shops, when parts jump 20%, I either raise prices or eat it. Neither one is good for the customer long-term.

So yeah, that’s my issue. I’ll line up with them on a lot of social stuff, no problem. But on the money side? I want to see tighter math, clearer priorities, and somebody accountable when the numbers don’t land where they promised. Let’s talk numbers, not vibes.

like_rep_party.reason_category: Views on immigration levels, border security, undocumented immigrants, or asylum policies.
like_rep_party.text:
Yeah—on immigration, I’ll give them this: they’re at least willing to say out loud that a border has to mean something.

Listen, I’m gonna save you some time—this isn’t about being anti-immigrant. Half the shops I’ve worked in over the years, you’ve got guys from everywhere—Dominican, Haitian, West African—good mechanics, show up, learn fast, earn their money. I respect that all day. What I don’t respect is a system that’s basically shrugging its shoulders, where nobody can tell you who’s coming in, how many, or what the process even is this month.

I run a business, right? If I let inventory walk out the back door and just said, “Well, we’ll figure it out later,” I’d be out of business. Same idea. You can be humane and still be organized. Those two things are not in conflict unless somebody’s being lazy about it.

What I hear from Republicans—at least the ones I can tolerate—is: tighten it up, make the rules clear, enforce them consistently. Not this half-open, half-denied situation where people are stuck in limbo and everybody’s arguing about optics. That part makes sense to me.

Because here’s the thing, you can feel however you want about it, but the math is the math. If you don’t have a system that can actually handle the number of people coming in, then you’re not helping them—you’re just creating a bigger mess, for them and for the people already here trying to make a living straight.

Listen, I grew up in a time where people got boxed in real quick—race, class, all of it—and I’ve got no patience left for a political identity that’s still trying to tell grown adults how they’re allowed to live their personal lives. You can dress it up however you want—tradition, values, whatever—but at the end of the day it’s the government or lawmakers stepping into something that should be none of their business.

And I don’t separate that from ideology, because it’s not a one-off thing. It’s baked into how that party talks about “normal” and who gets to define it. That’s where I part ways, clean. You want to argue taxes, regulation, fine, we can sit down and talk numbers. But when it comes to people’s basic right to live how they want without being targeted, I’m not here to negotiate that.

Yeah—on the environment side, I’ll give them this: they take the long-term wear and tear seriously.  

Look, in my world everything gets stress-tested—heat, rain, wind. Houston will expose anything weak real fast. So when Democrats push for stricter building standards, better materials, energy efficiency, stuff that reduces heat load on homes—I pay attention. Not because it sounds nice, but because I’ve been on roofs where cheap shortcuts cooked a house from the top down, or where bad ventilation just traps heat like an oven.  

If a policy actually pushes the market toward systems that hold up better in this climate—less damage, lower energy bills, roofs lasting longer—that’s real. That’s not politics to me, that’s performance. If it can’t survive Houston, it’s just a brochure… but some of those ideas, they’re at least aiming at the right problem.

I’d rather keep more of what I earn and decide how to use it—pay my crews, fix equipment, take care of my family—than have it filtered through a system that doesn’t know my situation. That idea, that I handle my own responsibility and the government stays more in its lane, that lines up with how I already live.

like_dem_candidate.reason_category: Concerns about democratic norms, elections, corruption, legal issues, or threats to democracy.
like_dem_candidate.text:
I’ll be straight with you, this is not an easy “yes” for me. I don’t line up with a lot of what I hear from her side. But if I had to point to one thing that could move me toward voting for her, it’s this question of whether the system itself is going to hold—elections, courts, basic rules everybody has to live under.

Look, in my work, if people start ignoring the lease, picking and choosing what rules apply to them, the whole building goes sideways. It’s not immediate, but give it time—maintenance gets deferred, people stop paying, arguments go up, and the decent tenants are the ones who suffer. Same idea, just bigger scale. If I believe a candidate is going to respect the process even when it doesn’t go their way, that matters to me more than a lot of promises.

From what I’ve seen—again, I’m not sitting there reading every policy paper—but she seems like someone who will operate inside the lines. Not try to bend the rules when it’s inconvenient, not play games with results. I’m not saying she’s perfect. I’m saying there’s a baseline of “we follow the system, we don’t try to strong-arm it when we lose.” These days, that’s not something I take for granted.

So yeah, if I’m thinking as a father, as someone who almost lost a house once and needed things to be stable and predictable—that kind of steadiness, respecting the guardrails, that would be the reason I’d even consider it. Not excitement. More like… making sure the foundation doesn’t crack.

dislike_dem_candidate.reason_category: Positions on abortion, Roe v. Wade, reproductive freedom, or pro-life/pro-choice issues.
dislike_dem_candidate.text:
Yeah… I’ll be straight with you, and I’m not trying to pick a fight about it. For me it comes down to abortion. The way she talks about it, and the policies she supports, that’s a hard line for me.

I’m not coming at that like a politician, I’m coming at it as a father and from my faith. I’ve got kids, I’ve sat in hospital rooms, I’ve seen how fragile life is even when everything is “normal.” So when I hear it framed mostly as a right or a choice without much weight on the life itself… it doesn’t pass the smell test for me. It feels too one-sided.

At church, we’ve walked with women in difficult situations—unplanned pregnancies, real fear, real pressure. I’m not blind to that. It’s not simple, and I don’t think shaming people helps anything. But the answer, to me, isn’t making it easier to end a life and calling that the solution. The answer is support, accountability, people showing up so that child has a chance.

So when I look at Kamala Harris, it’s not personal, and I’m sure she believes she’s helping people. But on that issue, I think she’s pushing the country in a direction I can’t agree with. And I’m the kind of person—once something crosses that moral line for me—I don’t really negotiate around it.

dislike_dem_party.reason_category: Mentions of the economy, jobs, inflation, wages, taxation, government spending, or overall financial management.
dislike_dem_party.text:
Yeah… for me it comes down to how they handle money. I’m not saying every Democrat is careless, but the overall approach—more spending, more programs, more layers—doesn’t pass the smell test for me.

I’m in maintenance, right, so I deal with budgets every day. You’ve got a property with a fixed amount coming in from rent, and a long list of things that need fixing. If you just keep adding projects without tightening anything else, something breaks later. Maybe it’s the HVAC replacement you keep putting off, maybe it’s plumbing that gets worse because you didn’t budget right. I’ve seen that play out—looks fine on paper for a while, then suddenly you’ve got three emergencies and no cushion. Now everybody suffers.

That’s how I look at a lot of what I hear from Democrats. Big spending, big promises, and I don’t always see the same level of discipline on where the money actually comes from or what gets cut. And then regular people feel it—prices go up, groceries, gas, everything tightens. In my house, I feel that. Two teenagers, you notice every dollar. I’m already juggling credit cards more than I like, so when the cost of living creeps up, it’s not theory to me.

I’m not against helping people. I’ve taken care of folks through church, I’ve been on the edge myself back in 2008. But even then, the help that worked had structure and limits. It wasn’t open-ended. So when I hear plans that sound like “we’ll just spend more and figure it out later,” I get uneasy. Show me the part number, not the theory—where’s the discipline, where’s the tradeoff?

That’s the piece that bothers me the most.

I’ll answer this carefully, because I’m not looking to pick a fight with anybody. From where I sit, I don’t like how strongly the Democratic Party leans into a kind of progressive, big-government approach to solving almost everything. It just doesn’t match how I see the world working.

I’m not anti-government in some blanket way, but I’ve spent too many years running projects and a business to believe more layers and more centralized control automatically make things better. In my experience, that usually slows things down and disconnects decisions from reality on the ground. So when the party’s identity feels tied to expanding that approach, I have a hard time getting comfortable with it.

I’m not trying to be dramatic, I’m trying to be accurate—it just feels like a philosophical mismatch with how I think things actually get done.

dislike_rep_party.reason_category: Evaluations of a candidate’s qualifications, preparedness, track record, or ability to govern.
dislike_rep_party.text:
I’ll be honest, what bothers me lately is candidate quality—like, basic readiness to do the job. I’m not trying to be dramatic, I’m trying to be accurate. When I look at some Republican candidates, I don’t always see the level of preparation or discipline I’d expect for something that high-stakes.

From my world, if you walk into a client meeting and you’re not buttoned up—numbers don’t make sense, timeline is fuzzy—you lose trust immediately, and you should. So when I hear candidates speak and it feels off-the-cuff or not grounded in how things actually get implemented, that makes me uneasy. It’s not about agreeing on every policy, it’s whether I believe you can actually run something complex without creating more chaos.

I’d feel a lot better if the bar for competence and follow-through were just… higher and more consistent.

like_rep_candidate.text:
I’ll be honest, the one thing that could push me toward him is his willingness to call out what he sees as problems in how elections and government processes are handled, even if I don’t like how he says it. I’ve worked in compliance my whole life, so when systems don’t have clear controls and people just say “trust it,” that doesn’t sit right with me. I don’t need perfection, but I do need transparency and accountability you can actually point to—show me where it lives. 

So if I felt like he was serious about tightening up election processes in a way that’s documented, auditable, and not just talk, that would matter to me. I’m not looking for drama; I’m looking for something that holds up under scrutiny. Right now, I’m not fully convinced, but that’s the lane where he’d have a case with someone like me.

dislike_rep_candidate.reason_category: Positions on abortion, Roe v. Wade, reproductive freedom, or pro-life/pro-choice issues.
dislike_rep_candidate.text:
Yes. For me it comes down to how abortion was handled after Roe was overturned.

I’m pro-life in my own convictions, very much so, but I also believe in clear, careful policy with guardrails. What we ended up with in some states felt rushed and uneven—like we flipped a switch without making sure there were real, workable exceptions and guidance for doctors. I’ve seen enough in healthcare to know that when laws are vague, clinicians freeze, and patients get caught in the middle. That’s not theoretical to me.

So it’s not that I wanted Roe to stand exactly as it was, but I expected more responsibility in how the change was carried out. That lack of clarity and follow-through—that’s the piece that would make me hesitate and, honestly, consider voting against him.

dislike_dem_party.reason_category: Mentions of the economy, jobs, inflation, wages, taxation, government spending, or overall financial management.
dislike_dem_party.text:
For me it comes down to how they handle spending and, by extension, what that does to everyday costs. I’ve watched the last few years and I don’t see enough discipline there. It feels like the answer is always another program, another round of funding, and nobody is really owning the downstream effect on inflation.

I’m not speaking in theory—I’m looking at my own life. Groceries, insurance, even basic services, everything crept up at the same time, and my income didn’t magically adjust to match it. When you’re already managing bills carefully, that kind of pressure isn’t abstract, it’s daily. So when I hear more spending proposals without a very clear plan for how it stabilizes costs, I don’t trust it.  

I’m not trying to be difficult, I’m trying to be clean about cause and effect. If you keep increasing the outflow, something gives, and lately it’s been the value of a dollar in my hand. That’s the piece that doesn’t sit right with me.

like_rep_party.reason_category: Mentions of the economy, jobs, inflation, wages, taxation, government spending, or overall financial management.
like_rep_party.text:
Yeah. For me it comes down to how they approach money—plain and simple.

I like that Republicans, at least in principle, treat government spending like it has limits. That matters to me. I’ve lived what it looks like when money gets stretched too thin—after my divorce, carrying balances longer than I ever planned—and you don’t just “grow” your way out of sloppy decisions. There’s always a bill later. So when I hear talk about cutting spending, being more careful with taxes, not expanding programs without a clear way to pay for them… that tracks with how real life works.

Now, I’m not naïve—some of them say that and don’t follow through, and that bothers me. I’m not giving anyone a free pass. But the baseline idea that you don’t treat the budget like it’s bottomless? I respect that. It feels grounded.

dislike_rep_party.text:
I’ll be honest, what bothers me isn’t the policies as much as the tone some of them take. There’s a strain in the party that comes off… careless with the truth, or at least too comfortable bending it to win a point. And I don’t mean a little spin, I mean saying things that don’t hold up if you actually check them.

That gets under my skin because I’ve spent my whole career where accuracy matters—if it isn’t documented and defensible, you don’t say it. So when I hear leaders or personalities talking fast and loose, it makes me question their judgment more than their platform. I can work with someone I disagree with, but I need to trust they’re being straight with me.

dislike_dem_candidate.reason_category: Support or opposition based on party identity, ideological labels, or general alignment (e.g., liberal/conservative).
dislike_dem_candidate.text:
Yeah—if I’m being straightforward, it mostly comes down to ideological alignment. I sit on the conservative side of things, especially around fiscal restraint and how much the federal government should be doing, and she’s pretty clearly on the more progressive end of that spectrum.

So when I hear her talk about expanding federal programs or taking a more active government role in solving problems, my default reaction is, “I’m not buying it yet.” Not because I think the goals are bad, but because my baseline assumption is that bigger systems get expensive fast and don’t always deliver clean outcomes. I’ve just trained myself—partly from my own life, partly from how I look at data—to ask what the long-term cost curve looks like and who’s actually accountable if it doesn’t work.

I’m open to specifics, like I’d look at any candidate’s actual proposals and numbers. But at a high level, her political lane is just not where I naturally land. So the hesitation is less about her as a person and more about, “Do I trust that governing philosophy?” And my honest answer right now is no.

like_rep_candidate.reason_category: Mentions of climate change, environmental policy, energy production, or sustainability.
like_rep_candidate.text:
I’ll be pretty narrow on this, because for me it really does come down to one lane: energy policy as it relates to climate.

The thing that could make me lean toward Trump is his push for domestic energy production—especially the idea of keeping the U.S. energy-independent instead of relying on other countries while still consuming the same amount overall. My read on it is pretty simple: demand doesn’t disappear just because we say the right words about climate. If we still need oil and gas in the short-to-medium term—and I think we do—I’d rather that production happen under U.S. standards than outsourced to places with weaker environmental controls. That’s not “anti-climate” to me; it’s more like managing reality while you transition.

I remember during college, energy prices spiked and everything from shipping costs to groceries followed. In my current job, you can literally see fuel costs ripple through supply chains. So when I hear “just restrict production,” I’m not buying it yet unless someone can show me the full replacement plan that doesn’t just shift the footprint somewhere else or spike costs for people.

So yeah—if I boil it down, it’s that: a more aggressive stance on domestic energy production as a bridge, rather than policies that feel like they’re trying to skip steps. I’m open to being wrong, but I’d need to see a transition plan that actually closes that gap without outsourcing the problem.

dislike_rep_candidate.reason_category: References to race, gender, religion, or symbolic representation of social groups.
dislike_rep_candidate.text:
Yeah—if I’m being specific, it’s the way he talks about and handles religion in a kind of… transactional, almost performative way. That’s the piece that gives me pause.

I grew up in a church where faith wasn’t branding, it was discipline—show up, serve people, don’t make it about yourself. So when I see him use Christianity as a signal to a voting bloc, or hold it up in a way that feels more like identity politics than actual belief or humility, I’m not buying it yet. It reads like he’s treating religious people as a category to mobilize, not a community he’s accountable to in any meaningful sense.

And I’ll be fair—politicians do this across the board. I’m not expecting a pastor in office. But there’s a tone difference between “this shapes how I govern” and “this is a prop I know works.” I’m pretty sensitive to that distinction because of how I was raised. If I feel like someone is using my faith as a lever rather than respecting it as a conviction, that’s a reason I’d consider voting against them.

like_dem_party.reason_category: Views on immigration levels, border security, undocumented immigrants, or asylum policies.
like_dem_party.text:
Yeah—if I keep it to one thing, I do appreciate that Democrats tend to take immigration and asylum cases more on a human, case-by-case basis instead of treating it like a single enforcement problem.

My read on it is pretty simple: not everyone crossing a border is the same situation, and policy that acknowledges that is closer to reality. I’ve followed a few stories over the past couple years—families waiting on asylum hearings, people stuck in backlog for months—and I don’t love the idea of just defaulting to “shut it down and sort it out later.” There are real timelines, safety issues, and legal processes in play, and I think Democrats are at least trying to keep that front and center.

That said, I’m not buying the idea that compassion replaces structure. If anything, where I get frustrated is when it feels like the system gets overwhelmed because the guardrails aren’t clear or enforced consistently. But the underlying instinct—to treat immigrants and asylum seekers as individuals with claims to evaluate, not just numbers to reduce—that part I do respect. It feels more honest about what’s actually happening on the ground.

dislike_rep_party.reason_category: Views on international relations, war, defense, global leadership, or national security.
dislike_rep_party.text:
Yeah—if I had to pick one thing, it’s how uneven and sometimes reactive the party is on foreign policy, especially around long-term commitments and alliances.

My read on it is pretty simple: you can’t run national security on vibes or short election cycles. I get the instinct to avoid endless wars—I share that—but I don’t like the swing between “we’re overextended, pull back” and then a sudden, almost improvised show of force when something flares up. It feels less like a strategy and more like mood. And when you’re talking about alliances—NATO, Pacific partners, even just consistent signaling—you’re dealing with trust that compounds over years. If partners can’t predict where we’ll be in six months, they hedge, and that makes everything more fragile.

I follow this stuff mostly through news and a few analysts, so I’m not claiming insider knowledge. But even at a high level, I want a boring, durable doctrine: clear thresholds, consistent commitments, and an understanding that deterrence is partly about credibility over time. When messaging shifts depending on who’s talking that week, I’m not buying it yet. It makes the U.S. look less like a steady anchor and more like a variable, and that’s not a great place to be when the stakes are that high.

like_dem_candidate.reason_category: Mentions of climate change, environmental policy, energy production, or sustainability.
like_dem_candidate.text:
I’ll tell you the one thing that does get my attention, and it’s not abstract for me—it’s how seriously she treats climate and environmental policy as something that affects day-to-day life, not just polar bears and speeches. I live outside Charlotte, and a couple summers ago we had one of those stretches where the heat just sat on you, day after day, and our air conditioning unit was running so hard I remember thinking, “this is not what July used to feel like.” That kind of thing makes it real in a very practical, household-budget way, not ideological.

What I’ve seen from her is a willingness to push for cleaner energy and some accountability around emissions without pretending it’s painless. I’m willing to be persuaded by that approach because, to me, this isn’t about being trendy—it’s about whether we’re being good stewards of what we’ve been given and whether we’re setting up the next generation, including my grandkids, to deal with a mess we didn’t bother to manage.

So if I had to point to one thing that would move me toward voting for her, it’s that: she treats environmental responsibility like a real, ongoing obligation, not something you talk about every four years and then forget.

dislike_dem_candidate.reason_category: Views on immigration levels, border security, undocumented immigrants, or asylum policies.
dislike_dem_candidate.text:
I’m going to be very plain about it: what would make me hesitate with Kamala Harris is how her approach to immigration and the border actually plays out in practice. I’m not talking about slogans or being “tough” or “compassionate” in the abstract—I mean whether there’s a clear, enforced structure that people can understand and rely on.

From where I sit, it has felt at times too loose and too reactive, like we’re sending mixed signals about who can come in, how asylum is handled, and what the follow-through is. And when systems send mixed signals, people get hurt on every side—the families coming in, the communities receiving them, and the folks trying to manage schools, hospitals, and local services.

I’ll give you one concrete example. A church friend of mine volunteers with a local group that helps newly arrived families get settled—good people doing good work—but she told me there were weeks where they had more families arriving than they had any plan or resources for. No clear coordination, just “do what you can.” And I remember thinking, that’s not compassion, that’s a system not doing its job. You can be humane and still be orderly.

So for me, it comes down to that: I want to see immigration handled with clarity, consistency, and enforcement that matches what we say we’re doing. If I don’t trust that piece, it makes it hard for me to support someone, no matter how much I might agree with them elsewhere.

like_rep_candidate.reason_category: Evaluations of a candidate’s qualifications, preparedness, track record, or ability to govern.
like_rep_candidate.text:
I’m not someone who votes on personality, so for me it comes down to whether I think a person can actually run things. The one area where I can see an argument in his favor is that he’s already done the job once, so you’re not guessing about how he operates—you’ve seen the decisions, the staffing, the follow-through, all of it. I remember very clearly, during his term, how quickly the COVID vaccine effort got pushed through once it was underway—whatever you think about him, that kind of coordination between government, private companies, and distribution doesn’t happen by accident. 

Now, I’m not saying I agreed with how everything was handled around that, because I didn’t, but I do pay attention to whether someone can move a large, complicated system and get a result. I’ve spent my whole career in systems that don’t move unless somebody pushes them, so that matters to me. If I were going to consider voting for him, that’s the lane I’d be looking at—capacity to execute, not just talk.

like_dem_party.reason_category: Mentions of the economy, jobs, inflation, wages, taxation, government spending, or overall financial management.
like_dem_party.text: Yes, I’ll give them this: when they’re at their best, they take wages and basic cost-of-living seriously in a way that feels grounded in real households. I’m talking about things like pushing to raise the minimum wage and protect things like overtime—because that’s not abstract to me, that’s groceries and gas and whether a family can breathe at the end of the month. I’ve sat with school staff—teacher assistants, bus drivers—who were working full time and still doing the math at the kitchen table, and it didn’t add up. So when I hear Democrats talk about lifting the wage floor and not letting employers nickel-and-dime hours, I think, “Okay, that at least starts in the right place.” I’m not saying they always get the details right—run it by me again, but this time with numbers—but the focus on paychecks and day-to-day expenses, that I respect.

dislike_dem_party.reason_category: References to race, gender, religion, or symbolic representation of social groups.
dislike_dem_party.text:
I’ll be plain about it: what rubs me the wrong way is how often the party seems to talk about people primarily in terms of race or gender first, instead of as whole individuals in a shared community. I’m not saying those things don’t matter—they do, and I spent a career trying to make schools fair for children who were not getting a fair shake—but the tone can feel like everything has to be sorted and labeled before we even get to the actual problem.

I remember sitting in on a regional education nonprofit meeting a couple years ago—this would’ve been 2022, I think—and we were reviewing a program for early reading. And instead of starting with “Are these children learning to read better?” the conversation kept circling back to how each subgroup was represented in the materials and the messaging. And I kept thinking, quietly, if we don’t get them reading by third grade, we have failed them, full stop. I’m not being ugly, I’m being clear. You can care about representation—and I do—but when it starts to feel like the main lens for every decision, it loses me.

I want a party that can hold both things at once without turning it into a sorting exercise.

like_rep_party.reason_category: Mentions of climate change, environmental policy, energy production, or sustainability.
like_rep_party.text:
I’ll be honest, I don’t line up neatly with either party, but there is one area where I tend to nod along with Republicans more than I expected, and that’s around energy—specifically the idea that you don’t make good environmental policy by pretending people don’t still need reliable, affordable power every single day.

I remember sitting in a regional meeting a couple years ago—this was tied to one of the nonprofits I consult with—and a superintendent from a rural county was talking about how their school budget got squeezed because of rising energy costs. Not theory, not politics—just keeping the lights on and buses running. And what I appreciated from the Republican side in that conversation was the insistence on, “Okay, if we’re going to transition to cleaner energy, show me how it works without destabilizing communities in the meantime.” Not just the goal, but the path.

I’m all for being responsible about the environment—we should be—but I do think that focus on practicality, on not getting ahead of what infrastructure and families can actually handle, is something they tend to emphasize more consistently. I hear what some folks say about moving faster, and maybe they’re right in the long run, but the way this plays out on a Tuesday morning, people still have to heat their homes and schools still have to open. I respect that grounding in reality.

dislike_rep_party.reason_category: References to race, gender, religion, or symbolic representation of social groups.
dislike_rep_party.text:
I’ll just say it plainly: I don’t like the way the party, at least as it comes across to me lately, talks around issues of race and acts like acknowledging real differences in people’s experiences is somehow a problem in itself. That lands wrong for me, both as an educator and just as a person who’s tried to treat people fairly.

I remember a curriculum review meeting—this would’ve been, oh, late 2000s—where we were trying to include a few more books that reflected our actual student population, which wasn’t all one background anymore. Nothing radical, just making sure children could see themselves somewhere in what they read. And the pushback we got, very much in line with what I now hear echoed in Republican talking points, was that we were being “divisive” or “bringing race into things unnecessarily.” And I sat there thinking, it’s already in the room. Pretending it isn’t doesn’t make it go away; it just means some children keep feeling invisible.

I’m not being ugly, I’m being clear: when a party seems more comfortable dismissing those concerns than listening to them, I have a hard time trusting their judgment on anything that involves real, diverse communities.

Not all, obviously, but the ones who just say what they mean, don’t dance around it, and don’t try to package everything so carefully—it reads as more honest to me.  

At work I deal with a lot of people who won’t give a clear answer, and it creates a mess down the line. So when a politician just speaks plainly, even if I don’t fully agree, I can at least understand where they stand and plan around it. To me that feels more respectful than a lot of polished talking points.

I’m going to be honest, the main thing that doesn’t sit right with me is how strongly the Republican Party pushes that very strict, small-government, “you’re on your own” kind of approach. I understand the idea of personal responsibility—I believe in that—but when it turns into pulling back support systems too far, especially around healthcare, that’s where I part ways.

From where I sit, working in a hospital system, I see what happens when people don’t have enough coverage or hit a gap. It’s not theoretical. It turns into missed appointments, confusion, people putting things off until it’s worse. So when the party leans hard into cutting or limiting those kinds of programs, it just doesn’t line up with how I think things should work for regular people trying to stay afloat.

I’m not saying everything they stand for is wrong. It’s just that piece—the ideology behind it—feels too rigid for real life.

It makes me feel like the people running things either don’t see it or don’t care, and that’s where I pull back. I need somebody who gets what a regular week costs, not somebody talking around it.
