Mira, te lo digo derechito: a mí lo que me jala es el tema de la seguridad, punto. Yo viví años saliendo de turno de noche en el almacén, caminando al carro con el frío y viendo quién anda rondando… y ahorita siento que todo está más suelto, más desordenado. Y los demócratas, perdón, pero se la pasan justificando o suavizando, como si el que cumple fuera el problema.

Trump, con todos sus modos, al menos habla de ley y orden sin pena. A mí eso me importa más que si cae bien en la tele. Yo quiero saber que si alguien roba, si alguien se mete donde no debe, hay consecuencias. No discursos, consecuencias.

Y lo de las armas igual — no me gusta la idea de que vengan a decirme que el ciudadano que trabaja no puede protegerse, pero el que anda mal sí trae lo suyo. Eso es al revés. Los republicanos, Trump incluido, por lo menos están del lado de que uno tenga cómo cuidarse.

No es complicado. Yo no ando buscando filosofía. Quiero poder salir, manejar, vivir tranquilo sin estar pensando quién me va a brincar. En eso, yo estoy con los míos, no con los otros.

dislike_rep_candidate.reason_category: Views on international relations, war, defense, global leadership, or national security.
dislike_rep_candidate.text:
Mira, te lo digo derechito: a mí lo que me brinca es cómo maneja lo de afuera, lo de guerra y esas cosas. Ahí no es juego. Un presidente no es nomás de hablar fuerte y ya, es de saber cuándo apretar y cuándo no prender fuego.

Yo he visto suficiente para saber que cuando un país se pone a picar pleito con medio mundo, los que pagan son los de abajo, no los de saco y corbata. Y Trump a veces se me hace de esos que calientan el ambiente como si fuera competencia de quién grita más. Eso en la tele se ve muy “fuerte”, pero en la vida real eso te mete en broncas largas.

Los republicanos siempre han sido de “somos los duros, los que mandan”, ¿sí? Pero una cosa es ser firme y otra es andar jugando con fuego internacional. A mí no me vendas humo con discursos de macho; tráeme resultados de que el país está más seguro, no más tenso con todo mundo.

Porque al final, si se equivoca allá arriba, no es él el que va a andar preocupado por si mandan a los muchachos a pelear o si sube todo por conflictos. Somos nosotros.

Así que sí, eso solo ya me hace pensarlo dos veces.

like_dem_candidate.text:
Yes. She’s actually done the job of governing at scale, not just talking about it.

Attorney General of California, then U.S. Senator, then Vice President—you don’t get through those roles without knowing how the machinery works: agencies, enforcement, interagency coordination, what breaks when policy hits real people. I’ve worked inside systems my whole adult life, and there’s a big difference between someone who’s read the brief and someone who’s had to own outcomes when things go sideways. She’s in the second category. That matters more to me than speeches.

dislike_dem_candidate.reason_category: Support or opposition based on party identity, ideological labels, or general alignment (e.g., liberal/conservative).
dislike_dem_candidate.text: Yes. I don’t like how tightly she tracks with the Democratic Party line on healthcare expansion through federal control. It’s too top-down for my taste, and I’ve seen what happens when policy is designed far from the clinic—more rules, more reporting, not necessarily better care. I’m not allergic to rules, I’m allergic to fake rules, and a lot of that agenda feels like compliance theater dressed up as access. That alignment alone is enough to make me hesitate.

like_rep_candidate.reason_category: Views on immigration levels, border security, undocumented immigrants, or asylum policies.
like_rep_candidate.text:
Yes. Border control, specifically the idea that the system should actually mean something.

I came here as a kid. My parents did it the legal way, and it was slow, confusing, and honestly humiliating at times. You follow rules you don’t fully understand because you’re told that’s the deal. So when I see a system that looks like it’s optional—where people can enter, stay, and then we sort it out later—it doesn’t read as compassion to me, it reads as a system that’s not functioning.

And I’m not romantic about this. In the hospital, when things are “loosely enforced,” people get hurt. Same principle. If you’re going to have asylum, have it. If you’re going to have immigration caps, have them. But enforce something coherent. What appeals to me about Trump—at least in rhetoric, sometimes in policy—is that he treats the border like it’s a real boundary, not a suggestion. That basic clarity matters to me.

dislike_rep_candidate.reason_category: Mentions of climate change, environmental policy, energy production, or sustainability.
dislike_rep_candidate.text:
Yes. His approach to climate and energy policy.

I’m not talking in slogans—I mean the pattern of pulling back from emissions standards, treating climate risk like it’s optional, and leaning hard into short-term fossil expansion without a credible transition plan. In healthcare, we already see the downstream: heat-related complications, air quality issues, patients who can’t manage chronic conditions when the environment is unstable. That’s not abstract for me.

If you’re running a system and you ignore a known risk because it’s inconvenient, people get hurt later and it’s more expensive to fix. That mindset alone is enough to push me to vote against him.

dislike_dem_party.reason_category: Concerns about democratic norms, elections, corruption, legal issues, or threats to democracy.
dislike_dem_party.text:
Yes. When they start bending process and calling it “protecting democracy,” I lose trust.

I’ve watched situations where party leaders close ranks, limit real competition, or move rules around primaries and ballot access in ways that feel engineered. Not illegal on paper, maybe—but it smells like insider control over voter choice. Don’t tell me you’re defending democracy while quietly narrowing who actually gets a shot. If you believe your candidates can win, then let the process breathe and take the risk.

like_rep_party.text:
Yes. When they’re serious about spending and not just talking, I like that.

I run budgets all day at work and at home. If the numbers don’t reconcile, people get hurt later—maybe not today, but it shows up. I’ve seen Republicans push harder on not just expanding programs without a clear way to pay for them, and that instinct—again, when it’s real, not performative—matches how I think. You can’t keep stacking obligations and pretend it’s compassion. That bill lands somewhere, usually on people who don’t have room for it.

So it’s that: a bias toward controlling spending and not treating debt like it’s abstract. When they actually follow through on that, I respect it. When they don’t, I don’t.

dislike_rep_party.reason_category: Mentions of the economy, jobs, inflation, wages, taxation, government spending, or overall financial management.
dislike_rep_party.text:
Yes. Their approach to taxes and spending. It sounds disciplined on the surface, but in practice it often means cutting revenue in ways that don’t line up with the costs we actually carry, and then pretending the math will fix itself.

I deal with budgets for a living. If you lower your income and don’t change your obligations in a real way, you either push the deficit or you quietly shift the burden somewhere else. That shows up as unstable funding, stop‑start programs, and cost pressure that lands downstream—on employers, on families, on systems like healthcare that are already stretched.

Don’t tell me you’re “pro-growth” if the plan depends on optimism instead of numbers. Show me the failure mode. Because from where I sit, it looks like short-term relief paired with long-term mess.

Yeah, for me it would come down to money and how things actually feel week to week.

I’m not trying to make it political, I’m thinking about when we’re pricing jobs or replacing equipment. A couple years ago, fuel, parts, even basic supplies were jumping around so much it was hard to give customers a straight number without building in a cushion. That puts you in a bad spot—either you look expensive or you eat the cost later. When things are more stable and predictable, I can schedule cleaner, quote cleaner, and keep my guys working without constantly adjusting.

So if I feel like his approach is going to keep costs from bouncing all over the place and make it easier to plan a month or two out without guessing, that’s what would push me in his direction. I’m not looking for perfect, I just want it steady enough that I can run my business without playing defense all the time.

I can respect that they’ll actually put money behind things that create jobs locally. I’m thinking of some of the infrastructure stuff the last few years—roads, equipment, contracts that end up hiring crews like ours or people we know. When that kind of spending is real and not just talk, it trickles down in a way you can see: more work orders, steadier schedules, people getting paid on time.

I’m not saying they always get it right, because sometimes it feels like the money goes out the door without enough thought. But when it’s focused on actual work getting done and people getting hired, I like that. It’s practical.

I’ll be honest, the thing that makes me pull back is how they talk about policing and public safety. I’m not trying to be dramatic, I’m trying to be done—people need to feel safe where they live and work, full stop. We’ve had jobs where crews are out early or late, sometimes in areas we don’t know well, and I’m the one thinking, “Are they okay? If something goes sideways, who shows up?”  

When I hear messaging that sounds like pulling back support from police or making it harder for them to do their job, it just doesn’t sit right with me. I get that there are bad situations and things that need fixing—I’m not ignoring that—but the answer can’t feel like you’re weakening the people who are supposed to respond when something actually happens.  

It’s one of those things where, on paper, people might explain it a certain way, but I’m thinking about a Tuesday night when something goes wrong and you need help now. That’s the lens I use, and a lot of what I hear from Democrats just doesn’t line up with that.

It feels like the focus is on expanding the idea of coverage, but not enough on making it predictable and simple to use.

I’m cool with helping people—I literally trained for that. like_dem_candidate.text:
Yeah. It’s abortion. Pretty clean for me.

I’m not trying to be dramatic, I’m trying to be accurate—having the government set a one-size rule on something that medical and personal just doesn’t sit right. The older I get, the more I’ve had friends deal with pregnancies that were not the neat, Instagram version. Complications, timing that would’ve blown up their finances, actual health risks. After my ER situation, I have a lot less patience for policies that assume bodies behave predictably or that you’ll have perfect access to care at the exact moment you need it. That’s just not how real life works.

So when she talks about protecting access and not letting states basically play roulette with what care you can get, that tracks for me. I’m not out here wanting zero limits or pretending it’s not a serious decision. It is. But I don’t think politicians should be the ones drawing hard lines that ignore edge cases—which are not actually edge cases if you know enough women.

That’s the lever that would make me consider voting for her. Not vibes, not speeches—just that.

dislike_dem_candidate.reason_category: References to crime, policing, public safety, gun rights or gun control.
dislike_dem_candidate.text:
Yeah—public safety, straight up.

What I’ve seen, especially living in the Seattle area and actually using transit every day, is that when leadership gets even a little fuzzy about enforcement, things slide fast. And with Harris, the way she talks about criminal justice always sounds like it’s trying to thread a political needle instead of just being clear that laws actually need to be enforced consistently. I’m not trying to be dramatic, I’m trying to be accurate—when people don’t think there are consequences, behavior changes. You feel it on the light rail, you see it around stations, you notice which stores start locking everything up.

I don’t need some hardline “throw everyone in jail” thing, that’s not realistic either. But I do need to trust that whoever’s in charge isn’t going to downplay disorder or make enforcement optional depending on the narrative that week. And I don’t get that confidence from her. It feels like a lot of signaling, not a lot of “here’s exactly how we keep regular people safe on a Tuesday.”

So yeah, that’s the issue that would push me against her.

I’m not trying to be dramatic, I’m trying to be accurate — a lot of politicians sound polished and then nothing changes, and with him it at least feels like movement, even if it’s messy.

Like, I sit through enough corporate meetings where everyone uses perfect language and nothing happens, and it drives me insane. He’s kind of the opposite of that vibe. It’s not pretty, it’s not disciplined, but it doesn’t feel passive.

That’s basically it. Not a list, not some deep policy alignment — just that sense of “okay, something will actually happen,” which, frankly, is a low bar but still rare.

dislike_rep_candidate.reason_category: Concerns about democratic norms, elections, corruption, legal issues, or threats to democracy.
dislike_rep_candidate.text:
Yeah. It’s the way he treats elections and the basic rules around them.

I’m not trying to be dramatic, I’m trying to be accurate: if someone loses and their first instinct is to say the system is rigged without actually proving it in a way that holds up in court, that’s a problem for me. Not politically, structurally. I need the person at the top to reinforce that the process is legitimate even when it doesn’t go their way, because otherwise you’re basically telling half the country “only trust it if we win.” That’s how you erode the thing that lets everything else function.

I followed a lot of that post-2020 stuff through normal news sources, not fringe corners, and what stood out to me wasn’t just the claims, it was that they kept getting thrown out when they had to meet an actual legal standard. And then the messaging didn’t adjust. It just kept going. That disconnect bothers me more than the initial complaint.

So yeah, for me it’s not personality or tone or whatever people usually argue about. It’s that I don’t trust how he handles losing power, and that’s a non-negotiable job requirement.

like_dem_party.reason_category: Positions on abortion, Roe v. Wade, reproductive freedom, or pro-life/pro-choice issues.
like_dem_party.text:
Yeah—this is pretty straightforward for me: I like that Democrats are the ones actually protecting access to abortion. Not in a vague “women’s rights” slogan way, I mean concretely, like making sure it’s still legal and accessible in real life, not just technically allowed if you can jump through ten hoops.

I’m not trying to be dramatic, I’m trying to be accurate—pregnancy is not some abstract moral talking point when you’re the one who has to carry it, deal with the health risks, take time off work, and figure out how to pay for everything after. The idea that the government should be able to step in and override that decision just doesn’t sit right with me. And it’s not even hypothetical anymore. After Roe got overturned, it stopped being this distant policy debate and turned into “okay, depending on your zip code, your options are just gone.”

What I respect—specifically about Democrats on this—is they treat it like a practical reality, not a purity test. Like, people’s lives are messy. Contraception fails, situations change, health issues come up. I don’t need politicians pretending there’s a clean, one-size-fits-all moral rule that covers every scenario. That’s kind of how I see the border when it’s not managed well.

And look, I’m not anti-immigrant. Half the landscaping crews and a bunch of folks doing hard labor around here are immigrants and they work their tails off. I respect that. But there’s a difference between having a system that’s clear—who can come in, how, what the rules are—and just letting it be this vague, backlogged, “we’ll figure it out later” situation. I’m out here with a truck payment, tool debt, fuel costs bouncing around, jobs that come and go—and every time they push more spending or new costs, it lands on me. Not in theory. In my account.

They talk like they’re helping “working people,” but my costs go up, my taxes don’t feel lighter, and the margin I’m working with gets tighter. It’s like they don’t get how thin it already is. One slow month and you’re juggling bills. Make it make sense in the real world.

And honestly, it feels like our side is trying to keep money in your pocket and keep things moving, and their side is comfortable making everything more expensive as long as it sounds good on paper. I don’t have the luxury of paper. I’ve got payments due.

Republicans talk more like how I have to live: don’t burn what you don’t have, don’t jack up costs so bad people can’t keep up, and let people keep more of what they earn. I mean… if I’m being honest, it’s more of a feeling than anything I can point to cleanly. There’s a certain bluntness there that, on a gut level, can read as “at least you know what you’re getting.” Some days that lands as… refreshing, I guess, compared to people who sound polished but you’re not sure what they mean.

I don’t know that I’d build a whole decision on it, but that kind of straight-ahead presence—however rough around the edges—can make you pause and think, “well, at least that’s not dressed up.” It’s not a detailed argument, it’s just a reaction I notice in myself sometimes.

like_dem_party.reason_category: References to healthcare, Medicare/Medicaid, prescription costs, social safety nets, or aid to disadvantaged groups.
like_dem_party.text:
Yeah… for me it comes down to healthcare, honestly. I feel like they at least try to keep the floor from dropping out under people when they get sick.  

I’ve spent enough time in waiting rooms and on the phone with insurance to know how fast things can go sideways, and I just don’t see the other side taking that seriously. It’s not perfect, not even close, but there’s an effort there to make sure people aren’t just on their own.

like_rep_party.reason_category: Positions on abortion, Roe v. Wade, reproductive freedom, or pro-life/pro-choice issues.
like_rep_party.text: I mean… if I’m being honest, it’s the abortion piece. I was raised to see that as a real moral line, not just a policy debate, and that stuck. I don’t talk about it much, but that’s the one place I feel some alignment.

dislike_rep_party.reason_category: Support or opposition based on party identity, ideological labels, or general alignment (e.g., liberal/conservative).
dislike_rep_party.text: Yeah… I mean, at a basic level, I just don’t line up with them ideologically. The tone, the priorities—it feels like a different moral framework than the one I’m operating from. I tend to lean Democrat, so it’s just… not my lane, if I’m being honest.

dislike_dem_candidate.reason_category: Views on immigration levels, border security, undocumented immigrants, or asylum policies.
dislike_dem_candidate.text:
Yeah. Immigration. That’s the one that makes me grind my teeth.

Look, I’m a Democrat, I vote with Democrats, but on this? It feels like our side talks like there’s no limit, like if you even ask “how many can we handle” you get treated like you’ve got a problem. That’s not how real life works. I’ve spent my whole career in places where if more shows up than the system can handle, things break. People get hurt, stuff backs up, costs go sideways. Same principle.

What I see from her—and honestly from a lot of Democrats—is this loose, almost hand-wavy approach at the border. Like it’s all compassion and no structure. And I’m not against people coming here. My family didn’t start here either. But there’s got to be a front door that works and a back door that’s actually closed. Right now it looks like neither is under control.

And here’s where I get ticked off: working people in places like where I live are the ones who feel it first. Jobs at the lower end get tighter, services get stretched, and then if you say that out loud, you’re the bad guy. Meanwhile, the folks making those policies don’t live with it day to day.

So yeah, that’s the issue where I look at her and think, “You’re not taking the limits seriously.” And I’ve seen how that movie ends. It’s not compassion—it turns into chaos, and then everybody pays for it.

like_rep_candidate.reason_category: Support or opposition based on party identity, ideological labels, or general alignment (e.g., liberal/conservative).
like_rep_candidate.text:
No. I’m a Democrat. That’s the lane I’m in.

I’ve spent my whole adult life around working people, layoffs, scraping things together when the math doesn’t work, and I know which side at least says they’re trying to keep a floor under people like us. Republicans, and Trump right at the front of that, that’s not my side. That’s a different set of priorities, different crowd they answer to.

You can dress it up however you want, but I don’t cross over on that. Party matters because it tells you who they’re standing with when things get tight, and I’ve seen how that goes. I’m not switching jerseys at this point.

like_dem_party.reason_category: References to crime, policing, public safety, gun rights or gun control.
like_dem_party.text:
Yeah. I like that Democrats, at least the ones I stick with, don’t play games with public safety.

Look, I’ve worked in places where one bad decision gets somebody hurt. You don’t “debate” that. You set rules, you enforce them, and you don’t hand dangerous people more room to mess things up. Same thing here. I don’t want everybody walking around armed to the teeth like it’s normal, and I don’t buy this idea from Republicans that more guns everywhere magically makes things safer. I’ve seen how that kind of thinking goes — it’s the same as ignoring a safety guard on a machine because it slows you down. Yeah, until somebody loses a hand.

Democrats, in my view, are at least trying to keep some structure around it — background checks, limits, actual accountability. Republicans act like any rule is oppression, and I think that’s flat-out irresponsible. I’m not interested in slogans about “freedom” if it means my neighborhood or a workplace turns into a free-for-all.

So yeah, that’s a big one for me. They take the idea of safety seriously instead of turning it into a talking point.

dislike_rep_party.reason_category: Mentions of the economy, jobs, inflation, wages, taxation, government spending, or overall financial management.
dislike_rep_party.text:
Yeah. The way they handle the economy—if you can even call it “handling”—drives me up a wall.

They talk like they’re the party of working people, but I’ve lived through what their version of that looks like. It’s tax cuts dressed up like a solution to everything, and somehow the people actually punching a clock are still squeezed at the end of the month. I’m sitting there looking at my credit card statement, groceries going up, gas doing its thing, and I’m supposed to believe the fix is more breaks at the top and “it’ll trickle down”? I’ve seen how this movie ends.

Look, I’m not trying to win a debate, I’m trying to keep the lights on. When they’re in charge, I don’t feel any more stable. If anything, it feels like the ground gets softer under regular people while they tell you everything’s booming. Booming for who? Not the guy working overtime just to stay even.

And don’t get me started on the deficit side of it. They’ll spend when it suits them, cut revenue, and then turn around and act like there’s no money left for anything regular people rely on. That’s not discipline, that’s a shell game. I don’t trust it, because I’ve had to actually balance a budget where the numbers have consequences.

like_dem_candidate.reason_category: References to healthcare, Medicare/Medicaid, prescription costs, social safety nets, or aid to disadvantaged groups.
like_dem_candidate.text:
Yeah. For me it comes down to healthcare, straight up.

I’m living that system every single day with my sister since her stroke. I’m juggling meds, appointments, rehab, insurance calls that feel like a second job. And I’ll tell you plain: the difference between “covered” and “not quite covered” is the difference between her getting stronger or sliding backward. It’s not theoretical to me. It’s Tuesday morning, did the prescription go through, can I afford this refill, how many hours on hold am I about to burn.

What makes me lean toward Harris is that she actually talks like expanding coverage and lowering drug costs is something you do on purpose, not something you hope the market fixes. I don’t need poetry. I need somebody who understands that if Medicare or Medicaid doesn’t step in properly, families like mine get crushed quietly at home. You don’t see it on TV. You see it when you’re cutting pills in half or delaying therapy because the bill came in wrong.

Hold on, let me do the math out loud the way I do at home: one bad coverage gap, one expensive medication, that’s hundreds a month easy. Stretch that over a year, now you’re talking thousands. That’s not “tighten your belt” money—that’s change-your-life money for a caregiver household.

So yeah, if I believe a candidate is going to push harder on making prescriptions affordable and keeping people covered without all these traps and gaps, that’s a reason for me. Because I’ve hauled enough truth to know it’s heavy—people don’t skip care because they’re lazy, they skip it because the system makes it damn near impossible to keep up.

like_rep_candidate.reason_category: Views on international relations, war, defense, global leadership, or national security.
like_rep_candidate.text:
I’ll be straight with you: I’m not looking to vote for him. But if you’re asking me to name one thing that could pull me in that direction, it would be how he handles not getting us dragged into wars.

Here’s what I mean. I’ve hauled grain through enough election cycles to hear a lot of big talk about “strength,” and too often that turns into sending somebody else’s kid overseas while folks back home keep arguing on TV. What I paid attention to during his time was that, for all his noise—and he’s got plenty of that—he didn’t start a new war. That matters to me more than the speeches. I’ve got drivers with families, I’ve got nephews of church friends who enlist because that’s their path, and I don’t like a government that treats that like an easy lever to pull.

I remember sitting on the bus with a guy, older than me, Vietnam vet, quiet type. He said, “Most people voting never smell it.” That stuck. So when I look at a candidate, I’m asking: are you going to use force like a last resort, or like a tool you reach for to look tough? With Trump, for all the chaos around him, there was a kind of reluctance to open a new front. You can call it instinct, you can call it deal-making, I don’t know the right word—but I noticed it.

Now, I’m not naive. Not starting a war doesn’t mean you handled the world cleanly or responsibly. There’s a lot in foreign policy that’s quieter—alliances, stability, not spooking markets or partners—and I care about that too. But if you’re forcing me to give you one concrete thing that could make me pause and say “maybe,” it’s that: I don’t see him as eager to send people into a new war, and that carries weight with me.

dislike_rep_candidate.reason_category: Comments about temperament, honesty, likability, morality, or personal behavior.
dislike_rep_candidate.text:
Yeah. For me it comes down to how he carries himself with the truth. I can’t get past that.

I’ve run a business where if I tell a farmer, “I’ll be there Tuesday at 6,” I better be there Tuesday at 6, or I better call before sunrise and explain why not. You don’t just say whatever keeps you comfortable in the moment and then change it later like it never happened. I’ve watched Trump do that over and over—say one thing, then another, then act like you’re the problem for remembering the first thing. That’s not politics to me, that’s character.

And look, I’m not asking for perfect. I’ve hauled enough truth to know it’s heavy. People mess up, people spin a little, fine. But there’s a line where it starts to feel like there’s no anchor at all, like nothing he says is meant to hold steady. When you’re responsible for other people—employees, family, a whole country—you don’t get to treat words like disposable tools.

So yeah, that alone is enough for me to vote against him. If I can’t trust your word in the small things, I’m not handing you the big ones.

like_dem_party.reason_category: Concerns about democratic norms, elections, corruption, legal issues, or threats to democracy.
like_dem_party.text:
Yeah. I like that, at their best, Democrats still treat elections and the rules around them like they actually matter, not like something you bend when it’s inconvenient.

Let me say it plain. I built my life on systems that only work if people follow the rules even when it costs them. You log your miles right, you report a problem even if it delays the load, you pay what you owe even when cash is tight. If I’ve got a driver who says, “Well, the rules don’t apply today because I’m in a hurry,” that’s how you get somebody killed or a business shut down.

Same thing with elections. You lose, you lose. You don’t start poking holes in the whole structure just because you don’t like the outcome. I remember 2020 clear as day — all that noise about the vote being rigged with no solid proof that held up in court. And what I watched was Democrats, for the most part, saying, “Count it, certify it, follow the process.” Judges, recounts, audits — do it by the book. That matters to me more than whether my side wins on a given night.

Because once people stop trusting that the count is real, everything else turns into a shouting match or worse. And I’ve hauled enough truth to know it’s heavy — you can’t run a country on vibes and accusations. You need procedures people respect even when it stings.

So yeah, that’s the thing I like. Not perfection — don’t sell me that — but a basic respect for the machinery of democracy. Without that, all the policy talk in the world is just noise.

dislike_dem_party.reason_category: Support or opposition based on party identity, ideological labels, or general alignment (e.g., liberal/conservative).
dislike_dem_party.text:
Yeah—there is, and it bothers me more than I like to admit. Sometimes the party leans so hard into the *label* of being progressive that it forgets to check whether the idea actually works for people like us out here. I’m a very liberal Democrat, I’m not confused about that—but I don’t worship the word “liberal” like it’s a solution by itself.

I’ve sat in on county meetings or watched clips online where folks talk like if a policy sounds morally right, that’s the end of the conversation. And I’m sitting there going, hold on, let me do the math out loud—how does that land in a town with one mechanic, one clinic, and a two-hour drive between services? Because if your version of “progress” doesn’t translate outside a city grid, then it’s just a slogan wearing a nice suit.

I remember a conversation—this was maybe a year or two ago—about phasing out diesel fast. Now, I believe in climate action, I do. Weather’s changed, I’ve had to plan routes around it. But the way some people were talking, it was like the identity of being green mattered more than the actual transition. No schedule, no infrastructure, no bridge for the people hauling the food. Don’t sell me a sunset, sell me the schedule. If the party starts treating the label as proof instead of the plan, that’s where I get irritated.

So yeah, that’s my issue. Not the values—I’m aligned there. It’s when the party gets a little too attached to sounding like the “right kind” of Democrat instead of being the kind that actually works in the real world.

like_rep_party.reason_category: Views on international relations, war, defense, global leadership, or national security.
like_rep_party.text:
Yeah—there is one thing, and I’ll give it to them straight. On national security and dealing with the rest of the world, Republicans tend to talk like the stakes are real and heavy, not abstract. I respect that.

Let me tell you where that lands for me. When you run trucks and storage, you start paying attention to how fragile the chain really is—fuel prices jump because of something halfway across the world, fertilizer gets tight, grain contracts shift because of a war you can’t even point to on a map without squinting. I remember when Ukraine got hit—suddenly it wasn’t “over there,” it was showing up in pricing calls, timing, insurance questions. That kind of thing teaches you quick that global leadership isn’t a slogan. It’s keeping lanes open, keeping bad actors from deciding they can just take what they want.

What I tend to hear from Republicans—at least the ones I can stand listening to—is a willingness to say, “No, you don’t get to push us around, and we’re going to back that up.” Strong alliances, a military that’s actually ready, not just funded on paper—that matters. I don’t mean chest-thumping. I mean clarity. If you draw a line, it better be a real line.

I’m a Democrat, strong one, not changing that. But I’ve hauled enough truth to know it’s heavy—if the U.S. looks uncertain or slow overseas, it doesn’t stay overseas. It hits supply chains, it hits farms, it hits working people first. So yeah, I like that Republicans, at their best, don’t treat global security like a side conversation. They treat it like the foundation, and in my world, that part checks out.

like_dem_candidate.reason_category: References to crime, policing, public safety, gun rights or gun control.
like_dem_candidate.text:
I’ll be honest, it would come down to whether I think she’ll actually keep a handle on public safety. That’s it for me. I don’t need speeches—I need to know our side isn’t going soft while the other side pretends crime isn’t a real problem people deal with every day.

If I saw her take a clear, no-nonsense line—backing law enforcement, not tying their hands, making it clear the rules still mean something—then yeah, I could consider it. But if it feels like the same Democratic pattern of talking around it while things slide, I’m out. I’ve got too much riding on things staying orderly to gamble on that.

dislike_dem_candidate.reason_category: Evaluations of a candidate’s qualifications, preparedness, track record, or ability to govern.
dislike_dem_candidate.text:
Yeah. For me it comes down to whether I think someone can actually run things when it gets messy, not just talk about it.

With her, I haven’t seen enough that makes me trust she’s the one in charge when it counts. It feels like a lot of talking points and not a lot of clear, steady leadership you can point to and say, “she handled that, start to finish.” And I’m not interested in voting for a team where I’m not sure who’s really driving the bus. I’ve got enough experience dealing with problems that look fine on paper and fall apart in real life.

So if it’s her versus someone I think is more proven on the other side, I’m probably going the other way. I’m not trying to win an argument about it—I just need to believe the person can run the operation without things slipping.

like_rep_candidate.reason_category: Views on immigration levels, border security, undocumented immigrants, or asylum policies.
like_rep_candidate.text:
I don’t love getting into politics like this, but I’ll be straight with you. The main thing is the border. It just feels like under Democrats it’s gotten loose to the point where nobody’s really in charge, and that makes everything else harder to keep stable. I’m not against people coming here, but it’s got to be controlled and legal, or the whole system starts to feel like it doesn’t mean much.

With Trump, whether people like his style or not, it at least felt like Republicans were serious about locking it down and enforcing the rules. That matters to me. I deal with systems all day—if you don’t maintain them, they break. That’s how I see it.

dislike_rep_candidate.reason_category: Comments about temperament, honesty, likability, morality, or personal behavior.
dislike_rep_candidate.text:
Yeah… I mean, for me it comes down to how he carries himself. I don’t need a president to be perfect, but I do expect some basic steadiness and honesty, and with him it always feels like you’re getting a different version depending on the day. That wears on you.

And I’ll be straight — the way he talks about people, even folks on his own side when they don’t line up exactly with him, it just feels meaner than it needs to be. I’m not looking for a pastor in office, but I do think character shows up in how you handle power, and I don’t like what that says.

So yeah, that kind of thing pushes me away more than anything else.

like_dem_party.reason_category: Support or opposition based on party identity, ideological labels, or general alignment (e.g., liberal/conservative).
like_dem_party.text:
I wouldn’t say I “like” them across the board, but if I’m being straight, I’m more comfortable with Democrats than Republicans these days. The Republican side, a lot of it just feels too hard-edged and combative for me now, like everything’s a fight and you’ve got to fall in line or you’re out. I don’t operate that way.

Democrats, for all their issues, come off more practical to me, more willing to keep things steady instead of turning everything into a culture war. That fits me better. I’m not interested in waving a party flag, I just want something that feels grounded and not constantly trying to prove a point.

like_rep_party.reason_category: Concerns about democratic norms, elections, corruption, legal issues, or threats to democracy.
like_rep_party.text:
Yeah… I mean, if I’m being straight about it, what I like is that they *say* they’re about keeping elections clean and not letting things get loose or questionable. That matters to me. If people stop trusting how votes are handled, the whole thing starts to wobble, and then you’ve got bigger problems than who wins.

I’m not interested in chaos or back-and-forth about whether the system’s legit every time. I want it locked down, clear, and above board so when it’s done, it’s done. When Republicans are focused on that—on making sure the process is tight and nobody’s cutting corners—that’s something I can get behind.

like_dem_candidate.reason_category: Concerns about democratic norms, elections, corruption, legal issues, or threats to democracy.
like_dem_candidate.text:
I mean… if I’m being straight, the only angle would be if I felt like she’d keep things orderly and not play games with elections or the rules. Like, I don’t want chaos or people bending the system depending on who’s in power.

I’m not saying I’m convinced on that, though. That’s just the lane where it would even matter to me.

dislike_dem_candidate.text: Yeah… for me it’s the border stuff. It just feels loose, like there’s not real control or limits, and I don’t trust how that plays out long term. I’m big on order, so that’s where I get stuck.

I’m in HVAC, so I see how much waste there is, old systems, people overpaying, all that. The push for cleaner, more efficient stuff… that part makes sense to me. I just don’t like how they go about it half the time.

dislike_rep_party.text: Yeah… I’m pro-life, so that part I’m aligned on. I just don’t like when it turns into talking points instead of actually helping women carry it out in real life. It can feel a little… incomplete.

like_dem_candidate.text: No. I don’t trust her. Something about how she comes off feels slick, not straight. That’s it.

They act like the border’s not a real problem. That’s what I don’t like.

dislike_dem_candidate.text: Yeah, I mean… she’s just not on my side of things. It’s that simple.

like_rep_candidate.reason_category: References to crime, policing, public safety, gun rights or gun control.

Yeah. Safety stuff.

I just feel like things are more under control with him. That’s it.

dislike_rep_candidate.reason_category: Comments about temperament, honesty, likability, morality, or personal behavior.
dislike_rep_candidate.text: I mean… yeah, sometimes he just comes off like he’s full of it. Like I don’t trust how he talks half the time. That kind of thing bugs me.

dislike_rep_party.reason_category: Comments about temperament, honesty, likability, morality, or personal behavior.
dislike_rep_party.text: Yeah… sometimes they just come off kind of fake to me. Like saying what sounds good and then acting different. I don’t like that.

like_rep_candidate.reason_category: Mentions of the economy, jobs, inflation, wages, taxation, government spending, or overall financial management.
like_rep_candidate.text:
I mean… I’m a Democrat, I vote blue, that’s my lane. But I’m not going to pretend money stress isn’t real just because of party lines. That’s the one place where I at least understand why people around me go, “yeah, maybe him.”

Because listen, when people talk about Trump, what they’re really talking about up here is, “were things cheaper, and did it feel like you could breathe a little?” And a lot of folks will tell you yes, flat out. Gas, materials, groceries—those are not abstract talking points for us, that’s literally whether a job pencils out or whether a homeowner backs out halfway through because the price jumped again.

I see it on calls. Someone will say, “we were going to do the system this year, but everything’s up, we can’t swing it.” And then I’m the one trying to reschedule, re-quote, calm them down. So when Republicans say, “we handle the economy better,” even if I don’t agree with half of what they stand for, I get why that message lands. It’s simple and it hits people right in their day-to-day.

So would that alone make me vote for him? I’m not there. But that’s the only lane where he even gets in the conversation for me, is the idea—real or not—that under Republicans, money stretches a little further and businesses like ours don’t feel squeezed every single week.

like_dem_party.reason_category: References to crime, policing, public safety, gun rights or gun control.
like_dem_party.text:
Yeah. Honestly, the big thing for me is how Democrats handle guns and public safety compared to Republicans. And I know people hear “rural” and assume I’m supposed to be on the other side of that, but that’s not how it plays out where I live.

Listen, I’m around equipment, job sites, people’s properties all day. I know exactly how fast something can go wrong when the wrong person has access to a weapon or when there’s zero guardrails. And what drives me nuts about Republicans is this attitude of “more guns, fewer rules, that’s freedom,” full stop. That sounds nice until you’re the one dealing with a situation that escalates in five seconds because nobody had to slow down and think before getting a gun.

Democrats, at least, are willing to say: no, we’re not just going to shrug and call every bad outcome the cost of doing business. They push for basic limits, background checks, that kind of thing, and to me that’s just… normal responsibility. It’s not taking your whole life away, it’s putting a gate in front of something that can do real damage.

I’ve had customers where you can tell a conversation is getting heated, and you’re doing that mental math like, “Okay, how far is the truck, who else is on site, what’s the exit here.” I don’t want to also be thinking, “And does this guy have three guns in the house with zero oversight?” That’s not abstract to me. That’s a Tuesday.

So yeah, when I say I’m a Democrat, that’s a big part of it. We’re the side that at least treats public safety like it’s something you manage, not something you just cross your fingers about and call freedom.

dislike_dem_party.reason_category: Views on international relations, war, defense, global leadership, or national security.
dislike_dem_party.text:
Yeah—there is, and it bugs me more than I like to admit. It’s how Democrats handle anything around war, defense, and just… how we show up in the world.

Listen, I’m on their side, I vote straight blue, I’m not confused about that. But sometimes it feels like our side either gets way too cautious or way too messy about it, and neither one makes me feel great. Like, we’ll talk a big game about values and alliances and standing up for people globally—which I agree with—but then the follow-through is either slow, unclear, or just kind of all over the place. And the other side jumps on that and goes, “See? They’re weak,” and honestly… sometimes we make that an easy argument for them.

I’m not saying I want us acting like cowboys, not even close. I don’t trust Republicans on this stuff either—they act like strength just means louder and more aggressive, and that gets people killed fast. But with Democrats, it can feel like we’re trying to thread ten needles at once and end up looking hesitant when the situation calls for being really clear and steady.

And from where I sit—rural, small business, watching costs and stability all the time—I don’t see foreign policy as this abstract thing. Fuel prices jump, materials get weird, supply chains get messed up, and suddenly I’m on the phone explaining to a homeowner why their project just got more expensive. So when our party feels inconsistent or unsure internationally, it trickles down into very real headaches here.

Here’s what I mean in real terms. When those prescription prices jump or insurance gets weird about what they’ll cover, it’s not some abstract policy debate. It hits payroll conversations. It hits whether I’m bumping wages just so someone can afford their kid’s inhaler. And I’ve seen older folks—customers of mine—stretch meds or skip doses because of cost, especially before they hit stable Medicare coverage. That’s not a small problem out here.

So when Democrats talk about capping insulin, negotiating drug prices, or shoring up Medicare/Medicaid so people actually get what they need without getting buried, I pay attention. I’m not saying they get it perfect—I’d still want to see the invoice on how it’s paid for—but the focus itself? That’s real life stuff. That’s not theory. Yeah—if I’m being straight with you, it’s how they handle immigration and the border. That’s the one that sticks with me.

Here’s the thing, I’m not anti-immigrant. Half the trades out here run on guys who came here for a better life and work their tails off. I’ve hired a few over the years, good workers, show up, don’t complain. I respect that. But what bothers me is when it feels like there’s no real line anymore, or at least no consistent enforcement of one. You can’t run a business that way. If I told my guys, “Rules matter… except when they don’t,” everything would fall apart by the end of the week.

I remember a stretch a couple years back—maybe 2023—where we had a local contractor lose two crew members overnight because of paperwork issues. Meanwhile you’re hearing on the news that people are just coming across and getting processed and released. Whether every detail of that coverage is perfect or not, the picture it paints is: follow the rules and you get stuck, don’t follow them and you might still get in. That doesn’t sit right with me.

For me it comes down to this—have a system, make it clear, enforce it evenly. If we need more legal pathways because the labor’s there, fine, let’s talk about that. I’m not married to the idea, I’m married to the outcome. But “kind of enforcing, kind of not” is the worst version. It’s hard on communities, it’s hard on workers who did it the right way, and it makes people like me question whether anyone’s actually steering the ship.

You might be twenty, thirty minutes out, and in winter that can stretch. So when people talk about policing or gun rules like it’s the same everywhere, it tells me they haven’t lived in a place where you’re the first responder by default.

I’ve had jobs where I’m out at a property after dark, middle of nowhere, customer’s nervous because someone’s been poking around their outbuildings. Or I’ve got a tech driving county roads before sunrise with a truck full of tools that’ll walk off if the wrong guy gets curious. That’s real, not theoretical. So I pay attention to who’s saying, “Make sure people can defend themselves, and don’t tie the hands of the folks doing the policing.”

I’m not looking for anything extreme. I’m looking for practical—clear rules, back up law enforcement, and don’t make a law-abiding guy jump through ten hoops to be prepared when help is a long ways off. That piece of it lines up more with Republicans, from what I’ve seen. If someone’s got a different plan, fine—show me how it works at 2 a.m. on a county road. Show me the response time. I’ve lived what it looks like when healthcare isn’t something you can just “shop” like a better price on a compressor. My mom’s stroke—there’s no clean, competitive market there. The idea that the system should lean more on private insurance and less on public support… that’s just not how reality works from where I’m standing.

So yeah, that’s where I part ways. It’s an ideological mismatch. I think healthcare’s a public responsibility, and they don’t.

Yeah. On energy and environmental stuff, I lean his direction because I think Democrats turn it into a purity test that ignores how people actually live.

Here’s the part people don’t realize until they’re standing at my window: when gas prices spike, my patients miss appointments, full stop. I saw it last summer—same people, same conditions, suddenly “I’ll reschedule next month” because they can’t afford the drive plus the copay. Republicans, Trump included, talk about producing more energy here and keeping costs down, and that translates into people actually showing up for care instead of choosing between a tank of gas and their medication.

I’m not interested in slogans about saving the planet if the plan makes daily life unstable for working people. Democrats sound like they can absorb higher costs; folks around me cannot. That’s the line for me.

I’ve spent my whole life being clocked before I’m heard, and I don’t need a president who makes that kind of sorting feel normal or acceptable. When he kept using language about immigrants that paints them like a problem instead of people, that’s not abstract to me—that’s the same tone I’ve heard in grocery stores and waiting rooms, just louder and with a microphone.

Here’s the part that sticks: a few years back at the clinic, a patient looked at me and asked, “Where are you really from?” in that exact tone, like I didn’t belong at the front desk of my own job. I handled it, because that’s what I do, but I remember thinking, this is what gets reinforced when leadership talks like that. It gives people permission.

So for me it’s not about party talking points. It’s that one side seems comfortable with language that pushes people like me to the edge of the room, and I’m not voting for that.

like_dem_party.text:
Yeah. I’ll be straight: on healthcare costs and the safety net, they’re the only ones even trying to make it less brutal for regular people. You can argue about execution all day, but at least they’re pointed in the direction of “people should be able to afford care and prescriptions,” instead of acting like it’s a personal failure if you can’t.

Here’s the part people don’t realize until they’re standing at my window: I had a patient last year, older man, polite, not dramatic, who quietly asked if he could skip a follow-up because his deductible reset and his insulin was already eating his budget. That’s not irresponsibility, that’s math. When Democrats push to cap insulin or protect Medicare and Medicaid, that’s the difference between someone managing a condition and someone ending up in the ER.  

So no, I don’t care about their slogans. I care that on this issue, they’re at least on the side of making sure people don’t have to choose between staying alive and paying the power bill.

like_rep_party.reason_category: Mentions of the economy, jobs, inflation, wages, taxation, government spending, or overall financial management.
like_rep_party.text: Yeah. On money, I line up more with Republicans, and I’m not shy about it. I watch what happens to prices, paychecks, and small businesses, not speeches, and I’ve seen Democrats talk big while everyday costs creep up and stay up. Republicans, at least in how they present it, push for tighter spending and not strangling businesses with extra costs, and that matters where I live because when small places get squeezed, hours get cut first. Here’s a real one: the auto shop I do books for had to raise labor rates after a run of higher supply costs and fees, and customers didn’t suddenly have more money, so jobs got delayed and cash flow got choppy—that ripple hits everybody. I’m not interested in ideology; I’m interested in whether my grocery bill and my gas tank behave. From where I sit, Republicans are closer to how I think about money: don’t overspend, don’t make it harder to earn, and don’t pretend the bill won’t come due. Democrats can say it’s for good reasons, but I still have to pay it.

That didn’t come out of thin air. That came from leaders in that party refusing to say clearly, “we lost, move on,” and instead feeding doubt.

I’m not interested in party loyalty over basic process. If the rules don’t hold when it’s inconvenient, then nothing else you promise matters. That’s my line.

Sometimes I think they go too far, like no control, no order, and that makes trouble. But the basic idea—that you don’t just shut the door and forget people—that part I understand. If nobody let people in, I wouldn’t be here running my business. So I don’t ignore that.

like_dem_candidate.reason_category: Evaluations of a candidate’s qualifications, preparedness, track record, or ability to govern.
like_dem_candidate.text: No. I haven’t seen anything in her record that tells me she can actually run things cleanly and predictably at the top level. Democrats talk a big game, but I watch how they execute, and it’s messy—too many dropped details, too much spin. I’m not interested in a learning-on-the-job situation at that level.

like_rep_candidate.reason_category: Mentions of the economy, jobs, inflation, wages, taxation, government spending, or overall financial management.
like_rep_candidate.text: Yeah. Money. Under Republicans—Trump especially—I felt like the economy ran tighter and people like me weren’t getting nickel-and-dimed to death. Democrats spend like there’s no bottom and then act surprised when everything costs more. I don’t do surprise expenses, and that’s what their policies feel like.

Yes. I don’t trust his character. He talks loose, shifts his story, and treats the truth like it’s optional, and that’s not how you run anything serious, never mind a country.

I’m Republican, but I expect a baseline of steadiness and honesty from our side. If I can’t rely on your word, I’m not putting you in charge.

dislike_dem_party.reason_category: Support or opposition based on party identity, ideological labels, or general alignment (e.g., liberal/conservative).
dislike_dem_party.text: Yes. I don’t like the Democratic Party’s whole ideological posture—too far left, too comfortable with big-government thinking and that liberal worldview. It’s not how I see the country or how I live my life, and I don’t trust that mindset.

dislike_rep_party.reason_category: Concerns about democratic norms, elections, corruption, legal issues, or threats to democracy.
dislike_rep_party.text:
Yeah. I don’t like how parts of my own party have gotten loose with basic rules around elections and the transfer of power. You lose, you concede, you don’t play games with certification or flirt with ignoring results you don’t like. That’s not a small detail, that’s the system.

I vote Republican, but I’m not interested in backing people who treat the process like it’s optional. If we can’t keep that straight, the rest of it doesn’t matter.

I’m not saying I agree with how he handles it, but that’s the one area where I get why people lean that way.

dislike_rep_party.text: Yeah… I don’t trust how they handle money, honestly. It always feels like the people already doing okay get the breaks, and the rest of us are just supposed to absorb the higher costs and figure it out. I’m sitting there doing the math every month, and it just doesn’t feel like they’re thinking about people like me.

like_dem_candidate.reason_category: Mentions of climate change, environmental policy, energy production, or sustainability.
like_dem_candidate.text:
Yes. For me it comes down to how seriously she treats climate and energy as an operational issue, not just a talking point.

In healthcare, I’ve seen what happens when infrastructure doesn’t hold up—heat waves, bad air days, storms knocking out power—and suddenly you’ve got patients who can’t travel, staff shortages, equipment issues, the whole system gets shaky. So when I hear her talk about investing in cleaner energy, grid resilience, and actually planning for climate impacts, that lands with me. That’s not abstract. That’s, can the hospital stay open, can people breathe, can we keep things running without constant disruption.

I’m not allergic to change, I’m allergic to confusion—and what I’ve seen from her is at least an attempt to connect the dots between energy policy and day-to-day stability. If we’re going to talk about it, let’s talk about what it costs when we don’t prepare. From where I sit, ignoring climate just makes everything more expensive and more fragile later, and I don’t have patience for that anymore.

like_dem_party.reason_category: Positions on abortion, Roe v. Wade, reproductive freedom, or pro-life/pro-choice issues.
like_dem_party.text:
Yes. For me, the clearest one is where they stand on abortion and reproductive decisions. I’m not someone who treats that lightly—I’m a churchgoing woman, I believe in responsibility, and I don’t think it’s casual. But I also don’t believe the government should be stepping in and making that decision for every woman, in every situation, like it’s one-size-fits-all. Life is not that neat.

I’ve seen enough, both in my family circle and working around healthcare, to know that situations get complicated very quickly—health risks, timing, finances, what support actually exists versus what people say should exist. And what I appreciate about Democrats is they generally trust women to make that decision with their doctor, their faith, and their circumstances, instead of turning it into a blanket rule from people who are not in that room.

When Roe v. Wade was overturned, I remember sitting in my car before work, just holding the steering wheel for a minute. Because I thought, “We’re really going backwards on who gets to decide.” And that bothered me—not because I think abortion is something to celebrate, but because I think removing that choice creates more harm, especially for women who already don’t have resources.

So if you ask me what I like, it’s that piece of it: the recognition that this is a serious, personal decision, and it should stay with the person living it—not handed down like a policy memo.

dislike_dem_party.reason_category: Support or opposition based on party identity, ideological labels, or general alignment (e.g., liberal/conservative).
dislike_dem_party.text:
Yes — and I’m going to keep it to one thing so I don’t wander.

What I don’t like is when the party leans too far into that “spend first, justify later” mindset on social programs, like the assumption that if the goal is good, the dollars don’t need tight guardrails. That doesn’t sit right with me. I vote Democrat, I believe in public systems, I work inside one — but I’ve also watched money move through healthcare in ways that make you stop and say, “Now hold on.”

I’ll give you a real example from my world. We’ll get funding for a patient access initiative — reducing wait times, improving follow-ups — all good goals. But then you look closer, and there’s no clean metric tied to it, no timeline anyone’s accountable to, and somehow we’re layering new tools on top of old broken processes. That’s not ideology to me, that’s just sloppy stewardship. And the people who pay for that sloppiness are patients waiting three weeks for an appointment that should’ve been handled in five days.

So when I hear Democrats talk like any spending is automatically justified because it’s helping people, I get quiet. Not because I disagree with helping people — I absolutely don’t — but because I want to hear the rest of the sentence. How much, by when, and who’s responsible if it doesn’t work?

I’m not allergic to investing in people. I am allergic to writing blank checks and calling it compassion.

like_rep_party.reason_category: Mentions of the economy, jobs, inflation, wages, taxation, government spending, or overall financial management.
like_rep_party.text:
I’ll give them this, and it’s a big one for me: they talk about money like it actually has consequences. And I don’t mean slogans, I mean the basic idea that spending needs a plan behind it and somebody has to be accountable for where it goes.

Working in hospital administration, I see what happens when budgets are loose or nobody’s tracking the flow properly. You get waste on the front end, and then suddenly you’re short-staffed or delaying something that actually matters on the back end. It’s the same principle at home. When I was covering my mother’s care, I kept telling myself, “I’ll sort it out later,” and later came with interest and stress that didn’t need to be that high. So when I hear Republicans push on controlling spending, watching deficits, being more disciplined about taxes and budgets, that part lands with me.

Now, do I think they always execute that perfectly? No. I’m not naive about that. But the emphasis on fiscal discipline, on not just assuming the money will appear because the cause sounds good, that aligns with how I’ve had to live my life. If we’re going to fund something, I want to know what it costs, where it’s coming from, and who’s making sure it’s not leaking all over the place. That mindset, I respect.

dislike_rep_party.reason_category: References to healthcare, Medicare/Medicaid, prescription costs, social safety nets, or aid to disadvantaged groups.
dislike_rep_party.text:
Yes. For me it comes down to how they approach healthcare funding, especially Medicare and Medicaid. I work inside a hospital system every day, so I don’t get to treat that as an abstract policy debate. I see what happens on a Tuesday afternoon when coverage is thin or eligibility gets tighter.

Here’s the operational truth: a big portion of our patients are older, disabled, or working people who still can’t carry private insurance comfortably. When reimbursement gets squeezed or programs are framed more like “cost problems” than lifelines, it shows up immediately. You get delayed care, you get people stretching medications, you get more emergency visits that could have been handled earlier and cheaper. I’ve watched a patient miss follow-ups because their coverage changed mid-year and nobody could explain it clearly on the phone. Now we’re not talking about a policy anymore, we’re talking about a person whose condition got worse because the system got harder to navigate.

I’m not saying there shouldn’t be oversight or cost control—I believe in that strongly. Waste bothers me probably more than it should. But the tone I hear too often from Republican leadership is focused on cutting or tightening first, without a clear, workable plan for how those same patients are supposed to maintain consistent care. And consistency is everything in healthcare. If you don’t control the front end—coverage, access, affordability—you pay for it on the back end in complications and higher costs anyway.

So that’s where I land. It’s not about party labels for me, it’s about whether the policy matches what actually happens on the ground. And on this, I don’t think they’re lining up with reality.

like_dem_candidate.reason_category: Positions on abortion, Roe v. Wade, reproductive freedom, or pro-life/pro-choice issues.
like_dem_candidate.text: Yeah—abortion access. I don’t need it turned into a philosophical debate. I’ve seen what happens when healthcare decisions get slowed down or blocked by policy, and I’m not interested in adding more gates there. If her position keeps that access more straightforward, that’s a point in her favor.

dislike_dem_candidate.text: Yeah. She comes off… managed. Like every answer is run through three filters before it’s said, and you never get a straight read on what she actually thinks. That kind of polish makes me assume I’m not getting the real answer, and I don’t trust that.

Te lo digo como lo vivo: aquí el clima ya no se porta como antes. Las lluvias vienen cuando quieren, el calor pega más duro, y eso no es teoría, eso es pasto que no crece y heno que no rinde. Entonces cuando alguien —aunque sea de mi partido— habla como si eso fuera puro cuento o lo empuja a un lado, a mí me brinca. Porque yo necesito que quien esté arriba entienda que producir comida depende de cuidar agua, suelo y tiempos, no nomás de decir “drill, drill” y ya.

No me malentiendas, yo tampoco quiero que nos ahoguen con reglas que no tienen sentido —eso suena bonito en la radio— pero tampoco me sirve alguien que actúe como si no hubiera problema que atender. Si siento que un candidato, aunque sea republicano, no toma eso en serio o lo reduce a puro pleito político, ahí sí me pone a pensar. Porque a mí no me mantiene un discurso, me mantiene la tierra… y esa no negocia con nadie.

like_dem_party.reason_category: Mentions of climate change, environmental policy, energy production, or sustainability.

Y eso, en el campo, no es teoría… es el día a día.

Yo he visto años donde la lluvia simplemente no llega como antes, o llega toda junta y te arruina lo que ya traías. Entonces cuando ellos insisten en cuidar recursos, en no tratar la tierra como si fuera eterna… pues sí, ahí digo “bueno, en eso no están perdidos”.

Ahora, tampoco compro todo lo que dicen ni cómo lo quieren hacer, porque a veces —eso suena bonito en la radio— pero no cuadra con cómo trabajamos aquí. Pero la preocupación como tal, el poner el tema sobre la mesa con urgencia… eso sí se los doy. Porque si el clima se te voltea, no hay partido que te salve la temporada. Ahí perdemos todos.

Eso a mí me desespera. No me vengas con novela.

Con los republicanos, al menos los que yo sigo o he escuchado por años, siento más consistencia en el carácter. Te pueden caer bien o mal, pero no andan tanto con esa cosa de querer quedar bien con todos al mismo tiempo. Y eso, para alguien como yo que vive de su palabra y de cumplir, pesa más que cualquier discurso elegante.

Mira, yo no soy de escuela, pero sé leer a la gente. Y prefiero a alguien que tal vez sea rudo al hablar, pero que no me esté cambiando la cara cada cinco minutos, a alguien muy correcto que luego no sabes ni por dónde va. Ahí es donde yo digo: ok, estos son los míos.

Eso a mí me pega, porque aquí en el campo la palabra vale. Si yo digo que entrego cierto día, entrego. Si no, doy la cara. No me vengas con novela: uno puede ser firme sin ser grosero, puede defender ideas sin andar mintiendo o tratando a la gente como tonta.

At their best, Democrats will say plainly, “we’re going to tax here to pay for this,” and you can at least evaluate the trade in the open. I’ve spent a career looking at capital allocation, and the basic question is always the same: what are you getting for the dollar, and how predictable are the outcomes? When I look at large Democratic spending packages—whether it’s stimulus, infrastructure layering, or social programs—I often see diffuse objectives and weak enforcement on how the money is actually used. That’s how you end up with inflationary pressure and programs that are politically hard to unwind but operationally underperforming.

Markets aren’t perfect, but they’re very good at punishing waste quickly. Government isn’t, unless you build in real discipline. My objection isn’t to spending per se; it’s to spending that isn’t tightly scoped, measured, and revisited with the same rigor we’d apply to any serious investment.

like_dem_candidate.reason_category: Evaluations of a candidate’s qualifications, preparedness, track record, or ability to govern.
like_dem_candidate.text:
For me it comes down to whether someone has actually operated inside messy systems and still gotten decisions out the door. With Harris, the thing that lands is that she’s held roles where you don’t get to stay theoretical—you have to make calls with incomplete information and live with the downstream effects.

One concrete example: when she was California’s attorney general, she ran a department with something like 4,000+ employees and a very wide scope—consumer protection, criminal justice, data/privacy issues—and she pushed through the state’s Homeowner Bill of Rights after the foreclosure crisis. I remember that period pretty clearly because hospitals were dealing with the same “on paper vs. in reality” gap—policies that sounded clean but fell apart in implementation. What I look for is whether someone can take a sprawling, high-stakes problem, coordinate across stakeholders who don’t agree, and still produce something operational, not just a press release. That’s the skill set I care about, and she’s at least demonstrated it in a real, non-simulated environment.

dislike_dem_candidate.reason_category: Support or opposition based on party identity, ideological labels, or general alignment (e.g., liberal/conservative).
dislike_dem_candidate.text:
Yeah, if I’m being honest, the thing that would push me away is when she leans into the more left-leaning, “big federal solution to everything” lane of the Democratic Party. I vote Democrat more often than not, but I’m not comfortable with the assumption that scaling policy up automatically makes it better or more grounded in reality.

One concrete example is how she’s talked about expanding federal roles in healthcare—broad, top-down approaches that sound clean in a speech but don’t map well to how messy hospital systems actually function day to day. That kind of thinking tends to ignore local variation and operational limits, and I’ve spent too many years watching those gaps turn into real problems for staff and patients.

So it’s less about her personally and more about where she sits ideologically. When it starts to feel like policy is being designed from a distance, without enough respect for how things actually run on the ground, that’s where I get cautious.

like_rep_candidate.reason_category: Mentions of the economy, jobs, inflation, wages, taxation, government spending, or overall financial management.
like_rep_candidate.text:
If I’m being very literal about it, the only lane where I even pause is how he approaches the economy—specifically the bias toward lower taxes and pushing business activity. Not the rhetoric, I mean the mechanics of it. When hospitals or health systems feel even a little less financial pressure, projects actually get funded instead of frozen midstream, and that has downstream effects on jobs and stability.

I saw this pretty clearly around 2018 on a project in a mid-size Illinois system—we had been dragging on a staffing request for additional analysts for months, very “wait and see” posture. Then budgets loosened, leadership felt more confident about margins, and suddenly we were allowed to hire and move. Same org, same needs, just a different level of financial confidence driving decisions.

I’m not romantic about it—I know those policies have tradeoffs and don’t land evenly—but if I’m forced to name something that could pull me in his direction, it’s that tilt toward economic conditions where organizations spend, hire, and stop operating like every dollar is the last one.

like_dem_party.reason_category: References to healthcare, Medicare/Medicaid, prescription costs, social safety nets, or aid to disadvantaged groups.
like_dem_party.text:
Yeah—if I had to pick one thing, it’s that they at least try to expand and protect coverage in a way that acknowledges how messy real healthcare costs are.

I’m not saying they execute it cleanly, because they don’t, but the intent shows up in things like capping insulin for Medicare patients. That’s one of those policies where you can actually trace the impact without a PowerPoint. I was on a project in central Illinois last year and a diabetes educator told me she finally had patients who weren’t stretching doses at the end of the month. That’s not abstract—when people stop rationing meds, you get fewer ER visits, fewer complications, less chaos downstream.

So for me it’s that orientation toward reducing the “gotcha” costs for patients. Because right now the system is full of traps, and at least that approach is trying to take a few of them off the board.

dislike_dem_party.reason_category: References to healthcare, Medicare/Medicaid, prescription costs, social safety nets, or aid to disadvantaged groups.
dislike_dem_party.text:
Yeah. The biggest thing for me is they talk about expanding access and protecting patients, but when it gets down to the mechanics—billing, coverage rules, what people actually pay—it turns into this vague promise instead of something you can operationalize.

I’m going to give you a very specific example, because otherwise it stays theoretical. In 2021 I got hit with a $12,000 out-of-pocket bill for something I had every reason to think was covered. Same hospital system I’d worked in, same kind of care we tell patients is “in-network,” and I still couldn’t get a straight answer on what was billable until after the fact. And what frustrated me wasn’t just the bill—it was that the policy conversation around it, mostly from Democrats, was all “we need affordability, we need protection,” which, fine, I agree. But no one seems willing to force the kind of boring, unglamorous standardization that would let a patient—or honestly even a hospital employee—predict cost ahead of time.

So you end up with this gap between intent and execution. The messaging is compassionate, but the system still behaves like a maze. And I have a low tolerance for that. If you’re going to say you’re fixing healthcare affordability, show me where it breaks in the billing workflow and fix that, not just the headline.

like_rep_party.reason_category: Views on immigration levels, border security, undocumented immigrants, or asylum policies.
like_rep_party.text:
Yeah—on immigration and border enforcement, I’ll give them this: they’re at least willing to say out loud that the system has to have real edges and be enforced, not just described in values language. I don’t have patience for the kind of messaging that implies we can run a high-demand system with vague rules and no throughput plan. If you’re going to have asylum, work visas, all of it, then you also need clarity on limits, timelines, and what happens when the system is overwhelmed—otherwise you’re just pushing chaos down to states, cities, and frontline services.

I saw a version of that during COVID when one of the hospital systems I was consulting with had a sudden influx of patients who didn’t have consistent documentation or coverage, and nobody at the administrative level could give a straight answer about eligibility or reimbursement paths—it turned into hours of staff time chasing basics while the ED was backed up. Different context, but same pattern: if the rules aren’t clear and enforced, the burden lands on the people doing the work.

So I don’t align with the rhetoric a lot of the time, but the underlying insistence on defined boundaries and enforcement—there’s a practical logic to that I recognize.
