Yeah—if I strip everything else away, the thing that actually moves me is her stance on abortion and how explicitly she talks about restoring federal protections after Roe was overturned.

I had my first kid in 2021, and it was… not a smooth, Instagram pregnancy. Nothing catastrophic, but enough gray-area medical decisions where I remember thinking, “I really need my doctor making this call based on me, not on what their legal team thinks is defensible this week.” That experience rewired how I think about this. It’s not abstract rights language for me—it’s operational. Who is in the room making the decision when something goes sideways?

What I associate with Harris is a pretty clear throughline: codify Roe-level protections, don’t leave it to a patchwork of state rules, and don’t put physicians in a position where they’re hesitating in real time because they’re worried about prosecution. I’m not looking for rhetoric, I’m looking for a system where the incentives are aligned so doctors can act quickly and patients don’t have to second-guess access based on their zip code.

And to be honest, this is one of those areas where I don’t love ambiguity. I can tolerate nuance on tax policy or regulation because you can iterate. This is not that. You don’t get to “pilot” access to care in an emergency. So having a candidate who is very unambiguous about protecting abortion access at a federal level—that’s a real check in the “for” column for me.

dislike_dem_candidate.reason_category: Evaluations of a candidate’s qualifications, preparedness, track record, or ability to govern.
dislike_dem_candidate.text:
Yeah—if I strip it down to one thing, it’s that I still don’t have a clean read on her as an operator, and that matters more to me than almost anything else.

What I mean by that is: when I try to evaluate her track record, I can’t easily map decisions to outcomes. As Vice President, she’s had some defined lanes—like the border portfolio early on—and I don’t feel like I’ve seen a clear, accountable throughline of “here was the goal, here’s what we did, here’s what changed.” It feels more like messaging than management. And I’m not expecting a VP to run the whole show, but I am looking for evidence of execution under constraints, because that’s the job.

This is probably my “spreadsheet brain” thing, but I want to see how someone handles ambiguity, tradeoffs, and follow-through when things get messy. At AmEx and now in consulting, you learn very quickly who can actually drive an outcome versus who can narrate one after the fact. With her, I hear a lot of framing and not enough operational clarity. Do we know what success looked like in the areas she owned, or do we just feel like she was “working on it”?

I’m open to being wrong—maybe there’s work she’s done that just isn’t communicated well—but if I can’t trace competence from inputs to outputs, that’s a problem for me. When you’re voting for someone who might have to step into the top job, I need more than potential; I need evidence that they can run something complicated and be accountable for the result.

like_rep_candidate.reason_category: Mentions of the economy, jobs, inflation, wages, taxation, government spending, or overall financial management.
like_rep_candidate.text:
If I’m being very specific, it’s the emphasis on restraining government spending and, by extension, not just assuming we can paper over everything with more federal dollars. That’s the lane where I’m at least open to him.

I have a pretty strong bias here from both my economics background and my work—“show me the incentives” is basically how I process any policy. When I hear proposals that just stack new spending on top of old programs without a clean mechanism for accountability, I get uneasy. It’s not abstract to me; I’ve seen how budgets behave inside large organizations. If no one owns the downside, costs drift, and then suddenly you’re surprised by the bill you should have predicted.

So the idea—at least rhetorically—of pushing for tighter fiscal discipline, lower taxes, and more pressure on efficiency does register for me. Especially right now, where inflation has felt very real at the household level. Groceries, childcare, everything is just… stickier than it should be. I don’t think any president controls that cleanly, but I do think signaling matters, and I’m more comfortable with a posture that treats government spending as something that needs to be justified line by line, not expanded by default.

Now, do I take any of that at face value? Not really. I’d want to see actual follow-through and what tradeoffs get made. But if you’re asking what could pull me in that direction, it’s that instinct toward fiscal constraint and not treating the federal budget like it has no ceiling.

like_dem_party.reason_category: References to race, gender, religion, or symbolic representation of social groups.
like_dem_party.text:
Yeah—if I’m being precise, the thing I genuinely like is that the Democratic Party at least tries to make representation real, not just rhetorical, across race, religion, and gender. Not perfectly, sometimes awkwardly, but intentionally.

I feel it most as a Jewish woman, honestly. There’s a difference between being “tolerated” and being assumed to belong in the room. With Democrats, I’m more likely to see people who look like a wider slice of the country—women in leadership, religious minorities, people who don’t fit one narrow cultural mold—and that changes how policies get talked about. Even when I disagree with the policy, I can usually hear that someone at the table is thinking about communities like mine in a first-order way, not as an afterthought.

And it’s not abstract. I notice it in how they talk about antisemitism alongside other forms of hate, or how they handle religious identity without immediately flattening it into “private, keep it out of public life.” It feels like they’re at least trying to build a coalition where multiple identities are visible at once, which is messy but more honest than pretending those differences don’t exist.

Do I think they always get the balance right? No. Sometimes it tips into performative, and I have a pretty low tolerance for that. But if you’re asking what I like, it’s that baseline assumption that power and visibility shouldn’t be concentrated in one narrow demographic—and that shows up in who they elevate. That matters to me more than I probably expected it would.

dislike_dem_party.reason_category: Positions on abortion, Roe v. Wade, reproductive freedom, or pro-life/pro-choice issues.
dislike_dem_party.text:
Yeah—this is pretty specific for me, it’s how absolutist the party can sound on abortion, especially post-Roe.

I’m broadly pro-choice in the sense that I think early pregnancy decisions belong with the woman and her doctor, full stop. Having gone through two pregnancies, I have a very concrete sense of how non-theoretical this is—timing, health variables, things that don’t fit into clean categories. So I’m not coming at this from a place of “ban everything.” That’s not realistic and, honestly, not humane.

But where I start to pull back from the Democratic Party is the way the rhetoric sometimes collapses all nuance into a kind of moral certainty that I don’t think matches reality. Late-term scenarios, viability questions, medical gray zones—they’re rare, but they’re not imaginary. And when the messaging is basically “any restriction equals oppression,” I have this instinctive reaction of, okay, do we know that, or do we feel that? Because policy lives in edge cases, not just the 80% scenario.

I also think there’s a missed opportunity to acknowledge tradeoffs openly. You can defend access and still admit that, at some point in a pregnancy, there are competing moral interests. Pretending that tension doesn’t exist makes me trust the argument less, not more. It starts to feel like a loyalty test instead of a serious policy position.

So it’s not that I disagree with the core goal of protecting access—I do. It’s that I want a framework that’s honest about complexity and limits, and I don’t consistently hear that from Democratic leadership.

With Trump, whatever else you want to say, he’s not part of that polished, inside-the-club crowd. And I’ve spent my whole working life watching what happens when a small group thinks they run the whole shop—decisions get made that don’t match reality on the floor. Same smell to me in politics sometimes.

I’m not saying he’s perfect—far from it—but I trust him more to push back on what I see as a system that protects itself first. The other side, they talk like they’re the referees and the players at the same time. That’s a long walk for a short drink.

So yeah, if the choice is between someone rough around the edges who I think will challenge that, and a group I feel is getting a little too comfortable deciding how everything’s supposed to go, I lean his way. I’d rather have a disruption than a system that keeps tightening the screws while telling me it’s for my own good.

With Trump, what sticks in my craw is this idea from our side that everything under him was automatically strong and would be again, no questions asked. I don’t buy anything that clean. I remember watching prices, watching what things cost to run a house, and I’ve got a mortgage again at 79 years old because of how things played out over time—so I’m not interested in cheerleading, I’m interested in results that actually hold up.

I’m a Republican by habit, sure, but I’m not punching a straight ticket if I feel like our guy is just selling a picture instead of something that adds up. I want steady—costs I can predict, taxes that don’t jump around, not this up-and-down where one party blames the other and meanwhile guys like me are just trying to keep the lights on.

So yeah, if I look at it and it feels like more of the same “trust us, it’ll be great” from our side, and it doesn’t line up with what I’ve actually seen in my checkbook, that’s enough for me to say no. I’m not made of money, and I’m not interested in political loyalty that costs me every month.

I spent my whole life in places where you had to have things in order. You don’t just let material walk in and out of a plant with no tracking, no checks, no nothing — you’d be out of business or somebody’d get hurt. Same basic idea here. A country’s got to know who’s coming in, who’s staying, and under what rules. Otherwise it turns into a free-for-all, and then the people who do it the right way are the ones getting played for suckers.

And what gets under my skin is the other side acts like even bringing that up makes you some kind of bad person. No — it’s not about hating anybody. I worked with guys from all over, didn’t bother me one bit. Show up, do the job, you’re fine by me. But there’s a difference between legal and just waving people through and pretending it’ll sort itself out.

So yeah, when Republicans talk about locking things down, enforcing the rules, not just shrugging — that lines up with how I think. It’s not fancy, it’s just… keep it under control. Measure twice, cut once. Same principle.

I’ve lived long enough to know when numbers on TV don’t match what hits your mailbox. Property taxes, utilities, insurance—those don’t care what party’s in charge, they just keep creeping. And I’ll hear Republicans pounding the table about being the “responsible” ones, but then you look around and think, alright, show me where the discipline is, because I’m still writing bigger checks every year.

It’s like on the shop floor—you don’t get credit for talking about efficiency, you either cut waste or you don’t. And sometimes our side sounds like management promising improvements while the scrap pile keeps growing. That’s a long walk for a short drink.

I still lean that way, don’t get me wrong, but I’m not blind to it. If you’re gonna say you’re the party of handling money right, then handle it right. Otherwise it starts to feel like the same game with a different label, and I don’t have much patience for that.

That’s not abstract—it hits revenue, and then it hits whether I can make a loan payment in February.

So if I see her backing policies that fund actual on-the-ground stuff—fuel reduction, prescribed burns done correctly, support for drought-resilient agriculture, water management that isn’t written by someone who’s never waited on a well report—that gets my attention. Same with incentives for energy and infrastructure that actually pencil out in rural areas, not just urban pilot projects. I don’t need speeches; I need to know someone understands that one bad fire season can cascade into three years of financial cleanup for farms like mine.

Show me the numbers, not the vibe. If her approach to climate is tied to measurable outcomes—less catastrophic fire, more stable growing conditions, support that small operations can actually access—then yeah, that’s a reason I’d lean toward voting for her.

Here’s the constraint I live with: I can have a great summer—tourism’s up around Bend, online orders are steady, wholesale moves—and then January hits and it’s like someone turned the revenue faucet off while the bills just keep marching in. Payroll, packaging invoices, loan payments, fuel, insurance. The bank doesn’t care that lavender doesn’t bloom in winter. So when someone says they want lower taxes or fewer layers of cost on small businesses, I don’t hear ideology, I hear “does that give me a little more margin to survive the off-season?”

I remember doing my books in early 2024, sitting at the kitchen table with three spreadsheets open—farm ops, household, and debt—and realizing how much of my stress isn’t about profit on paper, it’s about cash timing and how much gets shaved off the top before I can even stabilize things. So when Trump talks about cutting taxes or reducing regulatory costs, my brain goes straight to: okay, does that actually leave more cash in my account month-to-month? Because I can’t pay bills with good intentions.

Now, I’m not just taking that at face value—I’d want to see the numbers, not the vibe, like what specifically changes for a business my size, in a seasonal agricultural setup—but that’s the lane where he’s most persuasive to me. Not personality, not messaging—just the possibility that the overall cost pressure might ease even a little.

dislike_rep_candidate.reason_category: Mentions of climate change, environmental policy, energy production, or sustainability.
dislike_rep_candidate.text:
Yeah—his approach to environmental policy, specifically how he handles climate and land use, is a real sticking point for me.

I’m not talking in abstract “save the planet” terms, I mean very practical, on-the-ground consequences. I run a lavender and honey operation, so I live or die by weather patterns, pollinator health, water timing—all of it. When you have leadership that downplays climate change or rolls back environmental protections, that shows up for me as earlier blooms, weird frost timing, drought stress, and bees getting hit harder. I’m not guessing—I’ve watched seasons get less predictable, and it messes with harvest planning and yield.

There was a stretch a couple years ago where we had a warm spell that pushed the lavender early, then a cold snap clipped part of the yield. That kind of volatility is becoming more normal, not less. So when I hear rhetoric or policy moves that treat climate science like it’s optional or political, I can’t get behind that. Show me the numbers, not the vibe—and the numbers say this is a real, measurable risk to operations like mine.

And it’s not just the crops—it’s the bees. Pollinators are sensitive to temperature swings, forage availability, pesticide exposure tied to regulation decisions… all of that stacks. If federal policy leans toward short-term energy expansion or deregulation without accounting for long-term environmental impact, small producers like me absorb that risk directly. There’s no buffer. I can’t pass “bad climate policy” off to someone else—it lands in my yield and my cash flow.

So yeah, that’s a clean reason for me. If I don’t feel like a candidate takes environmental stability seriously in a way that lines up with actual science, I’m going to have a hard time voting for them.

I’m not saying they always execute it well, but the *orientation* matters to me. I’ve got a soil science background, I deal with bee health, crop yields, my grandma’s medical care—my whole life is basically “what do the results say, what do the numbers say.” So when a party at least claims it wants policy grounded in research, data, public health studies, climate data—that feels like a language I can work in.

Like, if we’re talking about something like environmental regulation or healthcare guidance, I’d rather start from “what’s been tested, what’s the measurable impact,” even if we argue about the details later. That’s just how my brain works. Show me the numbers, not the vibe.

So yeah, that’s the part I actually like. Not the branding, not the personalities—the underlying idea that policy should follow evidence more than gut feeling or tradition. That’s the closest thing to a political “home base” I’ve got, even if I don’t feel fully at home there.

Out here, I’m running a seasonal business, coordinating healthcare, and driving everywhere because nothing is close. So when I hear Democrats talk like one set of rules or one big program is going to fit everybody, I immediately start asking, okay, where does that leave people who don’t live in a city and don’t have predictable income month to month? Because it usually feels like those policies are built around a totally different lifestyle.

I’m not anti-government across the board, but that ideological tilt—more centralized control, more standardized solutions—just doesn’t line up with how I have to operate day to day. It feels like they’re solving for a version of America that isn’t mine.

I’m not anti-medicine—if anything I trust doctors more than politicians—but I get uneasy with the idea that there shouldn’t be any meaningful boundary at all. Pregnancy isn’t abstract to me; I’ve dealt with enough animal births, and I’ve sat in enough medical conversations with my grandma, to know that biology gets real, fast. dislike_rep_candidate.reason_category: Concerns about democratic norms, elections, corruption, legal issues, or threats to democracy.
dislike_rep_candidate.text:
Yeah—this is where I get off the train with him, and it’s not a small thing. It’s the way he handled the election and everything around it. I don’t care what party you’re in—Republican, Democrat, Martian—there’s a basic rule: you don’t mess with the handoff of power when you lose. That’s load-bearing. You crack that, the whole structure’s suspect.

I watched that period like I’d watch a jobsite where someone says, “Eh, the safety check probably passed.” No—show me the paperwork, show me the chain of custody, show me who signed off. What I saw instead was a lot of noise, a lot of pressure on officials, and a refusal to say, “We’re done here.” That’s not how you run a system that people are supposed to trust. I’ve seen what happens when “probably fine” turns into “too late.”

And I’ll tell you straight: this isn’t me carrying water for the other side. I don’t trust them either. But there’s a difference between arguing about policy and poking at whether the whole vote-counting machine is legitimate after the fact. Once you start treating elections like something you can lean on until they tilt your way, you’re asking for chaos. Next guy does it, then the next, and pretty soon nobody accepts anything unless their team wins. That’s not a country, that’s a bar fight.

So yeah, that’s the line for me. I can argue taxes, spending, all day. But if I think a candidate is loose with the basic rules of how power transfers—especially in a way that pressures the system after the vote—that’s enough for me to say no, I’m not voting for that, I don’t care what jersey he’s wearing.

Yeah, I’ll give you a straight one, no dancing around it. The big thing for me is how the Democrats handle immigration and the border. From where I sit, they talk like it’s mostly a paperwork problem or a compassion test, and I think that’s missing the failure point completely.

Look, I spent a career in environments where if you don’t control access, you don’t have a system—you’ve got chaos. Jobsite, machine room, doesn’t matter. You need to know who’s coming in, under what rules, and what the load limits are. Same idea here. When I hear Democrats downplay border enforcement or treat it like secondary, it sounds like brochure talk to me. You can’t run a country on “we’ll sort it out later.” Later is where things break.

And I’m not anti-immigrant—half the guys I worked with came from somewhere else, good workers, solid people. But there was a process, or at least there was supposed to be. What bugs me is the sense that Democrats are more focused on signaling they’re welcoming than on making sure the system actually holds together. If you don’t enforce the rules, you’re not being kind, you’re just shifting the mess onto the next guy—local services, hospitals, housing. I’ve seen what happens when capacity gets ignored. “Probably fine” turns into “too late” real fast.

So yeah, that’s where I part ways with them. To me it feels like our side is saying, “lock the door, check the list, then let people in properly,” and their side is saying, “leave it open and we’ll figure it out.” I’m not signing my name to that.

like_rep_party.reason_category: Views on immigration levels, border security, undocumented immigrants, or asylum policies.
like_rep_party.text:
Yeah—on immigration, I’ll give them this: they at least talk like the border is something you’re supposed to control, not just manage after the fact. That matters to me. You run any kind of system—jobsite, building, whatever—you need to know who’s coming in, under what rules, and who’s accountable if something goes wrong. Otherwise it’s just… open loop, and open loop is where mistakes pile up.

I’ve seen it in the trades. You get crews coming in where nobody’s really checking credentials, nobody’s clear on who trained who, and suddenly you’ve got guys on a lift who shouldn’t be there. Maybe they’re good, maybe they’re not—that’s the problem, “maybe” is not a safety plan. I’ve seen what happens when “probably fine” turns into “too late.” So when Republicans hammer on border security and say, “No, you don’t just wave people through,” that lines up with how I think about risk.

And look, I’m not saying everyone coming in is a problem. That’s not my point. My point is the system should be tight enough that you don’t have to guess. Who’s here, why they’re here, are they documented, are they following the same rules as everybody else—that should be clear. The Republican side, for all their noise, at least frames it like there are standards and you either meet them or you don’t. The other side, a lot of the time, sounds like they’re more worried about how it feels than how it functions.

Show me the failure point—that’s always my question. With loose borders, the failure point is you don’t know what you’ve got until something breaks. I’m not comfortable running anything that way.

I spent my life in work where if something’s out of tolerance, you don’t argue your feelings about it, you measure it, you sign off, or you don’t. End of story. Elections are supposed to be like that. You’ve got procedures, checks, recounts, courts—fine, use all of it. But once it’s run through that system and it holds, you don’t keep yanking on it because you don’t like the outcome. That’s where I think the Republican leadership, not every voter but the people steering the ship, crossed a line.

And I’ll tell you how it lands for me personally. After my bypass, I had three different specialists telling me slightly different things about meds. I didn’t just “trust the process.” I checked, I compared notes, I asked questions. But once I got a consistent answer and saw the paperwork line up, I followed it. If I kept doctor-shopping until I got the answer I wanted, I’d be in the ground. Same idea here. You can question, you should question—but at some point you accept the result or you’re just undermining the whole system.

What I don’t like is watching that turn into a loyalty test—like if you’re “on their side,” you’re supposed to go along with the doubt no matter what the courts say, no matter what the certifications say. That’s not how you run a jobsite and it’s not how you run a country. I’m not signing my name to fog, and a lot of what they’ve pushed around elections feels like fog on purpose.

So yeah, that’s the piece that sticks in

dislike_dem_party.reason_category: References to healthcare, Medicare/Medicaid, prescription costs, social safety nets, or aid to disadvantaged groups.
dislike_dem_party.text:
Yeah, there is. And I’m not trying to be ugly, I’m just saying what it is.

The way Democrats handle healthcare and all these aid programs, it sounds good when they say it, but living inside it is a different story. I’m in that system every week—diabetes, heart stuff, specialists, prescriptions—and it’s a mess to navigate. Costs don’t go down like they promise, paperwork stacks up, and you spend half your time figuring out who’s paying for what. They talk like expanding programs fixes it, but from where I sit, it just adds more layers between me and actually getting care.

I’ve been on the phone arguing over a prescription that got denied, then approved, then priced different the next week. Same medication. That dog won’t hunt. And when they push more of that system, it feels like they’re building something that looks helpful on paper but leaves regular folks stuck dealing with confusion and delays.

I’m not against helping people—I’m in the middle of needing help myself, whether I like it or not. But the Democratic way of doing it, all wrapped up in big programs and promises, I’ve seen how that movie ends. It doesn’t feel like it’s built for people like me trying to just keep steady and get through the week.

Yeah, I’ll tell you straight. What I like about Republicans is they don’t act embarrassed about America being strong. That matters to me, especially with my boy having served. I don’t want leaders who hesitate or apologize when it comes to defending this country or backing up our people overseas. I’ve seen how that movie ends when folks in charge start second-guessing and trying to please everybody—you get confusion, and the people on the ground are the ones paying for it.

I’m not trying to be ugly, I’m just saying what it is: Republicans, to me, are a lot clearer about standing with our military and not playing games with it. Democrats, half the time, sound like they don’t even like the country they’re supposed to be leading, always talking it down or worrying more about what other countries think than what keeps our folks safe.

So yeah, for me it comes down to that. I want a party that treats strength like it’s a responsibility, not something to apologize for.

like_dem_candidate.reason_category: Support or opposition based on party identity, ideological labels, or general alignment (e.g., liberal/conservative).
like_dem_candidate.text:
Yeah—if I’m being straight, it’s that she’s a Democrat and not coming in with that whole “shrink government, let the market sort it out” mindset. At this point in my life, I don’t have the luxury to pretend that works. I spend my days watching older people try to survive on fixed incomes while bouncing between agencies, and yeah, government is messy, but it’s at least supposed to show up.

When I hear “Democrat,” what I translate it to is: there will be some baseline expectation that public programs should exist and be funded, even if they’re inefficient as hell. The other side tends to talk like those systems are the problem to begin with, and I’m sorry, that sounds nice until you’re the one trying to find a home aide for someone who can’t bathe safely on their own. Then it’s not ideology, it’s Tuesday at 3 p.m., and you need something in place.

So it’s not excitement, it’s alignment. I’m not voting for a personality, I’m voting for the general direction—government should still be in the business of providing something, not just stepping back and hoping families figure it out.

dislike_dem_candidate.reason_category: Support or opposition based on party identity, ideological labels, or general alignment (e.g., liberal/conservative).
dislike_dem_candidate.text:
Yeah—straight up, it’s the politics. She reads as very left-leaning to me, and that’s where I hit the wall.

I’m not a party guy, I don’t wear a jersey, but when I listen to her or see clips, it feels like that wing of the Democratic Party that wants bigger government as the default answer to everything. More programs, more oversight, more “we’ll handle it from the top.” And I’m sitting here dealing with agencies every week that can’t return a phone call, losing paperwork, giving three different answers depending on who picks up. So when I hear that flavor of politics, I’m not inspired—I’m thinking, “You’re going to scale *that*?”

Here’s what sounds good on paper: expand services, protect people, invest more. Here’s what actually happens Tuesday at 3 p.m.: my client’s daughter is on hold for 45 minutes, gets transferred twice, and still doesn’t know if her mom qualifies for home care hours. So when someone comes across as aligned with that whole “the system just needs to be bigger” mindset, I’m already bracing. I’m not being ideological about it—I’m being predictive.

So yeah, that’s the thing that would push me away. It’s not personal, it’s that her political lane feels like more of the same machinery I spend half my life trying to work around.

like_rep_candidate.reason_category: Support or opposition based on party identity, ideological labels, or general alignment (e.g., liberal/conservative).
like_rep_candidate.text:
Yeah. If I strip it down to one thing, it’s the general Republican approach of “less government in your day-to-day.” That’s the piece that still hooks me sometimes.

I spend half my work life dealing with systems that are supposed to help people and instead feel like they’re designed to stall them out. Forms that don’t match each other, offices that don’t talk, rules that change depending on who picks up the phone. Here’s what sounds good on paper, and here’s what actually happens on Tuesday at 3 p.m.—some 82-year-old is waiting on a service that got “approved,” but nobody can tell me when it starts because it’s stuck between agencies. And I’m the one explaining that to the family like I control it.

So when Republicans talk about smaller government, fewer layers, less interference—I get the appeal on a gut level. It sounds like fewer choke points, fewer places where things die in a queue. I’m not saying they actually pull it off cleanly—I’ve been around too long to believe campaign promises—but the ideology itself, that instinct to shrink the machine, that’s the part that makes me at least consider it.

Because from where I sit, more government usually means more paperwork roulette, not more help. And I can do hard, I can’t do endless.

dislike_rep_candidate.reason_category: Comments about temperament, honesty, likability, morality, or personal behavior.
dislike_rep_candidate.text:
Yeah—honestly, it’s his temperament. The way he carries himself, the constant need to swing at people, exaggerate, bend the truth, whatever you want to call it—I don’t trust it. And I’m not talking like some abstract “oh politicians lie,” I mean the day-to-day behavior. It feels like you’re dealing with someone who treats everything like a personal scorecard instead of a job.

In my work, if I talk to a family and they catch me being even a little slippery or dramatic, I lose them. Done. They stop listening, and now I can’t help anybody. So when I see someone in charge who seems comfortable saying whatever fits the moment, doubling down, never just saying “yeah, that was wrong,” it tells me I’d be spending four years wondering what’s real and what’s just… performance.

I can deal with someone I disagree with. I do that all day. I can’t deal with someone who feels unpredictable in a way that’s tied to ego. That’s the kind of personality that turns everything into noise, and I already have enough noise in my life.

like_dem_party.reason_category: Positions on abortion, Roe v. Wade, reproductive freedom, or pro-life/pro-choice issues.
like_dem_party.text:
Yeah. I’ll give you one, and it’s not abstract for me: I like that Democrats treat reproductive decisions as something the person actually living it gets to decide.

I’ve seen what happens when that’s not the case. Not in some debate club way—in real life, with clients’ families and the mess that comes after. A few years back I had an older client whose daughter had a complicated pregnancy, real medical risk, and they were bouncing between doctors who were suddenly cautious about what they could even *say* out loud. The stress in that family was through the roof. Not just “should we do this or that,” but “are we even allowed to consider this option without someone second‑guessing us later?” That’s not healthcare, that’s paperwork roulette with your body.

Here’s what sounds good on paper—“protect life,” “set clear rules.” Here’s what actually happens on Tuesday at 3 p.m.: a scared person, a doctor watching their words, and a family trying to make a time-sensitive decision with legal anxiety sitting in the room. I don’t trust a system that inserts itself there and says it knows better than the patient and the clinician.

So yeah, that’s the thing I like: the stance that this stays between the person, their doctor, and whoever they choose to involve. You can have your beliefs, fine. But turning that into policy that narrows options in the middle of a medical situation—I’ve seen the downstream of that. It’s not cleaner, it’s not safer, it just makes everything harder at the exact moment people can’t afford harder.

like_rep_party.reason_category: Mentions of LGBTQ rights, gender identity, transgender issues, or related cultural debates.
like_rep_party.text:
Yeah—honestly, the one place I find myself nodding along with Republicans sometimes is how they’re pushing back on some of the newer gender identity stuff, especially around kids. I’m not talking about adults living their lives—do what you want, I mean that—but when it gets into schools, medical decisions, all that, I start to feel like things are moving faster than the guardrails.

Here’s what it looks like from my side of the table: I deal with older clients and their families all day, and even getting consensus on basic care—meds, safety, who’s making decisions—is a mess. Now we’re acting like a 13-year-old can navigate identity, social pressure, online influence, and potentially medical steps without that turning into confusion or regret for some of them? That doesn’t pass a common-sense check for me.

And what irritates me is you can’t even ask a cautious question without getting labeled like you’re some kind of monster. That’s a nice brochure answer—“just affirm everything, no questions”—but real life is messier. I’ve seen families blow up over way less complicated issues.

So yeah, when Republicans say “slow this down, put some limits, involve parents more,” I get that. I’m not signing up for the whole party, not even close, but on that specific lane, at least they’re willing to say, “Hold on, maybe let’s not treat this like it’s all settled science and zero-risk.”

dislike_rep_party.reason_category: References to crime, policing, public safety, gun rights or gun control.
dislike_rep_party.text:
Yeah. The way they talk about crime like the answer is always just “more force, more guns, tougher posture,” and then they call it a plan.

I deal with older adults every day. Public safety is not abstract to me—it’s fall risk, confusion, wandering, getting scammed, neighbors calling the police because someone’s yelling at 2 a.m. Half the situations I see are not “criminal mastermind,” they’re dementia, mental health, or just people slipping through cracks. And what I hear from a lot of Republican messaging is basically, “crack down harder, arm up, don’t overthink it.” That sounds good on TV. Tuesday at 3 p.m., it doesn’t solve anything.

I’ve had cases where a client with cognitive decline gets picked up by police because they’re disoriented. You throw law enforcement at that without the right training or follow-up, you just cycle them through—scare them, release them, nothing changes. Or families asking me about guns in the house because Grandpa’s getting paranoid. The “everyone should be armed” mindset in that situation? That’s how somebody gets hurt. I’m not being dramatic, I’ve seen how fast judgment goes when cognition slips.

I’m not anti-police. I work with some good officers. But treating every safety issue like it’s a nail for the hammer—more policing, looser gun rules, harsher tone—that’s a nice slogan, not a system. Safety for the people I deal with is coordination: social services, healthcare, sometimes law enforcement, all actually talking to each other. When one side reduces it to “be tougher,” it tells me they’re not dealing with the reality I’m dealing with.

If she’s serious about keeping coverage steady and making medications affordable, that’s a real reason to vote for her.

I spent 30 years telling kids you don’t get credit just for who you are, you get credit for what you do, and I don’t change that standard for a politician.

Yeah… for me it’s his temperament, straight up. I’m not even talking policy, I mean the way he carries himself.

I’ve seen clips where he’s mocking people or just going off in a way that feels… petty, like he enjoys it. And that sticks with me more than anything. Real talk, if I acted like that with a customer—talking down, getting sarcastic when they’re already stressed—I wouldn’t have a business. People remember how you treat them when things are tense.

I’m not saying a president has to be perfect or polished all the time, but I need to feel like there’s some baseline of respect and self-control there. With him, it just feels unpredictable in a way that doesn’t sit right with me.

like_dem_party.reason_category: Mentions of the economy, jobs, inflation, wages, taxation, government spending, or overall financial management.
like_dem_party.text:
Yeah. For me it’s pretty simple—it’s wages and whether working people can actually stay afloat.

Like, when Democrats talk about raising the minimum wage or pushing for better overtime rules, that lines up with what I see every day. I’ve been the guy getting shorted, and now I’m the guy trying to pay fairly while still keeping the lights on. When labor costs are treated like they matter—like people shouldn’t be grinding in 105° crawlspaces and still barely covering rent—that makes sense to me.

I’m not saying they always get it right in practice, because sometimes the rollout or the rules get messy. But the direction—putting more money in workers’ pockets and not pretending the market just magically fixes that—I like that. That part feels grounded in real life.

dislike_dem_party.reason_category: Mentions of LGBTQ rights, gender identity, transgender issues, or related cultural debates.
dislike_dem_party.text:
Yeah… if I’m being straight, it’s how the party talks about gender stuff sometimes. Not the basic respect part—I’m not against treating people right, that’s not the issue. It’s more the way it gets pushed like there’s only one correct way to talk or think about it, and if you’re not using the exact language or you’re just trying to understand, you get labeled quick.

I’ve had a couple conversations—one at church, one with a customer actually—where I was just asking normal questions, like real talk trying to understand what’s changing and what it means in everyday life, and it turned into this tense thing fast. And I’m like… that’s not how you bring people along. Out here, people are practical. You gotta meet them where they’re at.

So yeah, it’s not the rights part that bothers me, it’s the delivery. It can feel like talking at people instead of with them, and that just shuts folks down.

Like, I’m not trying to spend my nights buried in forms or guessing which rule I accidentally broke just to install a unit and get paid. That general conservative angle of “let people run their business without someone breathing down your neck”—I get the appeal of that.  

I don’t line up with them overall, but that piece of the identity, I understand why people ride with it.

dislike_rep_party.reason_category: Concerns about democratic norms, elections, corruption, legal issues, or threats to democracy.
dislike_rep_party.text:
Yeah. The thing that sticks with me is how some of their leaders handled the election stuff—like pushing the idea that results don’t count unless they win. That doesn’t sit right with me.

Look, I don’t live on the internet, but I remember that stretch after the election, customers arguing about it while I’m just trying to fix their unit, and it felt like reality was optional depending on your team. For me, if we can’t agree that the process is legit—even when your side loses—then everything else gets shaky. Real talk, what’s the end game if nobody trusts the scoreboard anymore? That’s the part I can’t get behind.

What I’ve seen, or at least how it’s come across, feels too loose on enforcement and too willing to let things back up without a clear plan to handle the volume.

If you don’t have firm entry points, enough staffing, and a process that actually keeps up, you get strain on everything downstream—schools, healthcare, local services. That’s where I get uneasy, and that alone would push me away.

I’m not arguing government shouldn’t spend at all—I worked in regulated systems, I get investment—but if you keep pushing large programs without tightening somewhere else, that money shows up as inflation or higher taxes down the road. I felt it pretty directly the past few years just running a household again—groceries, utilities, insurance, all of it creeping up faster than it should. To me it looks like an inputs-and-outputs problem that’s not being managed tightly enough.

I spent a career in a heavily regulated industry for a reason. You don’t keep a power grid stable by saying “let the market sort it out” and hoping for the best. So when I hear blanket pushback against regulation as a matter of ideology, I don’t buy that. Some rules are there because we learned the hard way what happens without them.

like_dem_candidate.text:
Yeah. Abortion.

I don’t need a politician in my doctor’s office or making blanket rules for women they’ll never meet. Life is already complicated enough—health issues, timing, finances—and I’ve watched what happens when systems get rigid and start telling people “too bad, deal with it.” I’m not doing that on something this personal.

So if she’s clearly on the side of keeping that decision with the woman and her doctor, that’s a point in her favor. I’m not arguing with the math: taking options away doesn’t make situations better, it just traps people in them.

dislike_dem_candidate.reason_category: Mentions of the economy, jobs, inflation, wages, taxation, government spending, or overall financial management.
dislike_dem_candidate.text:
Yeah. The way she talks about spending and “investing” like money just appears. That’s a no for me.

I run on commission. I watch cash flow like it’s oxygen. When I hear plans that mean more federal spending without a clear, believable path for how it gets paid for, all I see is inflation and higher costs hitting my household. I already lived through groceries jumping, insurance jumping, interest rates squeezing deals. I’m not signing up for more of that.

I’m not arguing with the math. If your approach keeps pushing prices up and makes money looser without discipline, I’m voting against you.

like_rep_candidate.reason_category: Views on international relations, war, defense, global leadership, or national security.
like_rep_candidate.text: Yeah. He’s willing to act like the U.S. is actually in charge on the world stage instead of asking permission. I’m talking about how he handled adversaries—more pressure, fewer mixed signals, and not looking hesitant about using economic or military leverage if needed. You don’t have to like his personality, but other countries read strength and consistency, not feelings. When leadership looks unsure, things get messy fast. I’d rather have someone other leaders think twice about testing.

dislike_dem_party.reason_category: Views on immigration levels, border security, undocumented immigrants, or asylum policies.
dislike_dem_party.text:
Yeah. Immigration. I don’t like how loose and wishy-washy Democrats get on border enforcement.

I deal in housing, so I’m watching numbers, pressure, supply—real things, not talking points. You can’t keep letting large numbers of people in, whether it’s asylum claims dragging on forever or folks just crossing and staying, and then act surprised when systems get strained. Housing, schools, hospitals—it all feels it. I’m not arguing with the math.

And what bothers me is the messaging. It’s a lot of moral language, not a lot of “here’s the line, here’s how we enforce it, here’s what happens if you don’t follow it.” Every country has a border. Ours acts like it’s optional depending on the news cycle. That doesn’t make sense to me.

like_rep_party.reason_category: References to healthcare, Medicare/Medicaid, prescription costs, social safety nets, or aid to disadvantaged groups.
like_rep_party.text:
Yeah. I like that, at least in the circles I pay attention to, there’s more pressure to not let healthcare just run wild with costs and dependency.

I’ve spent too many hours in waiting rooms and on the phone over my mom’s diabetes care, watching bills show up like they were written by somebody who knows you don’t have time to fight them. What I don’t want is a system where the answer is always “just expand it, just add another layer,” and now you’ve got more people stuck in something slow, expensive, and hard to navigate. I’ve seen what that looks like up close.

What I do like is the push—when it’s real, not just talk—for more price transparency, more control, and not treating people like permanent patients of the system. If I’m dealing with prescriptions or ongoing care, I want to know what I’m paying, why I’m paying it, and have some say in how it’s handled. I’m not interested in being managed by a machine that doesn’t care how long I sit on hold.

dislike_rep_party.reason_category: Positions on abortion, Roe v. Wade, reproductive freedom, or pro-life/pro-choice issues.

I’m not a hawk by temperament, but I also don’t think you get to opt out of the world we’re actually in. What bothers me is this swing between overconfidence—like we can reshape a region if we just say the right things—and then a kind of hesitation where we won’t clearly define what we’re willing to defend or how far we’ll go. From a public health lens, instability overseas doesn’t stay overseas. It shows up as refugee crises, disrupted supply chains for medications, energy shocks that hit working people here. So when I hear Democrats talk about global leadership in broad moral language but get fuzzy on strategy—what’s the mechanism, who’s responsible, what’s the end state—it makes me uneasy.

I’m not trying to win a debate about it. I just want a party that can say, plainly, “Here’s what we will do, here’s what we won’t do, and here’s how we keep it from turning into a forever situation.” Right now it can feel like that middle piece is missing.

When I hear politicians frame wind like it’s unreliable fluff or something we should roll back, it tells me they’re either not looking at the data or they’re choosing not to. And either way, that’s a problem.

I’m not saying wind solves everything. It doesn’t. But I trust the manual more than the mood. The numbers show it’s part of the mix now, and it’s growing. So when a party keeps leaning into messaging that downplays or undermines that, it feels disconnected from what I see on the ground every day.

나는 은퇴했어도 매달 고정비가 딱 정해져 있어. 재산세 올라, 관리비 올라, 약값 올라—이게 다 연결돼 있는 거거든. 그런데 정부가 계속 지출을 늘리면 결국 세금이든 인플레이션이든 형태만 바꿔서 우리한테 다시 온다니까. 이건 현장에서 많이 봤어. 예산 통제 안 되는 프로젝트는 나중에 두세 배로 튀어. 그때 가서 “필요했다” 이런 말 해봐야 소용없어, 돈은 이미 나갔거든.  

카말라 해리스 쪽은 그런 면에서 브레이크를 거는 스타일이 아니라, 더 쓰는 쪽으로 기운다고 보여. 그게 나 같은 사람 입장에서는 바로 생활 압박으로 연결돼. 나는 거창한 구호보다 “이거 얼마 들고, 누가 책임지고, 언제까지 끝나냐” 이걸 보거든. 그게 명확하지 않으면, 나는 반대 쪽으로 기운다.

like_rep_candidate.reason_category: Evaluations of a candidate’s qualifications, preparedness, track record, or ability to govern.

있지. 내가 나쁜 소리 하는 게 아니고, 그 사람은 “집행하는 힘”은 확실히 봤어. 말이 아니라 실제로 밀어붙여서 결과를 내는 스타일.  

내가 토목 쪽 일을 오래 했잖아. 공사는 계획서 잘 쓰는 사람보다, 이해관계 엉켜 있을 때도 일정 맞추고 계약 끌고 가는 사람이 필요해. 트럼프는 첫 임기 때 보면, 방식이 거칠어도 자기가 정한 방향은 끝까지 밀었어. 예를 들어 규제 풀고 에너지 쪽이나 기업 활동 쪽에서 속도를 낸 건, “이건 하겠다” 하면 실제로 행정이 움직이게 만든 거거든. 그게 말처럼 쉬운 게 아니야. 워싱턴은 말만 많고 아무것도 안 되는 경우가 많아.  

나는 후보 볼 때 인기나 말투 안 봐. 준비돼 있냐, 실제로 조직을 굴려봤냐, 결과를 만들어봤냐 그거 본다. 그 기준으로 보면 트럼프는 이미 한 번 해봤고, 어디서 막히는지 알고 다시 들어오는 거라 실행력은 더 세질 가능성이 있어. 현장은 다르게 돌아가. 경험 있는 사람이 두 번째 들어오면, 시행착오 줄이고 바로 핵심부터 건드려. 그건 인정해야지.

dislike_rep_candidate.reason_category: References to race, gender, religion, or symbolic representation of social groups.

나는 이민자로 여기서 40년 넘게 살았고, 처음 왔을 때 영어도 서툴고, 현장에서 사람들 시선이 어떤지 다 겪었어. 그때 제일 힘든 게 뭐냐면, 나 개인을 보는 게 아니라 “아시아에서 온 사람” 이렇게 하나로 묶어서 판단하는 거였거든. 근데 대통령 하겠다는 사람이 비슷한 식으로 말해버리면, 그건 그냥 말버릇 문제가 아니야. 사회 전체 분위기를 그렇게 끌고 가는 거지.

교회에서도 그래. 흑인, 히스패닉, 한국 사람 섞여 있는데, 서로를 개인으로 보지 않고 라벨로 보면 공동체가 유지가 안 돼. 구조가 무너진다고. 나는 그걸 너무 많이 봐서 알아.

그래서 결론은 간단해. 정책이 뭐고 이전에, 사람을 집단으로 뭉뚱그려서 말하는 태도, 그거 하나만으로도 나는 반대 쪽으로 간다. 그런 방식은 결국 현장에서 문제만 키워.

Yeah, for me it comes down to how he handles the money side of things. I’m not looking for speeches, I’m looking at whether the numbers feel like they’re moving in the right direction.

The one thing I’d point to is how he pushed for lower taxes on businesses. In my world, that’s not abstract. When I was running my HVAC supply company, margins were tight and cash flow mattered every single month. When taxes ease up even a little, that money doesn’t just sit there—it goes into inventory, hiring, keeping things stable when a contractor pays late. I’ve seen this movie. When the pressure from taxes and regulation gets too high, small and mid-size operations start cutting corners or pulling back.

I’m not saying everything he does is perfect. But if I had to give you one reason I’d lean his way, it’s that he tends to treat the economy like something that needs room to operate, not something you squeeze and hope it still runs.

Yo tengo hija, tengo nieta creciendo, y no me cuadra que alguien en el gobierno venga a decirles qué hacer con su propio cuerpo. Eso se me hace una falta de respeto, así de simple.

Y no, no es que yo ande promoviendo nada ni haciendo show. Es sentido común. Hay situaciones que nadie más que la persona y su familia entienden. Así que sí, si me preguntas por qué votaría por ella, es por eso. Porque en ese tema están de este lado —dejar decidir— y los otros están del lado de imponer. Y ahí yo no camino con ellos, punto.

Trump, con todos sus modos, al menos pone una línea clara: aquí hay reglas y se respetan. Y eso para mí pesa, porque si no hay orden, no hay nada que sostenga el sistema, y luego vienen los problemas que nadie quiere arreglar.

dislike_rep_candidate.reason_category: References to healthcare, Medicare/Medicaid, prescription costs, social safety nets, or aid to disadvantaged groups.
dislike_rep_candidate.text:
Mira, te lo digo sin rodeo: a mí lo que me brinca es todo lo que huela a recortar o jugar con Medicare, Medicaid, o encarecer medicinas. Ahí sí no. Yo ya estoy en edad donde eso no es teoría, es mi vida diaria y la de mi mamá. Las citas, las pastillas, el control de la presión… eso no es lujo, es mantenimiento básico, como cambiar una válvula antes de que reviente todo.

Y del lado de los republicanos —y Trump viene de ahí— siempre traen esa idea de “recortar”, “privatizar”, “dejar al mercado”. Eso en papel se oye precioso, pero en la práctica ¿quién acaba pagando? Nosotros. El que tiene seguro limitado, el que está contando pastillas, el que no puede andar probando a ver si “el mercado” le resuelve. Yo he visto gente en la clínica brincándose dosis porque no les alcanza, así de claro.

Yo no necesito discursos, necesito saber que no me van a mover el piso en algo tan básico como mi atención médica. Porque cuando te falla eso, ya perdiste todo lo demás: trabajo, tranquilidad, hasta la cabeza. Entonces sí, por ahí me voy en contra. No me metas mano en salud, no me hagas más caro lo que ya es pesado. Ahí es donde yo trazo la línea, y ese lado político no me da confianza, punto.

dislike_dem_party.reason_category: References to healthcare, Medicare/Medicaid, prescription costs, social safety nets, or aid to disadvantaged groups.
dislike_dem_party.text:
Mira, te lo digo directo: los demócratas hablan mucho de ayudar con salud, Medicare, medicinas… pero en la práctica lo enredan todo y al final el que anda batallando es uno. Yo con mi presión alta no puedo estar jugando, necesito algo claro: cita, medicina, seguimiento. Y ellos se la pasan metiendo programas, cambios, papeleo… “eso en papel se oye precioso”, pero luego vas y te traen de un lado a otro o te cambian reglas y ya no sabes ni qué cubre qué.

Según ellos están del lado de la gente, pero a veces parece que les importa más el sistema que si tú consigues tu medicina sin bronca ese mismo día. Yo he visto gente mayor, como mi mamá, que debería tener todo más fácil… y no, puro trámite y espera. Entonces sí, dicen que ayudan, pero yo digo: ayuden de verdad, sin tanto rollo. Porque la salud no es para andar probando ideas, es para resolver.

Es como ese compañero de trabajo que siempre llega con actitud de “yo tengo la razón” aunque el edificio se esté cayendo—no ayuda, nomás estorba el ambiente. A mí eso me dice mucho del carácter: si no puedes reconocer un error o tratar a la gente con tantito respeto, ¿qué vas a liderar?

Y te lo digo directo: yo no confío en gente así. En mantenimiento, si alguien se pone necio y no escucha, pasa un accidente, se inunda un piso, alguien sale lastimado. Esa misma vibra la veo ahí—mucho orgullo, poca autocrítica. Eso es lo que no me gusta, punto.

When my sister had her hip surgery in 2022, we ran into delays getting follow-up therapy approved—paperwork, referrals, waiting on callbacks. Not the doctors—the doctors were solid. It’s the layers around it. For a party that says healthcare is a priority, I expect them to make that part simpler and faster, especially for seniors. Sitting there waiting while someone’s mobility is on the line… that shouldn’t be normal.

I’m still a Democrat, but on that, I’m not impressed. If it’s important, fix the path, not just the speech.

like_dem_candidate.reason_category: Concerns about democratic norms, elections, corruption, legal issues, or threats to democracy.
like_dem_candidate.text:
I’ll be straight with you, because I don’t bounce around on this stuff. The one thing that could make me look twice at Kamala Harris is if I felt confident she would keep elections and the basic rules of the system steady and not play games with them when it’s inconvenient.

I’m not talking in big slogans—I mean the practical side. In my world, if people think the system isn’t fair or the rules change depending on who’s in charge, everything breaks down fast. Employees stop trusting payroll, clients start double-checking every invoice, and you spend all your time putting out fires instead of doing the work. Same idea with the country. I need to believe that whoever’s in charge is going to respect the outcome of elections, keep the process clean, and not bend things behind the scenes just to hold onto power.

So if I saw her acting in a way that was consistent—win or lose, no drama, no trying to stretch rules, just “this is the result and we’re moving forward”—that would matter to me. Not because I suddenly agree with everything else, but because if that foundation is shaky, nothing else you promise really holds up when it’s time to act on it.

In my world, if you don’t control who’s coming in and out of a building, you don’t have safety—you have confusion. It’s the same principle, just bigger. I’m not against people coming here, not at all. We’ve got folks on our crews from all kinds of backgrounds, and some of my best workers are immigrants. But there has to be a system you can point to and say, “This is the process, this is the standard, and it’s actually being followed.”

What I hear from her—and I’ve tried to listen, not just go off headlines—sounds more like broad compassion without enough attention to enforcement and limits. And I think that’s where things start to break down. Because once it feels like the rules are flexible or not really enforced, you put pressure on everything else—jobs, services, even just basic trust in the system.

Clean is a system, not a mood—and I look at immigration the same way. If I don’t hear a clear, practical plan for how you’re controlling it, tracking it, and keeping it consistent, I’m not comfortable putting my vote there.

I’ve got two teenagers, so this isn’t abstract in my house. We’ve had school forms change, language shift, policies come down where it feels like things are moving fast and parents are expected to just nod along. And I’m sitting there like, “Say it plain so I can fix it—what exactly are you asking my child to be part of, and where do I get a say?” I’m not against people being treated with respect, not at all. I run crews with all kinds of folks, and my rule is simple: you treat people right, period. But when it comes to minors, medical decisions, or what’s being taught as settled truth, I want a slower, more careful approach.

What I hear from Trump—whether people like how he says it or not—is a willingness to draw a line there and not just go along with whatever the newest standard is. And for me, that connects to responsibility as a parent. I’m the one accountable for my kids, not a school board or a trend that showed up last year.

So it’s not about being harsh or excluding anybody. It’s about wanting some guardrails and feeling like someone’s actually willing to say, “Hold on, we’re not going to rush this when it involves children.” That’s the part that would push me in his direction.

like_dem_candidate.reason_category: Concerns about democratic norms, elections, corruption, legal issues, or threats to democracy.
like_dem_candidate.text:
Mira, te lo digo en corto pero claro: lo único que a mí me podría jalar un poco hacia ella es si de verdad se ve que respeta las reglas del juego y no anda torciendo elecciones o usando el poder para callar al otro lado. Eso para mí pesa más que cualquier promesa bonita.

Porque yo ya he visto en chiquito cómo se pone la cosa cuando no hay reglas claras. En la obra pasa: si el contratista grande mueve números, cambia acuerdos o se inventa condiciones a última hora, el que se friega es el que está abajo trabajando. Y nadie responde. Entonces cuando oigo de política y todo mundo se acusa de fraude, de manipular votos, de usar el sistema a su favor… eso sí me prende foco rojo.

No te voy a mentir, no sigo cada detalle como analista, pero sí me fijo en la actitud. Si alguien se planta y dice “aquí hay reglas, se respetan aunque no me convenga”, eso habla. Si ella demostrara eso con hechos, no con discursos, que no le tiembla la mano para que el proceso sea limpio y parejo, ahí sí digo: ok, mínimo no me van a cambiar las reglas a media jugada.

Porque al final, mira, yo no necesito que el gobierno me salve, yo trabajo. Pero sí necesito saber que el terreno es parejo y que no hay truco escondido. Si ella representa eso de mantener orden sin mañas, eso sería lo único que me haría considerarla en serio. Todo lo demás… se puede discutir. Pero si eso falla, ya ni para qué.

dislike_dem_candidate.reason_category: Views on international relations, war, defense, global leadership, or national security.
dislike_dem_candidate.text:
Mira, te lo digo como lo veo yo: lo que me hace ruido es cómo se ve en temas de afuera, de guerra y seguridad. No es que yo ande metido en política internacional todos los días, pero sí sigo lo suficiente para saber cuándo alguien se ve firme y cuándo no. Y con ella… no me transmite esa firmeza.

A ver, en corto, ¿qué es lo que me preocupa de verdad? Que en un broncón serio —llámale conflicto grande, llámale presión de otros países— necesitas a alguien que no dude, que no mande señales mixtas. Porque allá afuera no perdonan eso. Si te ven flojo, se te montan. Así funciona, igual que en la obra: si el cliente o el proveedor ve que no traes control, te cambian las reglas.

He visto entrevistas y momentos donde siento que se enreda o no aterriza claro qué haría. Y eso, para mí, es peligroso. No es un tema de caer bien o mal, es de “¿quién responde cuando se pone feo?”. Porque al final, esas decisiones no son de papelito, son de vidas, de soldados, de estabilidad acá también. Si sube el desorden afuera, acá nos pega en gasolina, en trabajo, en todo.

Yo no digo que no tenga equipo o asesores, seguro los tiene. Pero para ese tipo de puesto, la cabeza tiene que verse sólida por sí sola. Y ahí es donde yo digo: no me da confianza suficiente. Y con algo así, yo prefiero no arriesgar.

like_dem_party.reason_category: Mentions of the economy, jobs, inflation, wages, taxation, government spending, or overall financial management.
like_dem_party.text:
Mira, te soy directo: lo único que sí les reconozco a los demócratas es cuando empujan lana para mover la economía en la calle, no en el papel. O sea, cuando sueltan proyectos, reconstrucción, cosas que generan chamba real. Después de Ike y en otros años que han metido dinero a infraestructura o ayudas para reparar casas, ahí se siente. No es teoría, es que de repente hay más llamadas, más roofs, más trabajo para la cuadrilla.

A mí no me importa el discurso, me importa si hay jale. Y cuando hay ese tipo de gasto —aunque luego digan que es “government spending”— pues sí cae trabajo para los que estamos en construcción. Yo lo viví: semanas flojas se vuelven meses llenos porque la gente tiene apoyo o porque hay proyectos andando. Eso sí lo respeto.

Ahora, tampoco me vendo la idea completa, porque luego ese mismo dinero se pierde en burocracia o tarda un chorro en bajar. Pero en corto, cuando le meten a la economía de forma que se ve en la calle, no en gráficas… ahí digo, bueno, eso sí sirve.

dislike_dem_party.reason_category: Views on immigration levels, border security, undocumented immigrants, or asylum policies.
dislike_dem_party.text:
Sí, te lo digo directo: lo que no me cuadra es cómo manejan el tema de la frontera y la entrada de gente. No es que yo esté en contra de los inmigrantes —yo soy uno, mi familia es de eso— pero una cosa es venir a trabajar y otra es que parezca que no hay orden.

Mira, en mi mundo eso sí pega. He visto trabajos donde llegan cuadrillas que nadie sabe de dónde salieron, sin papeles, sin seguro, y luego pasa un accidente o no pagan, y ¿quién responde? Nadie. El contratista grande se lava las manos y el problema le cae al que está abajo o al cliente. Eso no es justo para nadie, ni para el que viene a trabajar bien ni para el que ya está aquí haciendo las cosas derechas.

A veces escucho a políticos demócratas y suena como que todo es “déjenlos entrar y luego vemos”, y ahí es donde yo digo: a ver, ¿y el control dónde queda? Porque cuando no hay reglas claras, el que cumple es el que termina pagando multas, retrasos, o perdiendo chamba frente a gente que trabaja más barato porque no tiene las mismas responsabilidades.

Yo no digo que no se ayude, claro que sí, pero con orden. Registro, proceso claro, quién entra, quién responde, cómo trabajan. Si no, se vuelve un desmadre y al final ni los inmigrantes ni los negocios salen ganando. Esa es la parte que no me gusta, se oye bonito en discurso, pero en la vida real… otra cosa manda.

That’s not politics, that’s governance risk.

I’m not looking for a perfect person, but I need someone who will stay inside the guardrails when the outcome doesn’t go their way. If that piece isn’t solid, everything else becomes negotiable, and that’s how systems break. So no, there isn’t anything there that would move me in his direction.

dislike_rep_candidate.reason_category: Concerns about democratic norms, elections, corruption, legal issues, or threats to democracy.
dislike_rep_candidate.text:
Yes. For me it comes down to how he handled the election he lost and what he did afterward.

I’m a compliance guy — process matters, documentation matters, and when the result comes in, you follow the process to challenge it or you accept it. What I saw was someone pushing to overturn an outcome without producing evidence that could hold up under scrutiny, including in courts. That’s a problem for me. If the standard becomes “I don’t like the result, so I pressure the system until it changes,” then the rules don’t mean much anymore.

I’m not saying the system is perfect — it’s not — but you don’t protect it by ignoring its own procedures. That kind of behavior makes institutions weaker, not stronger, and that’s where I draw the line.

dislike_dem_party.reason_category: References to crime, policing, public safety, gun rights or gun control.
dislike_dem_party.text:
Yeah. My issue is how parts of the party talk about public safety like it’s optional or something you can experiment with.

I’m in Los Angeles, I’m driving myself everywhere, and I pay attention. When policies come out that reduce enforcement or treat repeat offenses like they’re just misunderstandings, I don’t see compassion—I see risk getting pushed onto regular people who didn’t sign up for it. I’ve watched neighborhoods change where businesses start locking everything up, people are on edge, and then officials act surprised.

I’m not asking for heavy-handed anything. I’m saying: rules have to mean something, and consequences have to be predictable. If you tell me a standard exists but you don’t enforce it, that’s not a system—that’s a suggestion. And in my experience, suggestions don’t protect anyone.

Yeah—there is one thing, and I’ll say it plainly. I respect that the Republican Party is clear and unapologetic about being socially conservative. They don’t dance around it, they don’t try to repackage it every six months. You know where they stand on family structure, on personal responsibility, on moral limits—that whole framework.

Now, I don’t vote with them, and I’ve got real disagreements, but that consistency matters to me. I get tired of people acting like those values are something to be embarrassed about or hidden behind softer language. At least with Republicans, on that front, you’re not guessing what they believe.
